title: Toumajian farm picnic
description:
Worcester, Massachusetts area, Toumajian Farm, 1918. Photo by Nishan Ohanian.
Armenian young men demonstrate their happiness for the newly formed Republic of Armenia by having a picnic with friends. Along with the American Flag, they hold the tricolor flag of Armenia (used from 1918 to 1920).
The photo donor, who is in front with hat on his knee wrote to Project SAVE:
"Some of the names in the picture are out of my memory. The great part was, I remember, the FESTIVAL of the day, all around the place. The ATMOSPHERE of Real Armenian Country [picnic]."

title: Armenian Vanetzis at Van Hotel, Asbury Park NJ
description:
Armenian Vanetzis (people born in Van or related to those who are), Van Hotel, Asbury Park, New Jersey, late 1940s or early 1950s; photographer unknown.
Near the Asbury Park boardwalk, Van Hotel was a summer gathering place especially for those from the Tri-State Area of NY, NJ and CT. They came together as a community and enjoyed the beach during the day, and music and dancing at night.
Courtesy of Vanetzi Vart Shirvanian Hachigian; and her cousin Seda Aykanian Mataevosian

title: Pharmacy of the National Hospital of Sepastia
place:
Sepastia
Sivas
Sepastia vilayet
Sivas vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Pharmacy of the National Hospital of Sepastia, Ottoman Empire, 1903.
Photographer is unknown, but most likely the Encababian Freres
The National Hospital, built in 1889 as stated in the Armenian words written across the medicine shelves, provided free treatment for the poor.
As stated in Armenian across the medicine shelves, the National Hospital was established in 1889. It provided free treatment for the poor. For reasons of lighting, the unidentified photographer posed nine hospital staff members and three patients outside, carefully camouflaging both the ground and building exterior with artfully draped oriental rugs and fabrics, and even two potted plants. The photographer also arranged  hospital props: medicine bottles and jars lined up perfectly on the shleves, along with surgical instruments, and tools of the hospital's pharmacy, like mortar and pestle, amd weights and mesasures on the worktable. With no confirming documentation, it is thought that the three men (one seated left taking a pulse, and two standing in back) wearing dark European style three-piece tailored suits, are probably doctors, and the men in white are possibly pharmacists. The function of the two men with pens in hand seated at small writing tables is not known. Wearing the fez (brimless felt hat) indicates professional status.
Stamp of the National Hospital of Sepastia is on the back.

title: Chamaian family picnic
description:
Members of the Chamian family and other Sepastatsis (people from Sepastia), many of them rug dealers, picnic on a fine carpet with a whole roast lamb and birthday cake, in honor of two-year old Mildred Chamian, right front.  Boston area park, c. 1935. Photographer unknown.
Courtesy of Midlred Chamian Nahabedian
Photo donor Mildred "Bunny" Chamian (Nahabedian) is the 2-year-old seated front right saying, "oh," in front of her sister Alice.  Their mother Nartoohi née Dushugian is the woman 2 rows behind them, and their father, Garabed Chamian, owner of City Oriental Rug Company, Boston, sits at Nartoohi's right. Mildred and her other sister Hazel, second row left (Dutch boy haircut) continued their father's business after he died.

Easter dinner, 1925, at Khachadoorian home, 48 Bellevue Street, Worcester, Massachusetts. Photographer Ben Khachadoorian, far right, holds cable release in his hand. He studied photography with K.S. Melikian, and also painted picture shown above mantel. The Khachadoorians in this photo were all born in Malatia, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire.
Left to right: Zachios Khachadoorian, Gregory Boyajian, Sooren D. Khachadoorian, Mary Simonian Boyajian, Elizabeth Boyajian Khachadoorian, Queenie Boyajian, Benjamin Khachadoorian

title: H. Hovhanisian, Stakhanovian worker
description:
Written in Armenian on back of photograph:
"Comrade H. Hovhanisian, worker at tufa factory, Stakhanovian"
Soviet Armenia, exact location unknown. Undated, c. 1935-early 1940s.
"Stakhanovian" refers to Stakhanovite movement – a campaign intended to increase worker productivity and to demonstrate the superiority of the socialist economic system. Named after Alexey Grigoryevich Stakhanov a Soviet and Russian miner, who set a productivity record in 1935 during his mining shift. Stakhanov's records set an example throughout the country and gave birth to the Stakhanovite movement, where workers who exceeded production targets could become "Stakhanovites".

title: 220th anniversary of the Academy of Sciences of the USSR
description:
220th anniversary of the Academy of Sciences of the USSR in Leningrad, Russia, photographed by Trakhman for Sovfoto (15th W. 44th St. NY, NY)
"Academician Leon Orbeli autographing his pamphlet for Professor E.D. Adrian (England) and Professor D.W. Bronk (USA) from left to right."
Leon Abgarovich Orbeli (1882-1958) was an Armenian physiologist active in the Russian SFSR. He was a member of the Academies of Science of USSR and Armenian SSR. Leon Orbeli became director of the Institute of Physiology in 1950.

title: Bedros Murachanian, Jerusalem
place:
Jerusalem
Palestine
Ottoman Empire
British Mandate
Israel and Jordan
Israel and Palestinian territor
description:
Bedros Murachanian, Jerusalem, c. 1880
The family story states that Bedros had a premonition from God in 1898 that he and his family should leave Diyarbakir. His wife Lucia Simsarian Murachanian, had just given to birth to a son, Levon Murachanian, the photo donor's father. They fled Diyarbakir on donkey, The next morning most of the family was killed. He and his family settled in New York City, where he established a jewlery business with his son Levon. They later moved to New Jersey. As he made money he sponsored many Armenians to move to the US.

"Stakhanovian" refers to Stakhanovite movement – a campaign intended to increase worker productivity and to demonstrate the superiority of the socialist economic system. Named after Alexey Grigoryevich Stakhanov a Soviet and Russian miner, who set a productivity record in 1935 during his mining shift. Stakhanov's records set an example throughout the country and gave birth to the Stakhanovite movement, where workers who exceeded production targets could become "Stakhanovites".

Hayastan
description:
Carolyn Mugar plants Armenia Tree Project’s 1,500,000th tree.
During the winter of 1992 while visiting Armenia, Carolyn saw that families desperate to heat their homes were burning their own furniture, and cutting thousands of trees for fuel. She decided to commit towards preventing further deforestation and founded the Armenia Tree Project. Since its inception, more than 5.5 million trees have been planted, and hundreds of jobs have been created for Armenians in seasonal tree-related programs.
Armenia, 2006, photographer unknown
Courtesy of Armenia Tree Project

title: Women's Reproductive Health Center
place:
Yerevan
Erivan
Russian Empire
USSR
Republics of Armenia
description:
Nora Nercessian, PhD, holding flowers and pictured with staff,was instrumental in establishing the Women's Reproductive Health Center in Yerevan, Armenia in 1991. It was the first walk-in outpatient facility of its kind throughout the former Soviet states.
With the commitment of the Mayor of Yerevan, she assembled physicians and advisors from Harvard Medical School, Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital and other Harvard affiliated hospitals. Together, they created a board to oversee the funding, training of staff and implementation of the Center.
Physicians and nurses from Massachusetts, Maine, Georgia and Alabama traveled to Armenia to train the medical and nursing staff of the Center. Dr. Nercessian contributed to crafting the bilingual medical education program for physicians, nurses, administrators and patients, focused on prevention and early treatment of a wide range of gynecological and obstetrical conditions.
The Center was the first in Armenia where the PAP was used in cytology. It was also the first to use the LEEP procedure for the treatment of some cervical conditions.
Between July 1992 and December 1997, over 80,000 patient visits were registered. The patients receiving care included the residents of Armenia, as well as Armenians and non-Armenians from Moscow, St. Petersburg, Kiev, the Crimea, Tbilisi, Kazakhstan, Syria and Iran.
The groundwork laid by Dr. Nercessian's activism over the past 26 years has yielded the now self-sufficient Women's Reproductive Health Center which continues to follow protocols and methods established in the early years. We are inspired by the use of Dr. Nercessian's expertise and the harnessing of her extensive network in service to the Center, staff, patients and their families.

title: Reverend Albert Wells Hubbard
description:
In 1997, Araxi Hubbard Dutton Palmer (Araxi Palmer) published the book Triumph from Tragedy, which describes and documents the lives of her adoptive family, the Hubbards from Corning and White Plains, New York, their missionary work in Turkey,and later work with Near East Relief,  spanning the years 1872-1920, including details about the plight of refugees and orphans, the rescue of baby Arpenia Karagosian (later named Araxi Hubbard) in Sivas by Mary Hubbard in 1920, and their subsequent life in America after 1920.
Reverend Albert Wells Hubbard, Corning, New York, 1872.

title: A. A. Bedikian album of Photographs of the Ottoman Empire
description: Original vintage albumen prints documenting the 19th-century Ottoman Empire, mostly Constantinople, c. 1870s to 1900s. The photographs depict typical Orientalist subject matter such as architectural ruins like the Aqueduct of Valens, Walls of Constantinople, and ethnographic types. Photographs also include views of the Bosporus, Topkapı Palace, and views of Brusa, among others. Many of the photographs are from the studio of Armenian photographers Pascal Sebah (and the later studio iteration Sebah and Joaillier), the Abdullah Frères and Gülmez Frères. Other photographs include Basile Kargopoulo (Greek), Guillaume Berggren (Swedish), and Félix Bonfils (French).
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Marie Bashian Bedikian

title: Trinity Cotillion Dinner Dance
description:
Trinity Cotillion Dinner Dance. Statler Hilton Hotel Ballroom. Boston, MA. Nov. 28, 1964.
Twelve women representing the Armenian Community, 500 guests. Sponsored by the Young Women's Auxiliary, Armenian Holy Trinity Church of Boston.
Left to right:
Louise Janigian, Judith Pilibosian, Carolyn Simonian, Beverly Yacobian, Vivian Samuelian (the photo donor), Deanne Gazarian, Donna Kaprielian, Gail Talanian, Judith Garabedian, Janet Garabedian, Janice Yagjian, Judy Davajlian
Behind Vivian Samuellian, ushers: Mid Arsenault, top Robert Krikorian
The photo donor, Vivian Samuelian Portner described the event at the time she gifted this photograph to Project SAVE: "I was an eager part of this group because it was the first time I got to meet other young women and men of Armenian descent from the Boston, Worcester and Rhode Island cities - in addition to my first cousins Janet and Judy Garabedian."
Photographer: Boris and Milton

title: Trinity Cotillion Dinner Dance
description:
"Me. Ever feel like a fairy princess? I have." Vivian Samuelian Portner, the photo donor.
Trinity Cotillion Dinner Dance. Statler Hilton Hotel Ballroom. Boston, MA. Nov. 23, 1963.
Sponsored by the Young Women's Auxiliary, Armenian Holy Trinity Church of Boston.
Photograph from the photo donor's cotillion scrapbook.
The photo donor, Vivian Samuelian Portner described the event at the time she gifted this photograph to Project SAVE: "I was an eager part of this group because it was the first time I got to meet other young women and men of Armenian descent from the Boston, Worcester and Rhode Island cities - in addition to my first cousins Janet and Judy Garabedian."
Also:
"What is really interesting is that John F. Kennedy was shot on November 22 and the funeral in Washington, DC was November 25 and yet they still had the Cotillion on November 30.
On the day that Kennedy was shot, I was on the Tufts campus which immediately shut down. I brought my roommate- who was from out of state - home with me to Milton. There was tremendous fear and confusion everywhere. And yet, I did not mention this national event when I created my cotillion scrapbook or in my diary!"

title: Arapkir Union of PA performance
description:
These amateur actors, members of the Arabkir (town in Historic Armenia) Union of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, pose in their costumes for a situation comedy—title is not remembered. The matchmaking parents are seated in front. Their lovely aghcheeg (daughter) played by Mary Jehanian (Kazanjian), our photo donor, stands at left, with her perspective pessa (groom) Zaven Baronian next to her.  The actors are also members of St. Gregory the Illuminator Armenian Apostolic Church, Philadelphia, where the play was performed in 1942.  The stage props tell their own stories, starting with the oriental rug. Hyrig (father), played by Armenag Tourigian, is pouring sourge (coffee) from a jezveh. Myrig (mother), who is the hars’ real mother Ovsanna Akedaian Jehanian, holds her sourgee kavat (coffee cup) ready to drink. Between myrig and hyrig sit two props: a glglag (water pipe, also know as nargeleh in Turkish and hooka in Arabic) which the unidentified character in the center holds to his lips; and a mangal (coal heater for the glglag). The pessa holds an unidentified prop and hanging from his belt wears a bag of perhaps coins. The prompter, Albert Sarkisian, standing at right, holds his script.
Photo is by Vartan Khazigian.

title: Mary Hubbard, Near East Relief
description:
In 1997, Araxi Hubbard Dutton Palmer (Araxi Palmer) published the book Triumph from Tragedy, which describes and documents the lives of her adoptive family, the Hubbards from Corning and White Plains, New York, their missionary work in Turkey,and later work with Near East Relief,  spanning the years 1872-1920, including details about the plight of refugees and orphans, the rescue of baby Arpenia Karagosian (later named Araxi Hubbard) in Sivas by Mary Hubbard in 1920, and their subsequent life in America after 1920.
Mary was born in Sivas where her American parents served there as missionaries from 1872-1899. As a young woman, she returned to Sivas in 1919 to work for the NER and was director of the girls' and boys' orphanages. She adopted an orphan baby whose dying mother entrusted her to Mary's care. When Mary's tour of duty was completed in 1921, she returned to the United States with baby Araxi.
Mary Hubbard in her Near East Relief uniform, 1919
Photo page 16 (follows page 67 of book)
p16_1 Mary Hubbard, in Near East Relief uniform, 1919
p16_2 U.S.S. Leviathan, trans-Atlantic passenger liner

title: Unidentified Armenian family before 1893
description:
An unidentified family from the Ottoman Empire. Though the photographer is identified as Walsh from Trenton, New Jersey, they must only have printed the photo--the style of clothing is clearly Ottoman. The inscription on the back  in Armenian reads "A gift to Baron (Mr.) Krikor [Sisagian?], Philadelphia. Nov. 19, 1893. Mardiros Kafeian, Newburyport, Massachusetts. "
Courtesy of Greg French who purchased photo in New Bedford, MA.

title: Man with a curl
description: Armenian Man, name unknown, c. 1905. Photo by The Dewey Studio, Lynn, MA. In the early 1900s many Armenian immigrants who lived in Lynn worked at local shoe and leather factories. By the 1920s some had moved west, especially to Fresno, CA. This man does not look like a factory worker, but people often dressed up to have their photograph taken. The photo was discovered in the home of Nevart Mathewson (originally Mateossian from Worcester, MA) Lexington, MA after her death.

Turkey
description:
Serpouhi Mendilian Encababian sitting with niece, Melanoush (named Noyemzar at birth). Sepastia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1920s.
Melanoush was the daughter of Serpouhi's sister, Nunufar Mendilian Tchitbachian. Nunufar perished in the forced marches of the Genocide, in the desert of Syria, near Hama. Serpouhi Mendilian Encababian was the photo donor's mother, and the wife of the photographer Karekin Encababian.
Photograph by Karekin Encababian of Encababian Frères.

title: Serpouhi Mendilian Encababian
place:
Sepastia
Sivas
Sepastia vilayet
Sivas vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Serpouhi Mendilian Encababian, Sepastia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1910s.
Serpouhi Mendilian Encababian was the photo donor's mother, and the wife of the photographer Karekin Encababian. She was born to Manoog and Anna Semerjian Mendilian on Aug. 17, 1893 in Gurun. Her father died during the massacre of 1895. She was then placed in an orphanage in Sivas where she received her formal education and became a teacher to the other children. At age 16 years she married Karekin Encababian (1909). Her mother, Anna Mendilian, later died in the forced desert march to Syria, along with Serpouhi's sister, two nieces and other family members.
Photograph by Karekin Encababian, of Encababian Frères.
Audio segment from 1988 of Serpouhi's children remembering her:

description: Armenian children, names unknown, Sebastia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1912. Photo by Encababian Freres. These are the children of the photo donor's husband's sister, but she did not remember their names. From her experience as a Genocide survivor, she said "If you lived with your family (while you were growing up,) you remember things. If your parents did not live, and you grew up in an orphanage, you are less likely to remember things, even later on."

title: Bath day at Sivas orphanage
place:
Sepastia
Sivas
Sepastia vilayet
Sivas vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Bath day at Sepastia Orphanage
Mayrigs (here, surrogate mothers) caring for Armenian orphans, Sivas (Turkish) aka Sepastia (Armenian), c. 1919; photo by Near East Relief (NER) worker, Mary S. Hubbard. Mary was born in Sivas when her American parents served there as missionaries from 1872-1899. As a young woman, she returned to Sivas in 1919 to work for the NER and was director of the girls' and boys' orphanages. She adopted an orphan baby whose dying mother entrusted her to Mary's care. When Mary's tour of duty was completed in 1921, she returned to the United States with baby Araxi.
In 1997, Araxi Hubbard Dutton Palmer (Araxi Palmer) published the book Triumph from Tragedy, which describes and documents the lives of her adoptive family, the Hubbards from Corning and White Plains, New York, their missionary work in Turkey,and later work with Near East Relief,  spanning the years 1872-1920, including details about the plight of refugees and orphans, the rescue of baby Arpenia Karagosian (later named Araxi Hubbard) in Sivas by Mary Hubbard in 1920, and their subsequent life in America after 1920.

title: Buchenwald Special Camp gate building
description:
Buchenwald Special Camp gate building. Nazi concentration camp liberated by US forces at the end of WWII, c. April 1945.
The cast-iron camp gate with the SS inscription “Jedem das Seine” (“To Each His Own”).
While sharing his photographs with Project SAVE Archives, George told of the shock that he and his buddies experienced when they happened upon this camp of horrors at the end of World War II. He began clicking his camera immediately.

title: Ancherte Village, Arabgir
place:
Ancherti village
Arabkir

Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Ancherti village/Arabkir/Kharpert Vilayet/Mamuretulaziz Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Ancherte village of Arabkir, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1910; photo by Derounian Freres. In 1922 the Vartanian Association of Aleppo reproduced this view as a postcard and added the Armenian initials for the traditional New Year and Christmas greeting in a fundraising appeal for orphans.
The Armenian initials SH N D and S DZ stand for Happy New Year and Blessed Birth, in that order since in the Armenian Apostolic Church, Christmas is celebrated after New Year's Day on January 6.
Ancherte, was the birthplace of both parents of the photo donor and home of their families (Tovmassian and Tanoian) for centuries.
Ancherte, which literally means "without water" was a village of 300 two-story homes, about equally divided among Armenian and Turkish extended families. There was a village square with a few shops. The village had plenty of water in spite of its descriptive name. Many of the Armenian men were merchants who went to Constantinople, Cairo and Aleppo, some for as long as two or three years at a time. When they returned home, they brought honor to their families by presenting gifts to the church. They were able to live on their earnings for a year or so before traveling again.
Armenians were massacred or deported from Ancherte in 1915.
The translation of the messafe on the back side of the postcard reads:
"What news did you bring to orphans like us? Do you know that we have neither parents nor home? We await help- with our gazes fixed."
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Vahan Martin Tomassian

title: Farewell to Mihran Agha Tabibian
description:
Armenouhi Buchakjian Tekeyan (right) with her mother and relatives say goodbye to Mihran Agha Tabibian (center, with scarf around his neck) as he starts back home to America; Hamadan, Iran, c.1928-1930; photo by Kaloust Jivan Tekeyan. Mihran, the family's benefactor and thus his title, Agha, had spent four years in Iran as a rug buyer for A&M karagheusian, Inc. of NYC. He arranged for many Hamadan Armenians to enter the United States. His driver (wearing lambswool cap) was allowed to take him in the 1920 Ford only as far as the border of Iran, where Mihran was taken to the port of Beirut by another vehicle more suitable for desert driving.
People numbered in the photograph:
1. Vahe Simidian
3. Nvart Tekeyan
5. Mannig Buchakjian Simidian
6. Mihran Agha Tabibian
7. Louise Paklavadjian
8. Melek Sarkisian Buchakjian
9. Armenouhi Buchakjian Tekeyan
10. Mardiros Simidian

title: Satenig and Ardashes Megerdichian, Japan
description:
Satenig and Ardashes Megerdichian. Most likely Yokohama, Japan, 1917.
Sister and brother escaped the Genocide in Van and made their way to the United States going east through Russia, Siberia, Manchuria, Japan, Seattle and finally, Boston. Before leaving for America, Satenig and Arshag posed for the camera in traditional Japanese kimono as a souvenir of their time in the country.

title: Marchand de mouhalebi. Salut de Constantinople
description:
Merchant of Mouhalebi. Greetings from Constantinople. c. 1900-1910.
Postcard addressed to A. A. Bedikian, the photo donor's father, in Chicago, IL. 1915
Armenian handwriting on back of postcard in English:
"My honorable friend.
On the occasion of the New Year
Please accept my mother Arusiag's,
my and my children's
heartfelt and sincere congratulations
and good wishes.
Since Nov. 12 I work for
the well-known America
oil company, My apologies
but my love is [remains]
the same.
Always yours.
? S. Kaprielian"
B. Village
1915

title: Water sellers, Constantinople
description:
Water sellers, Constantinople. c. 1900-1910.
Postcard addressed to A. A. Bedikian, the photo donor's father, in Chicago, IL. 1913
Translation of Armenian message on back:
Union Ha[ll?], Galata, c/o Standard Oil Co. of NY
June 25, 1913
Dear Antranig,
I am in receipt of your nice letter dated June 9. I gave the [?] to Prof. Panaretof [?].
I am terribly busy. At work during the day, and in the college early in the morning and
in the evening. Today your school is closing down. I have been tired of three weeks of
[?] by working day and night. I will write a long letter before the two weeks are past.
Forgive me for this short and hastily written lines. Think about the fact that I am doing
three jobs at once; this is not to be, I mean the College, my work, and my fiancé.
Heartfelt greetings,
Vahram [?].
[on the side]:
My warmest congratulations for your successes.
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Marie Bedikian

title: Unidentified family, possibly Movsesian
description:
Unidentified family, possibly Movsesian, Ottoman Empire. Undated
Armenian handwriting on the back of the photograph translated:
Dear ones,
….. Movsesian
Boston, Mass.
This remnant piece is the visualization of the past.
A more beautiful, precious and memorable family than this ….
The Turkish government massacred and made to disappear nearly
…. people, without sparing the women and even the unborn children.
… many thousands of families …. If you ask
me … do we know … with such people. Our fortune
… … [between] only our ba…
It seems … …..
to give as a wife [?] …. …
of/to the worlds, so that we too …. could
gather there [as] one family … …
… with love
Union City 20 2 1939[?]				.. ….

title: The Sagharian children
description:
The Sagharian children. From left to right: Hovaness (named after his older brother on the far right who died in the massacre), Yeghsaper, Hovhaness (first son who died). The completed composite photo was made by K.S. Melikian.
The older photo of Hovhaness was taken in Kharpert, 1912. The more recent photo of the surviving children was taken in 1924, Worcester. Photographed by Sagharian possibly.

title: Wedding of Agavni Boyajian and Nighohos Kalashian
description:
Agavni "Agnes" Boyajian and Nighohos Kalashian, Chelsea, Massachusetts, February 22, 1910. Agavni came to the United States with her widowed mother around 1906, having, as a small child, survived being hacked with a sabre sword during the 1895 Turkish massacre of Armenians in Heusenig, Kharpert. Nighohos, a factory worker, was a boy when he came to the United States around 1900. As a young man, he heard about a young lady who had a crooked mouth and a closed eye. Nighohos met her and liked her very much, and they were married. Their wedding picture could easily be mistaken for an old-country photograph had it not been carefully documented by their daughter.
Courtesy of Margaret Kalashian Cummings, their daugher.

Adana/Ataniya/Adana Vilayet/Cilicia/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description: Photo taken by American missionary Stephen Trowbridge at spot where his colleague and fellow missionaries Reverend Dr. Miner Rogers and Reverend Henry Mauer were killed by Turks in Adana on April 24, 1909. Fighting had broken out on April 14, and Rev. Rogers and Rev. Mauer had come out to help put out the fire endangering the girls' school when they were shot. Reverend Trowbridge was an eyewitness.

title: Mountaineers of Zeitoon
description:
Portrait of three Armenian men from Zeitoun, two appear to be holding long rifles, c. 1860s.
Photograph labeled on front and back "Mountaineers of Zeitoon" and "Mountaineers of Zeitoon, near Marash" on album page.
The man sitting at left is possibly Ghazaros Chorvoian, and the man sitting at right is possibly Asvadour Yéni-Dunia. They were from two of the four families that ruled Zeytoun, and led the First Zeytun Resistance in 1862. Photograph was probably taken in Constantinople during mediation to conclude the agreements after the resistance.
Photographer: Abdullah Frères
From an album of Carte-de-Visite photographs, "Peoples of the Ottoman Empire," owned by Kate P. Williams, Mardin, Turkey, 1871.

title: B-26 Marauder "Winnie"
description:
Bomber squadrons flying over unknown structures. Western Europe, exact location unknown. 1942-1945.
The plane is identified as the B-26 “Winnie” of the 386th Bomb Group. Major Chuch Lockhart was in command. "The 386th Bombardment Group played a significant role in the success of D-Day by clearing the coastal defenses that enabled the seaborne invasion. The bombing by the 386th Bomb Group allowed soldiers and sailors to come ashore that day." Robert Spiers, 386th Air Expeditionary Wing historian.

title: A B-26 Marauder "Yankee Guerilla" in flight
description:
A B-26 Marauder "Yankee Guerilla" in flight over a body of water. The plane shows Allied aircraft invasion stripes.
Western Europe, exact location unknown. 1942-1944. The plane has nose art with a nude woman wearing an Uncle Sam hat and holding bombs in both hands.
According to the American Air Museum on Britain: "AF Lost control of aircraft while returning from October 5,1944 mission in the area of Duren Barracks in Southwestern Germany. The plane crashed into a vacant house 5 miles Northwest of Compeigne, France."

title: Bomber over smoking farmland
description:
A B-26 bomber flying over farmland, a smoke plume reaching well into the sky pictured in the background.
Western Europe, exact location unknown. 1942-1945.
The plane appears to have D-Day invasion stripes and it's possible this photograph was taken over Normandy, France on D-Day, June 6, 1944. "The 386th Bombardment Group played a significant role in the success of D-Day by clearing the coastal defenses that enabled the seaborne invasion. The bombing by the 386th Bomb Group allowed soldiers and sailors to come ashore that day." Robert Spiers, 386th Air Expeditionary Wing historian.

title: B-26 Marauders on D-Day
description:
B-26 Marauder fighter bombers providing cover for D-Day landing in Normandy. France, June 6, 1944.
Plane in front has call sign of "Mr. Five-by-Five" from the 555th BS, 386th BG, 9th AF. All the planes have D-Day invasion stripes.
"The 386th Bombardment Group played a significant role in the success of D-Day by clearing the coastal defenses that enabled the seaborne invasion. The bombing by the 386th Bomb Group allowed soldiers and sailors to come ashore that day." Robert Spiers, 386th Air Expeditionary Wing historian.

title: B-26 Marauder "Yankee Guerilla"
description:
A B-26 Marauder "Yankee Guerilla" in flight over a body of water. The plane shows Allied aircraft invasion stripes.
Western Europe, exact location unknown. 1942-1944. The plane has nose art with a nude woman wearing an Uncle Sam hat and holding bombs in both hands. The plane appears to have D-Day invasion stripes and it's possible this photograph was taken over Normandy, France on D-Day, June 6, 1944. "The 386th Bombardment Group played a significant role in the success of D-Day by clearing the coastal defenses that enabled the seaborne invasion. The bombing by the 386th Bomb Group allowed soldiers and sailors to come ashore that day." Robert Spiers, 386th Air Expeditionary Wing historian.
According to the American Air Museum on Britain: "AF Lost control of aircraft while returning from October 5,1944 mission in the area of Duren Barracks in Southwestern Germany. The plane crashed into a vacant house 5 miles Northwest of Compeigne, France."

title: French Armenian Legion
place:
Adana
Ataniya
Adana Vilayet
Cilicia
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Adana, Turkey, 1919; photo by H. Berberian.
The title in Armenian reads, " The Army High Command and Military Police who conquered and took back Cilicia"
"X" marks Manoug "Khan" Bagdasarian, a volunteer in the military police of the French Armenian Legion. Beginning in 1914 with training in the United States, he served two years with General Antranig in Turkey, Russia, and Persia, and two years with the French in Syria, Egypt, Arabia, and Palestine. In 1920 he married and came to the United States.

title: Apcar family of Yokohama
description:
The Apcar family of Yokohama, Japan, April 1928.
children, front, left to right: Lucille, Katherine and Dorothy Apcar
back, left to right: Michael Apcar, Araxe Saunders Apcar, Diana Agabeg Apcar, Zumruth "Ruth" Apcar. Photographer unknown.
Diana, born into a Persian-Armenian merchant family, was a business woman in Yokohama, Japan, where she assisted many Genocide survivors who escaped east through Russia and China, and arrived destitute in Japan on their way to America. In 1920 the Republic of Armenia named her Honorary Consul to Japan.

title: Karabagh rally in Los Angeles
description: Rally in Los Angeles, City Hall, October 1988, in support of the Armenian people of Nagorno Karabagh, who, under the rule of Soviet Azerbaijan since Stalin separated it from Armenia in 1921, suffered human and civil-rights violations as well as pogroms.  Karabagh Armenians were demanding reunification with Soviet Armenia; photo by Jacob Demirdjian.  Present at this rally were representatives of the Armenian churches, political parties, and service organizations.  At podium is Archbishop Vatché Hovsepian, Primate of the Western Diocese of the Armenian Apostolic Church; 3rd from right of podium is Osheen Keshishian, Armenian Observer editor and publisher, one of many speakers.

title: Nurses graduating in Constantinople, 1922
place:
Constantinople
Istanbul
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Constantinople/Istanbul/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Graduating class of an Armenian Red Cross-sponsored nursing school, Constantinople, c. 1922; photographer unknown. Zarouhi Sahagian Terfanian (Andonian) seated right side. The photo donor’s mother, Zarouhie, from Yerzinga, lost her husband, youngest child, and brother in the Genocide, and was separated from her mother, sister, and other two children. She eventually found her way to Constantinople, and in 1923 came to the United States as the mail-order bride of Avedis Andonian and was widowed yet again two years later. She worked as a nurse to support her family and brought her mother and two older children to the United States.
Courtesy of Harry Andonian, son of Zarouhie

place:
Beirut
Beyrouth
Beirut Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Lebanon
description:
Beirut, 1923, photographer unknown; graduates of the St. James Girls' Industrial School.
All were previously together at the German Orphanage in Kharpert. They continued their lives in the Diaspora:  Fresno, California; Brighton, Massachusetts; New York City; Providence, Rhode Island; Pawtucket, Rhode Island and Silver Springs, Maryland; and Baghdad, Iraq. Shooshanig Inglizian (Avedisian), our photo donor, standing center, for years kept the classmates connected.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Shooshanig Avedisian

description: The Modern School for Girls, Smyrna, Ottoman Empire, c. 1913; photographer unknown.  The school’s founders, Kevork Partogh, seated center, and his wife (name unknown) seated at his right, are flanked at left by the English teacher (name unknown) and at right by another teacher, Levon Yeramian. Student Marguerite Abroyan (Hazarian) kneels in the second row next to Kevork Partogh. Later, in 1921, at age sixteen, she left Smyrna for the United States, one year before the city was burned.  Courtesy of Haroutune P. Hazarian (husband of Marguerite), New York, New York; additional documentation from Mer Izmiruh (Our Smyrna), published by the Smyrna Armenians Association, New York City, 1961.

title: Armenian Protestant Elementary School teachers and students
place:
Ordu
Kotyora
Trebizond Vilayet
Trabzon vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Armenian Protestant Elementary School teachers and students, three of whom hold diplomas, Ordu, Ottoman Empire, 1909; photographer unknown.  The varjouhie (teacher) Victoria Khatcherian, seated right, attended Marsovan College c. 1901-1904. Weeks after this photo was taken, she traveled to America and married Safar Santurian (a shoemaker from Malatia) in Lowell, Massachusetts. Later, Victoria and Safar moved to Fresno, California where they raised their son on the farm that is still in their family today.
Courtesy of Jane Knar Lanas, Victoria and Safar’s granddaughter, Fresno, California

place:
Adapazar
Adapazari
Kastamonu Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Adapazar/Adapazari/Kastamonu Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Armenian Girls’ High School, Adapazar, Ottoman Empire, c. 1905; photographer unknown. Students and staff can be seen in the yard around the building, which may be a classroom or dormitory, on each balcony, and in several windows.
"Dormitory or classroom building with students in front - probably morning before classes began, or after 4 p.m. Students came, two teachers came to the dining room window and gave the students a slice of bread and a piece of cheese."
This American Protestant missionary boarding school was located about 80 miles east of Constantinople. The students were Armenian, and teachers were both American and Armenian.

title: The Gabriel and Annah Vartabedian family
place:
Kharpert
Harput
Elazig
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Gabriel and Annah Vartabedian family of Heusenig, Kharpert, 1907 or earlier. Photo by Soursourian.
Left to right: Reuben Gabriel Vartabedian, Annah Bozoian Vartabedian, Sarah Anna Vartabedian, Maritza Vartabedian, Gabriel Vartabedian, and Anjel Vartabedian.
Sarah Anna, who was born in 1892, was married in 1907 to Hampartsoom Bogosian, a marriage arranged by her maternal uncle, Hatchadoor Bozoian. Hampartsoom had been sent to live in the U.S. at age 16 to avoid conscription into the Turkish army. Sarah was sent at 14 years old to Marseille, France to marry him, then the couple moved to Woonsocket, Rhode Island, sponsored by the uncle. Helena was born to the couple in 1908 and a boy, Paul, a year later. Hampartsoom became ill with lung problems and wanted to return to the homeland to die, so the four of them made the difficult journey back to Kharpert, where he died peacefully, The baby Paul died soon after. Sarah, with her young daughter, decided to stay with her parents. But in 1914 as the situation for Armenians in the Ottoman Empire worsened, Sarah sought help from the American Consul in Kharpert for safe passage to the U.S., possible because she and her daughter were American citizens. She had to say goodbye to the rest of her family. She returned to Woonsocket to live with uncle Hatchadoor Bozoian.
Only Sarah, Maritza, and Rouben survived the Genocide. Their sister Anjel wrote them, shortly before she was killed, that her baby boy had been kidnapped to be raised as a Turk.

description:
Bithynia High School, Bardizag, Ismid Turkey, pre-1915.
Bithynia High School, also called Bardizag High School Favre Boys' Home (orphanage).
The buildings are named after Americans who put up the money for their construction. The buildings no longer stand; the wooden structures were abandoned as a result of the massacres.

title: The Kazanjian family of Kharpert
place:
Kharpert
Harput
Elazig
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The extended Kazanjian family of Kharpert, Ottoman Empire, circa 1902-1907. Photographer unknown.
The donor's paternal grandmother, Altoon  "Florence" Kazanjian (later Emirzian)  is seated left. Seated next to her are her sisters Margaret and Almas, and behind each of them are their husbands (unidentified.) The others in the photo are cousins.
On July 8, 1907, Altoon left Kharpert with her mother-in-law to be, Anna Yapchian Emerzian, and her future brother-in-law Khachadom, to join her fiancee in Markar Sarkis Emerzian, who had emigrated in 1902. Traveling via Liverpool, they arrived in Philadelphia on August 27, 1907, and were married on September 15.
Courtesy of Jane A. Emerzian Scory

description:
Altoon Kazanjian's sisters, Margaret and Almas, with their mother, children, and husbands in Kharpert, names and date unknown, but it appears to be several years after #1-14 was taken (from this collection) and must be after 1907, when Altoon emigrated. This photo arrived with the last letter Altoon received from her family, before they were all killed by the Turks. The mother holds a photo of her son Manoog, who died in France while returning from Rhode Island.
Courtesy of Jane A. Emerzian Scory

title: Nishan Kalajian and unidentified man
description:
Sitting: Nishan Kalajian, photo donor's father's brother. Aleppo. Undated. c. late 1910s.
Postcard addressed to Sahag Kalajian in Watertown, MA.
Translation of Armenian writing on back of photograph:
"Mr. S. Kalajian,
Mr. Sahag Kalajian. My respectable brother.
The resent ---?
It's worse than it was before for us Armenians, no clothing, hunger, make a lot of devastation here in Aleppo and on the roads.
Help, help, and make your help as soon as possible.
Nishan Kalajian"

description:
Elbis and Ohan Chitjian and their children; Ordu, Ottoman Empire, c. 1905. Photographer unknown.  Both parents were killed in the Genocide.  The children heard their father being shot, saw their mother die on the march, were separated from each other, and given as servants to Turkish families. The children were later reunited.

title: The Hadji Bedrosian Family, Nirzeh
place:
Nirze
Nirzeh
Ankara Vilayet
Kayseri Sanjak
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description: The Hadji Bedrosian Family, Nirzeh, village of Caesarea, Ottoman Empire, 1913; photo by Stepanian Frères.  Fifteen-year old Nevart Hadji Bedrosian, back right, stands with her mother, Mariana Kaderian Hadji Bedrosian at left, and little sisters, Lousig and Arousiag, as well as her future mother-in-law, Maritza Zakarian, seated. Nevart wears her dowry necklace of gold coins and other jewelry for a portrait to send to Garabed, her intended, who was working at Hood Rubber Company in Watertown, Massachusetts. Of those pictured, only Nevart survived the Genocide. In 1922 she arrived in Watertown where Garabed was waiting for her.  She was the last living survivor from Nirzeh when she died in 1998 at age 100.

title: Cesar Algen at a desk - K2023.009.014
General Harbord with orphans at Etchmiadzin
place:
Etchmiadzin
Ejmiatsin
Vagharshapat
Russian Empire
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description:
General Harbord, left among men in uniform, entering courtyard at Etchmiadzin, Armenia, and greeting orphans, September 29, 1919. Photographer unknown.
American Military Mission to Armenia, also known as American Mission to the Middle East, headed by U. S. Army Major General James Gutherie Harbord, left, Etchmiadzin, Republic of Armenia, September 29, 1919; photo by United States Army Signal Corps (note their insignia, the crossed-flags with ID number, lower left corner).  General Harbord, with three of his officers, attracts the curiosity of orphan boys.  Harbord was sent by President Woodrow Wilson on this fact-finding mission to gather information in support of the president's League of Nations proposal to establish an Armenian nation in the Middle East.
Courtesy of Judge Quentin Kopp, whose father, Shepherd Kopp, was an officer with this mission.

description:
Shabin-Karahisar, Ottoman Empire; before 1913
The photo donor’s father, Krikor Bozaian, arrived in the United States with this photograph of his friends and native city. It was his only possession. The slopes of the rock hill in the background were home for about 6,000 Armenians. In other parts of the city lived 12,000 Turks and 300 Greeks. Photographer unknown

title: The Kaprielian Family, Agn
place:
Agn
Egin
Kemaliye
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Kaprielian Family, Agn, Historic Armenia, 1893;
The photo donor's aunt and uncle: Krikor Kaprielian and his wife Perlan Haskalian, with their first born Arshalyse, with another sibling on the way.
A message written on the reverse side of photo in Armenian (possibly by photo donor's mother more than 30 years after the photo was taken) reads:
"This photograph was taken in 1893- my father, mother, and sister. Happy were those days when we had just started life. We never thought we would lose each other eternally, that merciless death would separate us leaving the living in pain in this wide world. 2.9.24."
The family escaped Agn during the 1895 massacres and joined other family members in Hackensack, New Jersey. They changed the surname to Gabriel upon arrival in US 1897 to join Mgurdich Sumpad Kaprielian who went by the name Dr. M. Simbad Gabriel.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives, Courtesy of Syranoush Baltaian Movsesian, and Courtesy Marie Hekimian Alberian who treasured Syranoosh's photos and after her death presented them to Project SAVE.

title: The Gabrielian family of Constantinople
description:
The Kaprielian/Gabriel family, Constantinople, Ottoman Empire, c. late 1895-7. Photo by G. Amirayan.
Front, left to right: Arshalooys Kaprielian, Ervant Kaprielian
back, left to right: Perlan Haskalian Kaprielian, Krikor Kaprielian
Another daughter, Zephyr, was born June 1897, not long after the arrival of the family in the United States.
The family fled Agn after the September 1896 massacre with their extended family and joined other family members in Hackensack, New Jersey. They changed the surname to Gabriel upon arrival in US 1897 after joining brother Sumpad, who went by the name Dr. M. Simbad Gabriel.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Syranoush Movsesian

title: Serma Kuyoomjian
description:
Serma Kuyoomjian (nee Kanarian), the photo donor's great grandmother. Agn, Ottoman Empire, c. 1885.
Serma became the family matriarch after the passing of her husband Kapriel Kuyoomjian, a jeweler in Agn. Her 8 adult children would all start new lives in the US.
Based on the date, the photo shows Serma at about 45 years old.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Syranoosh Baltaian Movesian

Mamuretulaziz Vilayet

description:
Kaprielian family shortly before leaving Agn. Makrouhy and Nicolas Gabrielian pose with their children, c. 1896 (based on the birthdate 1896 of Aro, the youngest). Photo by A. E. Gabrielian, aka Armen Gabriel, who emigrated to the US earlier in November 1896. The family left Agn late 1896, arriving in the US in April of 1897. The girl in the middle is Lousig, who later became Lucy Gabriel Baltaian (K2005.053.068). The two oldest boys are Frank and George seen in (L1990.007.016). The family settled in New Jersey.
Courtesy of Syranoosh Baltaian Movsesian, daughter of oldest child, center back, Loosig Gabrielian Baltaian

title: Rev. Garabed Keropian
description:
Possibly a portrait of Rev. Garabed Keropian (b. 1854), as he appears to be wearing a clerical collar.
Rev. Keopian was a Protestant minister who worked for the American Board Commissioners of Foreign Missionaries at the Constantinople Bible House. He was arrested by the Ottomans in 1915 but released with help from US Ambassador Morgenthau He emigrated to New Jersey in 1920 but returned to Turkey in 1930.
His daughter married Dr. M. Sinbad Gabriel's only son, Osshin, in 1909.

title: Reunion in the Catskills
description:
Family reunion in Catskills of members of the Gabriel, Diradoorian, and Kuyumjian relatives, Catskills, New York, 1910.  Photo by R.S. McConnell.
Front row, left to right: Edna Diradoorian, "Harsa" Elmas Gabriel, Zvart Diradoorian.
Back row, left to right: Mardiros Diradoorian, Haiganoosh Diradoorian, Khoren Kuyumjian, Haiganoush Kuyumjian, Lucy Gabriel Baltaian, Nicholas Gabriel.
The family spent summers at Fleischmans, NY.
Haiganoush Kuyimjian, with her son Khoren left Agn to reach US in Dec. 1908, declaring to join her sister (at Makor's address) in Union, NJ." Thus her maiden name would also be DerGhazarian.
Haiganoosh Diradootian is Nicholas' sister who married Mardiros Diradoorian in Agn 1892, with her daughters Zwart and Edna. They were in the group that arrived in 1897. Marie Alberian is Zwart's daughter.
In the center is clearly the matriarch Serma (silver) but called "Harsa" Elmas (diamond) for reasons unknown.

title: Yeprad (Euphrates) College,
place:
Kharpert
Harput
Elazig
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Yeprad (Euphrates) College, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1900.
Founded by American Protestant missionaries in 1878, Euphrates College included elementary through high school, a hospital, windmill and water pump, dormitories and a Protestant church.
Esteemed Euphrates College professors (left to right): Nigoghos Tenekedjian, a Eurphrates graduate fluent in five languages, taught history and Turkish; Hovhannes Harutunian, known both as Tlgadintsi (native of Tlgadin) and Hovhannes Varjabed (Teacher), was a
writer who taught in local rmenian schools before his tenure at Euphrates; Donabed Luledjian, a graduate of Euphrates and Yale, returned home to teach biology at his alma mater; Garabed M. Soghigian, also a Euphrates graduate, taught Armenian. These professors all in their prime, were among the educated elite massacred during the Genocide.
Courtesy of Jim Melcon (son of Professor Luledjian’s brother), and Carnig and Lucy Astorian Alexanderian.

title: Women graduates from Euphrates College, class of 1912
description:
Women graduates of the teacher education department of Euphrates College (Yeprad College). Class of 1912. Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire. June 1912
L-R:
1. Vosketel Hampartumian
2. Aghavni Boghosian
3. Satenig Aghajanian
4. Dikranuhi Ayrasian
5. Elmas Kurkjian Deranian, photo donor's mother.
6. Esther Boghosian
7. Aghavni Vartabedian
8. Mariam Tervizian
9. Takuhi Tervizian
10. Anna Bonapartian
Euphrates College was established in 1878 by American missionaries. The girls section of Yeprad COllge existed until 1917. It presented diplomas to its last 2 students then closed its doors. Elmas was the last surviving graduate of Euphrates College and passed away at age 101 in May, 1993.

title: Cesar and Zevart Algen with friends
place:

New York
USA
description:
Berjouhie Dingilian Topalian, Zevart Algen, Cesar Algen, with an unidentified couple at left. New York, NY. c. 1922-1935
Woman sitting second from left may be "Big Armine" and man in glasses possibly her husband.
Family lore says that the Garabedian family helped her to get out of Turkey, and her son or grandson became a dentist in Maryland.
She was probably called "Big Armine" to contrast with Armine (Singilian) Aroian.
She is also in photo ROTH_Clint 321-22

title: Gathering in honor of Generals Nejdeh and Sebouh
description:
Gathering in honor of Generals Nejdeh and Sebouh.
Armenian Youth Federation from Watertown, Chelsea, New Britain, and Worcester gathered in Providence, Rhode Island, 1933-1934. Photo by Perham Studios.
2nd from left is Sparapet (colonel) Garegin Nzhdeh (Ter-Harutiunian) - Commander-in-Chief of Armed Forces of the Republic of Armenia in 1921, 7th is General Arshak Nersisian (Sepuh), MP of Armenia and 8th is Armenia's Prosecutor Vardges Aharonian, son of President of Parliament of Armenia, famous writer Avetis Aharonian.
Vahram Der Parseghian (the photo donor) is standing behind priest.

title: Door to the tomb of Saint Garabed - K2017.002.019
Vasken Catholicos and Sosi Kavafyan
place:
Ankara
Angora
Ankara Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
His Holiness Vasken I, Supreme Patriarch and Catholicos of All Armenians (identified by the cross on his hood), shares a light moment with Sosi Kavafyan, daughter of the photo donors. Ankara, Turkey, 1962. Photograph by Turkish photographer for an Istanbul newspaper.  P
ictured here at a luncheon where he greeted Ankara’s 200 Armenians, Vasken Catholicos gave Sosi a cross and a photograph of himself. Years later, Sosi traveled to Echmiadzin and gave him a copy of this photograph.  Looking on, left to right, are Harry Burt Movsessian of Florida, godfather to Vasken I, Aram Kahana (married priest) Basmajian, Deputy to the Patriarch of Istanbul (in Turkey, priests, excluding the patriarch, who nevertheless needs special permission, are not allowed to wear clerical garb outside the church); and Hayr Sourp (Holy Father) Komitas Ter-Stepanyan, who accompanied Vasken Catholicos from Echmiadzin, Soviet Armenia, to Istanbul for the funeral of Patriarch Karekin Archbishop Khatchadoorian. As part of his official visit to Turkey, Vasken Catholicos went to Ankara to meet with Turkish President General Gemal Gursel and to pay his respects at the tomb of Ataturk.
Featured in Project SAVE's 2001 Calendar "Armenian Christianity: Celebrating 1700 Years"

title: St. John the Baptist Monastery in Moush
place:
Moush
Mus
Mush
Bitlis Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
View of St. Charles Monastery in Moush, Ottoman Empire, 1923. Photo by Vartan A. Hampikian.
Sourp Garabedi Vank (Saint John the Baptist the Forerunner Monastery, not "Charles" as indicated ont he photogrpah,) Moush, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire before 1915; photo by Vartan A. Hampikian, copyright 1923. During the Genocide the Turks desecrated this sanctuary. After brutally killing Armenians here, the Turks searched for treasure, finidng none, they destroyed the monastery, leaving only the foundation stones. Later, Armenian survivors retrieved their well0hidden treasures - manuscripts, crosses and religious vessels and took them to Yerevan where they are part of the collections of the National Historical Museum of Armenia.

description:
Barsamian Groceries and Delicatessen, 521 East 87th St, Yorkville area, New York City, c. 1940; photographer unknown.  Bedros Barsamian is the proud grocer in his apron with his neighbor and close friend George Swing, who drove the cross-town city bus on 86th Street.  After operating stores at several locations in Manhattan and The Bronx, Bedros moved his family to this location in 1930 where they lived 3 flights up.  Bedros continued the Deli until he retired in 1960.  Photographer unknown.

description:
Graduation class of the historic Hripsimian Sarassarian Girls' School in Erzeroum, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, probably in 1913 or 1914. Photographer unknown.
In the center is Rostom Stepan Zorian, one of the founders of the Armenian Revolutionary Federation. Behind him is Ruben Kajberuni, Armenia's MP, Deputy Minister of Public Welfare and Reconstruction. Back row, far right, is Arevaluys, also known as Arevik, who survived the Genocide. She eventually found her way to Iran, where she married Kevork Aivazian and had five children, three of whom later moved to the  United States.

title: Soghomon Tehlirian and his financee
description:
Soghomon Tehlirian and his fiancee, Anahid Tatikian, circa 1923. They were godparents of the photo donor, Anahid "Annette" Eminian.
The following explanation is offered by Annette:
This photo is an unforgettable part of my life.
From the time I was a child, I remember my parents, Alexander and Elizabeth Eminian, telling people I was Soghomon Tehlirian's godchild. The people would hug me, pinch my cheeks, and kiss me.
I never realized, though, until I was old enough to understand, how honored they were, and how honored I should be. I was born on October 14, 1923 in Clevland, Ohio. My parents had a large home, and it became a "hotel" for transient and visiting Armenians. My parents were preparing for my baptism when Soghomon Tehlirian came to Cleveland.
When he learned that I was to be baptised, he said "I would be honored to be her godfather, and I would be doubly honored if you named your child Anahid after my fiancee.
Needless to say, my parents and relatives were thrilled.
Soghomon's fiancee Anahid lived in France and was called "Annette" there. That is how I got my name.
I never had the honor of seeing my godfather again, but this photo of Soghomon and Anahid is a treasure.
credit_line: Courtesy of Anahid Annette L. Eminian Tashjian

description: The family of Hagop and Anna Chooljian Tutunjian (thought to be the woman and man standing), Dikranagert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1899. Baby Mardiros was born in 1898 and in 1912 came to the United States where he found work as a weaver in a factory in Chelsea, Massachusetts. He lost all of his family in the 1915 Genocide. Later he settled with his wife in Fresno, California.

place:
Aleppo
Halab
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Syria
Aleppo/Halab/Aleppo Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/French Mandate/Syria
description:
Catholicos Sahag Khabayian of Cilicia (seated) assisted by 5 Vartabeds (celebate priest) and 12 Kahanas (married priests) at the first Blessing of the Holy Muron since the end of World War I; Aleppo Syria May 27, 1923.
The holy muron is blessed on Pentecost, as it represents the anointment of the Holy Spirit which decended on the Apostles on the Day of Pentecost.
Courtesy of Rose, Elizabeth and Anita Davidian, with assistance from Archbishop Tiran Nersoyan and Rev. Fr. Papken Maksoudian

description:
Photo of a city skyline (presumably New York City, prior to departure?)
Handwritten note: Looking toward the "Near East" July 1, 1919.
One photograph in an album of 120 photographs from Lucina Thompson Beeching, who worked for the Near East Relief, starting in July 1919. She was assigned to the Sivas unit where she took charge of the orphanage. The album is a collection of photographs from her journey to Sivas, as well as her experiences there.

Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Kharpert Vilayet/Harput Vilayet/Mamuretulaziz Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Passport picture for the Aharonian Family, Kharpert, 1907
This photograph identified as a "terk-i tabiiyet" photograph by Prof. Zeynep Gürsel.  Please credit appropriately when using this image.
For more information see Prof. Gürsel's lecture sponsored by Project SAVE:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uRmV6fj9KXA
Left to right:
Elmas Vartanian Aharonian, mother
Loosaper "Lucy" Aharonian
Hosrovedoohght "Anna" Aharonian
Maritza Aharonian
Katchadoor Aharonian, father
Notes on back of photograph indicate theat Katchadoor was placed into the photo in Worcester by Melikian, a photographer, and that he was a head shorter than his wife.
Notes also say: "All passport photographs had to be taken by a Turkish photographer. Anna would not stand in front of a Turk; she had to be given prayer beads to keep her quiet. "

title: Music student Anushig Babayan playing a bamboo flute
place:
Soviet Armenia
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description:
Music student Anushig Babayan playing a bamboo flute, Stepanavan, Soviet Armenia, c. 1980s; photo by Zaven Khachikian.
Anushig, a 5th grader at the Hovhannes Toumanian Middle School, also attended music school where she took piano and 'flute' lessons.  In the summer she played with the children's orchestra which performed concerts for vacationers in Stepanavan. Here she plays the srink (sring) which has 7 or 8 finger holes, sounds like a piccolo, and is typical of Eastern Armenia in the Caucasus.
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Aram Ohanian

New York
USA
description:
Part of the New York Suni Chorus, 1939, the year Suni died; the conductor seated in front is Harutiun Samuelian, Suni's conducting and musicology student; photographer unknown.
Photo donor Ferdinand Kaimakamian, musicology student of Grikor Suni, wrote about his teacher for a preface to Suni's Song Bouquet Vol. 4.  Find it at www.suniproject.org.  Ferdinand's sister Nectar Kaimakamian Pagdigian, who came with her brother to the United States in 1936, stands 4th from the right.
Courtesy of Ferdinand Kaimakamian; special thanks to Armena Marderosian for her knowledge of Grikor Suny's life.

title: Armenian Drama "Namus"
place:
Iraq
British Mandate of Iraq
Ottoman Empire
description:
Armenian play, Namus (Honor), Kirkuk Iraq, 1937; Amateur Dramatics produced by Sheram Baboorian (who became the priest Der Nerses Baboorian); Zaroohi Hamparsumian (later his wife) standing far right; photographer unknown.
Namus, orginally a novel by Alexander Shrvanzade (b.1858 Shemakha, Azerbaijan; d. 1935 Yerevan, Armenia) is the story of two young lovers and their tragic fate. In 1926 it was made into a silent film in Soviet Armenia, and through the years there have been various adaptations of this by many community theater groups.

title: Actors in play by William Saroyan
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
Mr. Kosak (William R. Murray), the Slovak grocer who, though poor himself, gave to others less fortunate, and his daughter Esther (Mae Grimes) in William Saroyan's first play "My Heart's in the Highlands," a Group Theater production at the Guild Theater, on Broadway, New York City, 1939.  Saroyan's message about the spirit of reaching out and giving is universal.
One year later, William Saroyan (b. 1908 Fresno, California; d. 1981 Fresno) won both the Pulitzer Prize and the New York Drama Critics' Award for his second play "The Time of your Life," which in 1948 was adapted into a film starring James Cagney.
Courtesy of Jacqueline Papazian Kazarian, Saroyan's niece.

title: The Krikorian Family, Van
place:
Van
Van Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Krikorian Family (spelled Gregorian in France) Van, Historic Armenia c. 1913
The matriarch Santought Aranigian Krikorian holds her first grandchild, born to her son Manoog Krikorian (seated) and his wife Calipse Poksrarian Krikorian (standing right). The other two women, names not known, are believed to be unmarried Krikorian daughters. This grandchild died of measles and two others died of chicken pox and "fright" from the terror of the Genocide before they were three years old. The fate of the matriarch and the two young women is not known.
During the Genocide the faily fled on foot from Van and the advancing Turkish Army, escaping east to Yerevan, Armenia. In 1921 Manoog and Calipse's fourth child Krikor was born in the Independent Republic of Armenia. Six months later, they made their way west ot Istanbul. According to their Ottoman passport they remained there until 1927, when they sailed to Mareseille France. On the eve of WWII they came to the United States.
Courtesy of Camille Gregorian, daughter of Krikor

title: The Der Mamigonian family
place:
Erzinjan
Erzincan
Erzinga
Yerzinga
Erzerum vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Der Mamigonian family, Erzigan, c. 1900
Seated, wearing a yasma (head scarf) is Yeretsgin (wife of proest) Surpoohe Aladinian Der Mamigonian, whose husband died ten years earlier as a result of over-zealous fasting during Lent.
Tradition would have a priest's son follow in his father's footsteps and study for the priesthood. However, Yeretsgin Surpoohe pleaded with the Bishop to excuse her only son, Hmayag "Harry" (standing center), from becoming a priest. Instead, he taught in the Yerzinga Getronagan Varjaran (Central School) under the jurisdiction of the Bishop, who told him to grow a mustache to look older than his students. Hmayag and his sister Ziadzan "Rainbow" (left), both well educated, immigrated to the United States in 1903, and then years later convinced Yeretsgin to join them.
The woman seated with her son is Yeretsgin's sister's daughter whose husband was killed by the Turks in 1895. Their names and fate are unknown.
Courtesy of Pauline Mamigonian Hovsepian, daughter of Hmayag

title: Funeral for Sarkis Kazanjian, Husenig
place:
Husenig
Hussenig
Husenik
Ulukent
in Kharpert
Husenig/Hussenig/Husenik/Ulukent/in Kharpert
description:
Funeral for Sarkis Kazanjian, Husenig, Kharpert, 1900-1902
Family and friends gather around the deceased for one of three funeral services- at home, at church, and at the cemetery. The priest holds his Manual of Special Services. Sarah, his widow, holds a photograph of their son Manoog Kaznajian, sent to her from America where he was living.  living in the USA. Customarily, the body was washed by family members, dressed or shrouded, and interred without a coffin, all on the day of death.
Sarkis Kazanjian's family occupation was coppersmithing and they owned much property and were wealthy. Sarah Boornazian Kazanjian, wife of the deceased died 1907-1914. Arakel Kazanjian, the other son Sarah and Sarkis Kazanjian, was born in 1882 and was about 20 years old in the photograph. He died in 1962 in America.

title: The Yemenidjian Family
description:
The Yemenidjian Family, Adana, Ottoman Empire, 1913; photographer unknown.
Repega and Krikor Yemenidjian pose with their children and Krikor's mother. Little is known about the family, but details of their wearing apparel reveals them to be well to do. The fez (brimless hat) Krikor wears indicates that he was a businessman.

title: Lousaper Kussajikian and Harry Sarmanian
description:
Lousaper Kussajikian and Harry Sarmanian, married just the day before on June 19, 1929, Havana, Cuba. Photographer unknown.
Although their families from Marash were related, they had never met each other. Harry, an American citizen living in Boston, arranged with Lousaper to meet and marry in Havana. Lousaper, a nurse with the Near East Relief in Beirut, was then able to enter the United States.

title: Wendell Brown, Derindje, 1919
description:
Man identified as Wendell Brown standing near train tracks, Derindje, 1919. Train cars can be seen in the background.
Handwritten caption: Wendell Brown, Derindje
(Cataloger's note: Wendell W. Brown of Yonkers, NY sailed on April 23, 1919, and served at Oulou Kishla. He married Margaret E. Dixon at Constantinople on January 14, 1920, and they returned to the U.S. on February, 22, 1920. https://neareastmuseum.com/memorials/
Both Margaret Dixon and Mary Hubbard were passengers on the U.S.S. Leviathan, which sailed on February 16, 1919, and both women worked in Sivas http://groong.usc.edu/orig/ak-20150216.html  See Araxi Hubbard Dutton Palmer (Araxi Palmer)  K2001.040

title: Portrait of Victoria Chamlian Texoon
description:
Victoria Chamlian Texoon (photo donor's mother), a 'picture bride', Constantinople, c. 1923
Photograph addressed to her future husband, Mr Harry Texoon, New York, NY.
Written on back of photograph in Armenian:
"Please accept this picture where sadly the photographer has obscured the smile that I placed on my lips. I hope that the picture will always be in your mind and it will give an effect more beautiful than the real thing. V. Chamlian. "
Harry lived near the Gulamirian family in New York. Araxie Gulamirian, who was from Constantinople, introduced Harry to Victoria by letter.
Translated by Mary Arutunyan.

title: Wedding portrait of Avedis Avakian and Gadar Tekmejian Avakian
description:
Wedding portrait of Avedis Avakian and Gadar Tekmejian Avakian, the photo donor's parents.
Worcester, MA. 1915
Based on notes from an October 1995 conversation with their daughter Armenie Kajarian:
Gadar lost her father in 1895, when the Turks shot him. She married her first husband when they were both 16. In 1905 rumors started that Turks were going to kill all the young men in Malatia, so her husband was sent to Adana for safekeeping. Sadly, he was killed in Adana. After her husband died, she gave birth to a baby boy who only lived for 2 to 3 months.
In the US, as a widow, Gadar lived with the Yazmadjian family in Watertown and worked at Hood Rubber at a stand up assembly line. The Yazmadjians encouraged her to remarry and they arranged her marriage to Avedis, who was also from Malatia.
Avedis was very quiet and didn't tell a lot of stories. Armenie, their daughter, learned about him from her mother. Avedis was at a draft age when he was a young man in Malatia. His family gave him to an Arab shepherd, who dressed him in Arab clothing, to protect him. He eventually made his way to Egypt, where he was a dishwasher, and from there to Marseilles, France. Because he had trachoma he couln't immigrate to America. He got a job shoveling coal on a freighhter and when the ship got close to the American coast, Avedis jumped overboard.

title: ARF parade float
description:
Armenian Revolutionary Federation (ARF) parade float, “How to knit rugs in Armenia” on Whitinsville Cotton Mill (WCM) Farm truck Whitinsville, Massachusetts, 1919; photographer unknown.
Patriotism towards both the United States and the newly founded Republic of Armenia are shown by the flag-bearing soldiers. Two Armenian men at left wear American suits and hats, and the women demonstrate traditional Armenian spinning and weaving. Weaver, Sirarpe Johnson, is the daughter-in-law of spinner, Sarah Hamamjian wearing head scarf, who was born in Basmashen, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire.  Sirarpe's husband Peter is seated at back of float.
Courtesy of Michael Peter Johnson, son of Sirarpe and Peter

title: Haighganoosh Fendekian Serverian with her husband Haiguin Serverian, and the groom's niece Vartouhi Charian
description:
Haighganoosh Fendekian Serverian with her husband Haiguin Serverian, and the groom's niece Vartouhi Charian, October 2 1923, Bridgeport, CT; photographer unknown.
The bride, Haighganoosh, came to the United States as a picture bride, although she didn't have a photograph of the stranger Hovaguim Serverian, who was to become her husband. They were married in a civil ceremony the day Haighganoush arrived; a week later they were joined in matrimony in an Armenian ceremony and had this photo taken with the groom's niece.
Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives, Inc. Courtesy of Haighganoosh Fendekian Serverian

title: Fendekian Family, Paris
description:
Haighganoosh Fendekian Serverian's family photograph [Serverian 25-78] on the picnic cloth unites two branches of the Fendekian family separated by the Atlantic Ocean. Paris, France, 1930
Courtesy of Haighganoosh Fendekian Serverian
This photograph brings together, in a clever yet simple way, two branches of a family separated by the Atlantic Ocean.
The Fendekian family had escaped from Tokat in central Turkey during the Genocide and hoped to go to America. By the 1920s they had made their way to Paris, but were unable to continue to the United States due to a new, restrictive United States immigration quota.
When an opportunity arose for Haighganoosh (the photo donor) to marry an Armenian in the United States, her widowed father urged her to do so. He comforted his shy daughter with the thought that he and her sisters and brother would join her soon Haighganoosh made the trip by herself and married the stranger, Hovahuin Serverian, the day she arrived. Her family, however, was never able to join her, except for one sister many years later.
Every year Haighganoosh, in Bridgeport, Connecticut, sent her family photograph to her father, Semon, in Paris, France. In 1930, Semon brought the latest photograph along on a picnic, set it on the picnic cloth, and unted everyone in a toast before the camera.

title: Play by young women's Friendship Circle at Armenian Church of the Martyrs
description: Tom Thumb's Wedding produced in English by the young women's Friendship Circle at Armenian Church of the Martyrs, Worcester, Massachusetts, c. early 1940s; photographer unknown.  The American public was so taken with the spectacle of the actual wedding in 1863 of legendary General Tom Thumb, the 2.5 ft. tall entertainer with Ringling Bros. Barnum and Bailey Circus, that the event was written up as a humorous play performed for decades by community theaters throughout the country.  It was a great opportunity for children to be cast as Mr. and Mrs. Tom Thumb.

title: Dinner honoring General Antranig Ozanian
description:
Dinner reception honoring revolutionary hero General Antranig Ozanian, seated center back beneath the crossed French flags, Marseille, France, June 3, 1919; photographer unknown.  General Antranig, 54, left Armenia tired and discouraged, and made his way through Europe to France and England before coming to the United States where he lived the rest of his life.
Courtesy of Mary Samourkashian; information source: Antranig Chalabian, General Andranik and The Armenian Revolutionary Movement, pp. 466, 468.

title: Voski "Gold" Martin
description:
Voski “Goldi” Martin, Boston, 1911, photo by Suren Diran.
When Project SAVE collected this photo from Phyllis Okoomian, she provided detailed documentation about her mother.  We borrowed the original photo, did our archival processing which included making a copy negative and color slide, and returned it to her.  Some years later, Barbara and Peter Schultz, members of the Photographic Historical Society of New England, purchased a collection of undocumented vintage photographs of Armenians while attending the Brimfield Antique Show in western Massachusetts, and donated them to Project SAVE.  Among the photos was this image of Voski!  With Phyllis’s previous documentation we were able to piece together the family connections in the Schultz collection.
Courtesy of Phyllis Nakashian Okoomian and Barbara and Peter Schultz

title: The Babaian brothers
description:
The Babaian brothers, Antranik, Levon (photo donor's father), and Karob; Lynn, MA, 1912.
The Babaian brothers were all born in Lynn. Their father, Mooshèr, arrived in Providence, Rhode Island, in 1894 from Bitlis, Historic Armenia, where he had been the Magistrate, well known by everyone. Their mother, Araxie Erganian, arrived at Ellis Island in 1906 as a picture bride, and married her intended several days later.
Mooshèr was well known in Lynn where he ran a coffee house, helped Armenians in need, and became a court interpreter.
Courtesy of Elizabeth Babaian, daughter of Levon

description:
Wedding at Bird's Nest orphanage, Jbeil, Lebanon, c. 1939, photographer unknown. The bride, Lily Jacobsen grew up as one of the adopted Armenian daughters of Maria Jacobsen, the Danish director of the orphanage who devoted her life to her "children". Leading the wedding procession are other younger orphans who like Lily had no family ties and were also adopted by Maria Jacobsen.
Written on back of the photograph in English: "The wedding procession."
Courtesy of Aznive Toughmanian Aznavorian who as a young orphan herself at Bird's Nest, helped take care of baby Lily.

title: The Kondazian Coat Manufacturing Co. factory sewing room
description:
The Kondazian Coat Manufacturing Co. (now the Eastern Clothing Company). Inside the factory sewing room. 1912. Watertown, MA
Sewing room, M. S. Kondazian Coat Factory, 18 Elm Street, Boston, Massachusetts, February 15, 1912; photographer unknown; photo appeared in M.S. Kondazian’s advertisement in the Hairenik Armenian language newspaper.  Minas S. Kondazian immigrated to Boston from Kharpert in 1891, and started his business in 1898 making men’s suits. By 1905 he had moved his growing business to the Elm Street location in this photograph. In 1924 he purchased land in Watertown and built the Kondazian Coat Factory building. He also developed property behind the factory with two-family houses for his factory workers on a street bearing his name. His son, and later his grandson, continued the business until the early1960s when they sold it to Eastern Clothing Company, which still has its showrooms at the Kondazian factory building in Watertown.
Courtesy of John Airasian, current owner and son of the founding owner of Eastern Clothing Company

title: M. H. Gulesian, Copper and Galvanized Iron work Moses Gulesian, copper-works owner
description:
M. H. Gulesian, Copper and Galvanized Iron work, Harrison Avenue, Boston, 1901. Moses H. Gulesian, a master coppersmith from Marash, stands with his staff between their lion and unicorn which still grace the Old State House. Later, Gulesian organized the effort to save an American icon, the U.S.S. Constituion, a 1797 Battleship nicknamed "Old Ironsides." Behind Gulesian stands James Michael Curley, who was later Mayor of Boston.
Moses Gulesian, center front, copper-works owner, with his staff and their cast of the lion and unicorn for the Old State House. 1901, Boston, Massachusetts; photo by Elmer Chickering.
In 1883, Moses, a 17-year old immigrant from Marash, Cilician Armenia, arrived penniless in New York.  Today Armenians remember him for saving scores of refugees fleeing the Genocide, and Americans recognize him for his philanthropy.
Courtesy of Vincent Beck, Mrs. Leon Dodge, and the Armenian Museum of America.
credit_line: Courtesy of Vincent Beck, Mrs. Leon Dodge, and the Armenian Museum of America.

title: Three generations of the Gulesian family
place:
Marash
Kahraman-Marash
Kermanig
Cilicia
Halab Vilayet
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Gulesians, Marash, Cilicia, Armenia, c. 1892.
Even as the photograph on the left was being taken, widow Margaret Gulesian, her four sons, daughters-in-law, and their children knew they had no future in Marash. After the massacre of Armenians in 1893, when Hovsep narrowly escaped to Alexandria, Egypt, 40 members of this extended family began making plans to leave their ancestral home. In 1897, Margaret and Hagop's wife Esther and children left Marash to join Hovsep, already in Marseilles, France.  Within a year they were all in Boston.
Decran Gulesian, the little boy seated at the left, told how, in 1898 "mother dried up madzoon (yogurt) in cheese cloth and brought it to the US. It took the first time."

title: Bedros Atamian
place:
Ukraine
Austro-Hungarian Empire
Russian Empire
Soviet Union
description:
Bedros Atamian. Elizavetgrad, Ukraine. Date and photographer unknown.
Bedros Atamian was a renowned Armenian actor in Constantinople, Tiflis, and throughout Russia.  He was beloved for his Shakespearean roles—King Lear, Othello, and especially Hamlet, which he played opposite Siranoush's Ophelia. The Bedros Adamian (Eastern Armenian spelling) Tbilisi State Armenian Drama Theatre honors this great actor who was nicknamed “The Armenian Salvini” by Russian critics and theater lovers after the great Shakespearean actor Tommaso Salvini.
The photographic image is on a glass plate with hand coloring, glass cover, and paper taped binding.
Courtesy of Zaven Der Hagopian, and Dr. Michael J. Taylor.

title: Merobé Sahag Kantarjian, "Siranush"
description:
Merobé Sahag Kantarjian (b. 1857 Constantinople; d. 1932 Cairo), known professionally as "Siranush," was a popular young actress in Constantinople's Armenian and Turkish theaters starting in 1874.  In 1878 when the Sultan banned all Armenian plays in the Ottoman Empire, she went elsewhere, including Tiflis and Baku, and did tours with her own theatrical group to Greece, Egypt, Bulgaria, Rumania, and Russia, with over 300 roles in her repertoire.  She was also a director, producer, and teacher.  In 1901, she played the male role of Hamlet, an extraordinary event.
The photographic image is on a glass plate with hand coloring, glass cover, and paper taped binding.
Courtesy of Zaven Der Hagopian, and Dr. Michael J. Taylor.
credit_line: Courtesy of Zaven Der Hagopian, and Dr. Michael J. Taylor

title: Soviet Revolution Day, Yerevan
place:
Yerevan
Erivan
Russian Empire
USSR
Republics of Armenia
description:
Soviet Revolution Day, Yerevan, Soviet Armenia. Armenians threw their red flags on the ground to protest the Soviet lack of defense against Azerbaijan's aggression. Later that winter the situation would grow worse, as the Kremlin gave Azeris a free hand to blockade Armenia and Artsakh and attack supply convoys. November 8, 1988. Lenin Ave, Yerevan, Armenia
Written on back of the photograph: "Shortly after the flags were thrown down, people not yet quite aware of what they've done, not believing the eyes, the idol of so many years in dirt, unbelievable!"

title: Hovhannes Abelian, renowned actor in Tiflis
description:
Hovhannes Abelian (b. 1865 Shamakh, Karabagh; d. 1936 Yerevan), renowned actor in Tiflis at turn of the 20th C., whose mentors were Bedros Atamian and Siranoush. His great roles were Father Superior in Levon Shant's Ancient Gods; Karl Moor in Schiller's The Robbers, and Shakespeare's Othello and King Lear.  He in turn mentored upcoming stars of Soviet Armenia's stage.
The photographic image is on a glass plate with glass cover and paper taped binding.
From lantern slides courtesy of Dr. Michael Taylor, Zaven Der Hagopian, and Zareh Der Hagopian
credit_line: Courtesy of Zaven Der Hagopian, and Dr. Michael J. Taylor

place:

Adapazari
