Adapazar/Adapazari/Kastamonu Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Teachers and staff, Armenian Girls’ High School, Adapazar, 1910.
Adapazar Girls High School was an American Protestant missionary boarding school located about 80 miles east of Constantinople. The student body was Armenian, and the teachers were both American and Armenian.
"Teachers 1910":
First row (left to right):
1. Armenia Kavaljian, Ada-Pazar, music teacher; sister of Dr. Zarouhie Kavaljian
2. Flora Kassabian, Ada-Pazar, Kindergarten teacher
3. Parouhie Tahmazian, Ada-Pazar
4. Armenhouie Galajikian, Istanbul, preparatory teacher
5. Priscilla Nodar, Chengiler, preparatory teacher
Second row (left to right):
6. Surpouhie Lazian, Ada-Pazar, Kindergarten teacher
7. Madame Tossounian, French teacher, Armenian husband
8. Mary Kinney, Milford, Massachusetts USA, literature and history teacher (died after appendicitis operation)
9. Laura Farnham, California USA, head mistress
10. Madeline Gile, USA, normal class teacher
11. Surpouhie Malkhasian, Bolis (Gedik Pasha), geography and anthropology teacher
12. Hranoush Arzoumanian, Bandurma, sewing teacher
Third row (left to right):
13. Dziadzan Hovagimian, Trebizond, Kindergarten principal
14. __ Paravazian, Turkish teacher
15. Housep Djedjizian, principal of school, pastor of church
16. Hovaness Alexanian, Ada-Pazar, Armenian teacher
17. Dr. Zarouhie Kavaljian, Ada-Pazar, medical doctor, physiology teacher, sister of Armenia Kavaljian

description:

The families of the hars and pessa were originally from Bitlis, and they arranged the marriage. Photographer unknown.

title: Boghos Balian
description:
Portrait of Boghos (Paul) Balian, Lyon, France, c. 1927-1930.
Photo donor's uncle (mother's brother).
Per notes from the photo donor: "My grandfather, who was in the Turkish army, did not want my uncle to be conscripted so he took him to Lyon to be with friends. My grandfather's intention was that the rest of the family was going to join him. This never happened because my grandmother had a small business in Istanbul. My uncle could never return to Turkey and so he lived his life in France and is buried in Lyon."

title: Refugees on the campus of St. Paul's Institute,
place:
Tarsus
Darzon
Cilicia
Adana Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Refugees on the campus of St. Paul's Institute, Tarsus 1909, photographer unknown.
In 1909 Armenians found refuge within the walls of this American Protestant Missionary School. Returning from deportation and exile in 1920, Armenians were again sheltered at St. Paul's while awaiting the return of their homes occupied by the Turks.

title: Paul Sarkisian with his cousins
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
Paul Sarkisian, 3rd from left, with his cousins. New York City, c. 1915-1917.
L-R:
1. Paul Hagopian
2. Krikor Hagopian
3. Paul Sarkisian, pd's father
4. Solomon Hagopian
All four men came to the United States from Kharpert.
Per notes from the photo donor:
"All of them lost fathers when the Turks came to their town around 1894 and killed all the men in their village. Krikor is the cousin who was in the army with my father."

title: Nishkian's bicycle shop
description:
Unidentified workers and customer in Nishkian's bicycle shop, Nishkian Cyclery - the Bicycle and Phonograph House, Fresno, CA c. 1910
These photos were included in the family archive of prominent SF lawyer Suren Saroyan. He married his cousin Neoma (family name Nishkian- same as Suren's mother) because at the time it was mandatory to marry a fellow Armenian. The family owned Nishkian Cyclery - the Bicycle and Phonograph House from the late 19th century after emigrating from Bitlis (the majority of Armenians in the area they lived were also from Bitlis, known as Bitlistzis) The three photographs show the interior of the shop.

title: Suren Saroyan, George Mardikian and General Shekerjian
description:
Suren Saroyan (left) and George Mardikian (right) visit General Shekerjian (seated). Location unknown, possibly Stuttgart, Germany. 1950
Photographer unknown.
Through their work with the Armenian National Committee to Aid Homeless Armenians (ANCHA), all three men were instrumental in resettling some 25,000 Armenian refugees in the United States from Germany after World War II.
General Shekerjian became chief ANCHA resident representative in 1948, near the Armenian Displaced Persons’ Camp, at Stuttgart, Germany.

title: Album of photographs, Peoples of the Ottoman Empire
description:
Album of photographs, Peoples of the Ottoman Empire
92 carte de visite albumen silver print photographs in an album. The photographs are by Abdullah Frères, Pascal Sebah, C. D. Philippides, Conrad (Russian), and some are by unknown photographers. The album belonged to Kate P. Williams of Mardin, Turkey, 1871.
Album is comprised of ethno-photography studio portraits showing peoples of the Ottoman Empire in the 19th century: Arabs, Albanians, Armenians, Circassians, Egyptians, Greek, Gypsies [Roma], Kurds, Moslems [sic], Persians, Syrians, Turks, etc. as well as different social classes: beggars, water carriers, Maronites, priests, cameleers and more.
Most photographs are 4 1/4 x 2 1/2" in size.
Portraits of historical figures include:
Sultan Abdulaziz
Sultan Mohammed Murad V
Huseyin Avni Pasha
Omar Pasha Latas
Mehmed Emin Aali Pasha
Tewodros II
Click on
this link
to see individual records of each photograph in this album.

title: Three Near East Relief workers
place:
Zahle
Lebanon
French Mandate
Ottoman Empire
description:
Lousaper Keshishian, seated, and her husband Hovsep Keshishian sitting on the arm of the divan.  Lousaper Kussajikian stands in back. Taken in Zahle, Lebanon, in 1923. The three were all working with the Near East Relief organization. Photographer unknown.
Lousaper Kussajikian joined Near East Relief in Marash as a young refugee. She assisted Near East Relief workers in transporting hundreds of orphans out of Marash to safety in Syria. Lousaper studied to become a nurse with Near East Relief. She worked in several Near East Relief facilities in Syria, including the Antilyas Orphanage and the Working Boys' Home in Beirut. Miraculously, Lousaper was able to find her sister Anna in a Near East Relief orphanage in Greece. She brought Anna to Beirut in 1924. Lousaper married Harry Sarmanian and raised a family. She was interviewed for the book The Cilician Armenian Ordeal by Paren Kazanjian in 1989.
Hovsep Keshishian survived the Genocide in Cilicia and joined Near East Relief as a teacher at Aintab Orphanage. Hovsep taught math and English, which he spoke fluently. After the French evacuation from Cilicia, Hovsep helped to evacuate thousands of orphans to safety in Syria. He taught at the Near East Relief orphanages at Beirut, Jubeil, and Hilltop (Sidon) and received commendations for his work. Hovsep worked with the boys at Jubeil to create a beautiful book of botanical samples. In 1926, Hovsep was transferred to the Nazareth Orphanage; he taught there until it closed. Hovsep and his wife Lousaper, who was also a survivor from Aintab, raised five children together.
Courtesy of the children of Harry and Lousaper Sarmanian

title: Deran Tashjian and his children on Christmas
place:
Yerevan
Erivan
Russian Empire
USSR
Republics of Armenia
description:
December 25, 1968, Lenin Square, Yerevan, Soviet Armenia.
Deran Tashjian, a repatriate born and brought up in Watertown, Massachusetts, purposely took his Beirut-born wife Zovig and their children, Nariné (standing) and Armen, for a photo in front of Tzmer Papik (Winter Grandpa) and the lighted Holiday Tree in Lenin Square. Deran was trying to replicate, as closely as possible, the American experience of Santa Claus for his children. Dzmer Papik was the icon the Soviet state invented to replace  Gaghant Baba (the Eastern Armenian version of Santa Claus) and the religious celebration of Jesus's birth on December 25th.  To replace the religious symbolism without sacrificing the festivities, New Year's Dat, a week later, became the holiday to celebrate. Dzmer Papik, a larger than life figure, stood in all Soviet towns and city central squares, in front of a huge lighted tree, starting on the first day of December. Parents, mostly repatriates from Europe and America, took their children for photos in front of Dzmer Papik and the tree. Many repatriates dares, at great personal risk, to celebrate traditional Christmas holiday in their homes with food and fun, often poker games, for all to enjoy, so that the next generation could experience Christmas.
Photo taken by ZovigTashjian.
Courtesy of Deran and Zovig Genjian Tashjian

title: Marchers in support of the Chinese revolution
description:
Marchers in support of the Chinese revolution. Soviet Armenia, c. 1930s.
Translation of Armenian handwriting on the back of the photograph:
"The demonstrators are marching through one of the main streets toward the meeting [demonstration] places."
Translation of Armenian banners in the photograph:
The multi-million masses of China's workers are waging a struggle against the imperialists. Long live the Chinese revolution. Down with Chiang Kai-shek's treasonous pack of wolves"
Photo: Foto-amateur, Tigran Mekhitarian.

title: memorial for Rev. Herbert M. Allen
place:
Van
Van Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Organizers of memorial service for the Rev. Herbert M. Allen, missionary with the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions, Van, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, 1911; photographer unknown.
Rev. Allen served in Van in 1893 after graduating from Bangor (Maine) Theological Seminary.  The inscription in Armenian below Rev. Allen's U.S. flag-draped photograph translates, “In unforgettable memory of Rev. H.M. Allen.”  Ottoman Empire religious flags flank the image.  Organizers left to right: Oriort (Miss) Hasmig, language teacher; Baron (Mr.) Vartan Tarpinian, a merchant; Baron Haratoun, Armenian language teacher; Baron Oksen, a druggist, and Rev. Dr. Ernest Yarrow, American missionary school principal and teacher of Turkish.
Courtesy of  Vart Shirvanian Hachigian, whose mother studied with these teachers
Featured in Project SAVE's 2008 Calendar "Armenians Fly The Flag"

title: Masters Graduation
place:
Ann Arbor
Michigan
USA
Ann Arbor/Michigan/USA
description:
Ann Arbor, Michigan, University of Michigan, 1943; photographer unknown; Alice Odian, MS degree in Pharmacology, receives congratulations from friend Siranoosh.
Alice, a Genocide Survivor, born in Ankara in 1906, arrived in the USA with her parents in 1919. After high school, she faked her age to get accepted into Mass College of Pharmacy, from which she graduated in 1922, then worked at NE Hospital for Women, and later authored several books.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Alice Odian Kasparian

title: Missionaries gathered in Curdian in summer
description:
Missionaries gathered in Curdian in summer 1911.
Written on back in English: I tho't you might like to know what Curdian looks like. It is our summer home further up in the Mt. This was taken last summer (1911) while we were all here at annual meeting. Miss Silliman speaking with Rev. Yarrow [near far?] tent while she holds Robt. M.and Neville U. is near them. Opposite to the left stand Professor Riggs and wife of Harpoot College. Then the Stapletons and Natalie of Erzroum. Lady Betty and I are side by side, and Miss McLaren and Miss Riggs are behind us. Bobby Barstow (Dartmouth) of Mardin in the center of the picture. And don't forget to admire our grand old walnut tree under which I shall loaf all summer.

Silliman, Caroline F.
Yarrow, Ernest
Ussher, Neville
Stapleton, Robert

description:
Elizabeth Barrows Ussher, a missionary in Van and wife of Dr. Clarence Ushher, with her three children, Eleanor and Sidney, front, and Neville, standing, Van, Ottoman Empire, circa 1909 to 1914.
Elizabeth was born into a missionary family in Kayseri. She and her husband were witnesses to the Armenian Genocide, and provided shelter in their own residence in Van for refugees in 1915. Elizabeth contracted typhus and died there in July 1915.

description:
At Aramian Varjaran (school), Sepastia, Ottoman Empire, 1909; poor children receive a free book and new shoes from Serop Kilarjian, known as Loosaper Emmi (uncle Shining Light.) Serop, educated in Germany, served as Director of Steins Deptarment Store in Constantinople and later, in Sepastia, formed the Loosaper Miutiun (Shining Light Society). Using monthly membership dues of 2 cents, he purchased books for needy children. Courtesy of Shinork Kilarjian (Son of Serop.)
Featured in Project SAVE's 1999 Calendar "Armenian Education: A Reverence for Learning"

title: Martha W.T. Raynolds
place:
Van
Van Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description: Mrs. Martha W.T. Raynolds, American Missionary in Van, Ottoman Empire, 1900. Martha opened the Van mission with her husband, Rev. Dr. George C. Raynolds in 1872, and devoted her energies into running schools and orphanages there. Through her husband's time in the U.S. from 1913 to 1915, in order to secure money for Van College, she remained in Van, and along with the other missionaries did her utmost to shelter, feed, and provide medical care to thousands of refugees. When the Russians evacuated the city, they brought the Americans with them to Tiflis, a horrendous journey during which Martha fell from a cart and fractured her leg. She surviived two more weeks before perishing in  Tiflis.

title: Rev. Dr. George C. Raynolds
place:
Van
Van Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description: Rev. Dr. George C. Raynolds, American missionary in Van, circa 1900. Dr. and Mrs. Raynolds opened the Van mission in 1872. A medical doctor who had served in the Union army during the Civil War, Dr. Raynolds established a mission hospital in Van, but also devoted himself to evangelistic work. After the massacres of 1894, he and his wife dedicated their lives to Armenian orphans, and after his wife's death in Tiflis, he returned there in 1916, to work among the orphans until German troops arrived in 1917.  Photo by unidentified missionary.

title: Just married, Mary Tutunjian and Onnik Kouyoumdjian
description: Just married, Mary Tutunjian and Onnik Kouyoumdjian in a Chevrolet, Cairo, Egypt, May 1, 1960; photo by Khatchig Kulhandjian. Prior to their move to the United States, the Kouyoumdjians lived in Soviet Armenia. Seated in the front are Mary's third cousins and best friends Bedros Boghigian and Maro Boghigian (Vatzbed) who  have now moved on to France and Switzerland, respectively. The gentleman standing next to the car is Hapet Boghikian, who was a famous shirt maker in Egypt, and the father of Bedros and Maro.

title: ARF members of the National Assembly
description:
Postcard addressed to Mr. H. B. Chakmajian in New  York, and inscribed in Armenian: "Hello to Arek, Elizabeth, and you, Deroyentz." Dated 1920.
45 members of the National Assembly convened in Paris, February 24-April 22, 1919,  and attempted to harmonize the positions and interests of the Republic of Armenia and the delegation headed by Boghos Nubar pasha who claimed to represent Western Armenians. In addition to geographic representation, the Assembly also included representatives who belonged to various Armenian political organizations. This photo seems to show participants in the Assembly who were members of the ARF (Armenian Revolutionary Federation), though one prominent member of the ARF, Vahan Papazian (Goms) is not in the photo.
Paris Peace Conference delegation in 1919, headed by Avetis Aharonyan.
Standing from left to right:
1- Simon Hakobian
2- Simon Narinian
3- Artavazd Hanemian
4- Dr. Hamazasb (Hamo) Ohanjanian (Participating in an advisory capacity, member of the delegation of the Republic of Armenia to the Peace negotiations in Paris)
5- Manuk Hambardzumian (Hampartsumian) (Representing the US)
6- Garegin Pasdermajian (Armen Garo)
7- Zadig Matigian/Matikian (Representing the US)
Sitting from left to right:
8- Levon Shant (Caucasus, representing Western Armenian refugees; also Deputy Speaker of Parliament in Armenia)
9- Prof. Astvatsatur Khachatrian (Representing Mesopotamia/Iraq)
10- Nikol Matinian
11- Avedis/Avetis Aharonian (Participating in an advisory capacity, member of the delegation of the Republic of Armenia to the Peace negotiations in Paris)
12- Armenag Parseghian/Barseghian (Representing Istanbul)
13- Hakob/Hagop Nevruz (Representing Egypt)

title: American Orphanage in Van
place:
Van
Van Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Some orphans at the American Orphanage for boys in Van, Ottoman Empire, October 1896.
George C. Raynolds, at bottom left wearing bowler hat. Rousas John Rushdoony. Standing at his shoulder with handkerchief in his pocket is [later Rev.] Y. K. Rushdoony, later Rev. Y. K. Rushdoony, was educated by the mission in Marsovan and Edinburgh, Scotland, and served it as an evangelist and teacher, then as a teacher at its college. He was present at both the siege and the evacuation of Van in 1915, before coming to the US.
Megerdich Shirvanian is fifth from left, front row, indicated by X on paper he is holding. Photographer unknown.

title: Armenian Soldiers in Turkish Army
description:
Armenian Soldiers in Turkish Army, Istanbul, 1916.  Hagop Khanzetian, brother of photo donor, is seated second from left. He is the Hagop Chavous, or master sergeant. Third from left is Kirkor Khalian. All four of these men were Armenian, and all went to live in Istanbul after the war. Hagop’s parents were both killed in the Genocide.  Photographer unknown.

title: Ottoman Empire inhabitants
description:
Photograph by unidentified missionary of native inhabitants, probably in Van, Ottoman Empire, circa 1900 to 1914. Written across the bottom in English: As the middle class of natives lives . Babe wrapped in swaddling clothes.
A further note attached by a missionary indicates that they found too-tight swaddling was widespread, and believed this to cause later problems with lameness and clubfeet.

title: The Revitalization of the Holy See of Cilicia
description:
Center His Holiness Karekin I, Catholicos of the Great House of Cilicia
From left to right: His Grace Bishop Khoren Paroyian, His Grace Bishop Zareh Payaslian (Prelate of the Diocese of Beroea [Aleppo]); His Grace Bishop Paren Melkonian, His Grace Bishop Ghevont Chebeyan (Catholicosal Vicar of Cyprus); His Grace Bishop Terenig Poladian (Dean of the Seminary)
As a bit of context, Catholicos Karekin I was elected in 1940, following the death of Catholicoi Sahag II (Kharpertsi!) in 1939 and the shortly lived Bedros I the following year.  Karekin I was living in the USA at the time as he had been serving as the Prelate of the Etchmiadznagan Diocese since 1937. World War II delayed by 5 years his ability to travel to Antelias to be consecrated as Catholicos. He was finally consecrated amid much anticipation as soon as he arrived. I would have to check some older sources, but I'm fairly certain Ghevont Srpazan was ordained as a Bishop in 1947 and I believe Zareh and Khoren Srpazans were around that same time as well. This would, assuming these three have been newly ordained as bishops -- hence the spirit of the inscription "revitalization of the Holy See," place the date of the photo sometime around 1947 or slightly later.- information provided by Rev. Fr. Stephan Baljian

title: Armenian clergy at Lake Van
description: Armenian Clergy of St. Kevork Chuch Monastery Island of Leem in Lake Van, Armenia, Ottoman Empire, late 19th Century The ancient Christian traditions of the Armenian Apostolic Church were maintained by these men at this lonely outpost in eastern Asia Minor. They include a married priest (seated left), hooded celibate priests and bishops, attended by a sexton holding a wooden mallet and gong, and deacons standing behind the priest they served. Photographer unknown.

4. Harry Kaspar BARSAMIAN, father
back row, standing, left to right
5. __________ TOROSIAN, Father's sister Youghaper's daughter
6.Victoria BARSAMIAN (BOULDOUKIAN) (KUDEN), father's sister
7.Youghaper BARSAMIAN TOROSIAN (KOOYUMJIAN) (OHANIAN) (PARSEGIAN)f sis
8. Rebecca BARSAMIAN (_________________), father's sister
9. Prapion BARSAMIAN (KALIGIAN), father's sister
The whole family except for Youghaper, her husband (not in photo) and her 6 children (two of whom are pictured here) came to the United States around 1908-1912. Youghaper's husband and all her children perished in the Genocide. She later came to the United States, remarried, and had one more child, Thomas Kooyumjian.

title: Megerdichian siblings, Seattle
description: The Megerdichian brothers, Arshag and Kevork, and their sister Satenig pose for the camera two years after arriving in the United States from Japan. Sister and brothers escaped the Genocide in Van and made their way to the United States going east through Russia, Siberia, Manchuria, Japan, Seattle and finally, Boston. In Seattle, they opened a business called "Ladies & Gents French Show Repairing." In the late 1920s, Arshag, now known as Arthur, and Kevork, now known as George, headed east to Boston. Satenig married, became "Davidian," and moved to Fresno, California.

title: Shahan Natalie
description:
Shahan Natalie, born Hagop Der Hagopian, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, 1920. Inscription on photo folder reads  "To Unger Hamo from Shahan, Boston, July 8, 1920." Photographer unknown.
Shahan was a writer and member of the Armenian Revolutionary Federation and the principal organizer of Operation Nemesis, a campaign of revenge against officials of the former Ottoman Empire who instigated the Armenian Genocide during World War I.

title: Bardizag countryside
description:
Bardizag countryside just east of Constantinople, Ottoman Empire, c. 1910.
"No 8" at the bottom most likely left refers to this being being the 8th in a series by the photographer, who is unknown.
There was a strong Armenian presence with churches and schools in Bardizag, where none exists today. The Armenian word at the bottom right transliterates as "Asdeeg" and is of unknown meaning.

title: Zabelle Carol Tashjian and Raffi Ronald pose with Santa Claus
description:
Zabelle Carol Tashjian and her brother Raffi Ronald pose with Santa Claus at one of Boston's many department store Christmas celebrations. Santa Claus in America has evolved from our consumer-oriented society to promote good behavior along with good merchandizing. In the weeks before Christmas, the department store Satan Claus welcomes children to sit on his knee and tell him if they have been good or bad and what they want him to bring them for Christmas. Parents purchase the photo taken by a professional photographer. Though laregely secular even with modestly religious name, Santa is heavenly, dashing across the sky in his sleigh pulled by Rudolph and the other eight reindeer!
December 15, 1947
Courtesy of Souren and Luvine Bulbulian Tashjian

title: Photo of Jansuzian children in Sunday clothes
place:
Aleppo
Halab
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Syria
description:
Jansuzian children of Antioch or Aleppo, c. 1909..Front (L-R): Sarkis, Navasart. Behind (L-R): Zavartoon, Mariam (grandmother of photo donor), and Lucia. Family sources say that this portrait was taken as the family was preparing to go to church as they seem to be wearing their church clothes, that were made by the family. They say that Lucia grew into a handsome young woman and was engaged to an Armenian physician serving in the Turkish army, who was killed in the massacres. Lucia is said to have pined away for her deceased fiance and died as a result of her grief. Her fiance is said to have been "skinned."
Jansuzian children, Antioch, Ottoman Empire c.1909
The children are all ready to go to Sunday schoo. The little one in the front is a boy. It was the custom to dress children under two years of age in clothing that today we consider to be for girls.
Couretsy of Anita Sunukjian

title: Reverend Nesbitt Chambers and Major Doughty-Wylie, Adana, 1909
place:
Adana
Ataniya
Adana Vilayet
Cilicia
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Nesbit Chambers and Major Doughty-Wylie, Adana, 1909.
Note on verso: "The Adana Relief Committee in session. Mr. Chambers on left and Major Doughty-Wylie on right."
(William Nesbitt Chambers 1853-1934. He became a Missionary American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions to Turkey in 1879. He conducted relief work in Erzerum Province, relieving 50,000 or 60,000 persons. He moved to Adana in 1899, and was made president International Relief Commission.
Charles Hotham Montagu Doughty-Wylie (1868-1915) was British Vice-Consul in Adana in 1909. He died at Gallipoli, 1915. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Doughty-Wylie)
This photo is a duplicate of CRARY_Douglas_145-88

(Wife of the British Consul, Major Charles Doughty-Wylie and a nurse, she is credited with establishing two hospitals in Adana - a medical hospital and a surgical hospital - before the establishment of the permanent American Red Cross Hospital. title: Armenian Legionnaire Vahan Khederian
description:
Armenian Legionnaire Vahan Khederian in uniform, the photo donor's father, c. 1920. Unknown location.
"Of all the Nirze Legionnaire, Vahan Khederian is the best known. Nicknamed 'the hero of Akardja,' Khederian was a community leader, a member of the Armenian Democratic Liberal Party (ADL) (Ramgavar Party), and commander of a unit of the Legion d'Orient. He was born in Nirze in 1895, where he received his elementary education, and immigrated to the US in 1910. Responding to the 'call of Cilicia,' Khederian joined the Legionnaires movement in 1919 and participated in operations across Palestine and Cilicia as commander of the ADL units in the Cilician plateau. He died in 1976 at the age of 83.

title: "Villie" William Chaderjian and Victoria Chaderjian
place:
Malatia
Malatya
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
"Villie" William Chaderjian and Victoria Chaderjian with their dog Pug, Malatia, Bethesda Mission.
Bethesda was concieved by Herr Ernst Christoffel as a Christian Mission for handicapped children; a school to learn some trade which would make the blind and crippled self-sifficient. The photo donor, William Chad (Chaderjian), is the child at left. His father, Garabet Chaderjian was an assistant administrator at Bethesda and his mother was one of the teachers. The family lived at the mission. According to the photo donor: "In August of 1915, while Herr Christoffel was in Berlin collecting moew funds for Bethesda, my father was murdered by the Mutasarief. Whar Germans were left behind by Herr Christoffel (his sister Hedwig and brother-in-law, Hans) left the mission to my mother and returned to Germany. The Turks tried to seize the building- a sprawling forty room compund- but my mother declared it a German consulate and with the aid of some Turkish friend was able to keep the Turks out of th ebuilding. Mother's open door policy saved many a displaced Armenian refugees from death."

title: Ted and David Kupelian on Christmas
description:
David and Ted Kupelian on Christmas, 1951.
According to the sister of the two brothers, Diane Kupelian: "The photo of my two older brothers was taken one year before I was born, so it was taken in December, 1951, in our house on McKinley Ave near Suburban Hospital in Bethesda, MD.  I'm sure my father, Vahey, was the photographer, and I'm sure my mother, Louise, was making fun faces to get them laughing like that!  My older brother is Ted, and the younger one is David.  (Or, in those days, "Teddy" and "Davey")"

title: Ruins of fire where missionaries Roger and Maurer were killed
place:
Adana
Ataniya
Adana Vilayet
Cilicia
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Adana/Ataniya/Adana Vilayet/Cilicia/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Written on the back of the photo: "Ruins of the fire they had been fighting when Rogers and Maurer were shot." Adana, April, 1909. Photo probably either by Stephen Trowbridge or Harry Gaylord Dorman.
The incident Trowbridge is describing is as follows:
Ottoman authorities denied responsibility in the shooting deaths of two American missionaries in the city of Adana, indicating instead that "the Armenians" killed Protestant missionaries D.M. Rogers and Henry Maurer while they "were helping to put out a fire in the house of a Turkish widow." The Ottoman account of the killings was later contradicted by an eyewitness, American priest Stephen Trowbridge of Brooklyn. Trowbridge indicated that the men were killed by "Moslems" as they attempted to extinguish a fire threatening to subsume their mission. From the New York Times, May 2, 1909.
credit_line: Douglas Crary Collection of Stephen and Blanche Horton Trowbridge, American Missionaries in Aintab

title: Site of killing of Missionaries
place:
Aintab
Gaziantep
Antep
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
According to printed caption on back of photo: The position of Mr. D.M Rogers when shot. Indicated by the cross in the street. Mr. Maurer's by the cross on the roof. No. 2 in a series that appear to have been prepared and captioned for publication. Photographer unknown, likely an American missionary.
The incident Trowbridge is describing is as follows:Ottoman authorities denied responsibility in the shooting deaths of two American missionaries in the city of Adana, indicating instead that "the Armenians" killed Protestant missionaries D.M. Rogers and Henry Maurer while they "were helping to put out a fire in the house of a Turkish widow." The Ottoman account of the killings was later contradicted by an eyewitness, American priest Stephen Trowbridge of Brooklyn. Trowbridge indicated that the men were killed by "Moslems" as they attempted to extinguish a fire threatening to subsume their mission. From the New York Times, May 2, 1909.
Photo by Stephen Van Renssalaer Trowbridge.
credit_line: Douglas Crary Collection of Stephen and Blanche Horton Trowbridge, American Missionaries in Aintab

Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Lebanon
description:
Armenian mothers and their babies with the staff of the Near East Relief Clinic, Beirut, circa 1925.
The Near East Relief provided maternity and child care for these Armenians who otherwise would have been at high risk of death and disease. The staff included the photo donor's mother, Lousaper Kussajikian (Sarmanian) the nurse standing back, third from the right. Identified staff in the back row include Dr. Khosrov Krikorian, fifth from left. Photographer unknown.

description: Near East Relief medical staff in Beirut, March 7, 1927. The original caption reads "Beirut. To: Mr. C[harles W.] Fowle, Director. For remembrance from all members of his medical staff." Front row: Mesag (last name unknown), unknown man, Mr. Derhouie. Second row: Elizabeth Bender Kunzler, Jakob Kunzler, unknown man and woman, Dr. Khosrov Krikorian, Dr. Wilson Dodd, unknown woman and man, general practitioner Dr. Bedir, Mrs. Persape Bedir, unknown woman. Third row: The woman in plaid is nurse Sarab Der-Bedrossian. The young woman just right of center (behind the woman with glasses) is Lousaper Kussajikian. . Sima Artinian and pharmacist Haig Artinian are at the far right. Fourth row: dentist, Dr. Serabian is at the far right. Special thanks to Joann Janjigian and Cathy Salibian for assistance with identifying the people in this picture.

Erzinjan/Erzincan/Erzinga/Yerzinga/Erzerum Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
This extended Palanjian family had another almost identical photograph taken around 1903--the same matriarch, name not known, sitting in the same photo studio chair in Yerzinga, Historic Armenia, with the same backdrop and carpets. The date of this image is 1914, by using the age of Shooshanig Palanjian, standing back left, who had attended  Euphrates College in Kharpert, Historic Armenia from 1912 to 1913, but was unable to return as the outbreak of war and the plight of Armenians worsened.  Only she survived the Genocide of 1915.  Photographer unknown.
Courtesy of Araxie Derderian

title: Der Hayr Matteos Mateosian with his son’s family
place:
Habousi
Ikizdemir
Kharpert Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Der Hayr (Rev. Father) Matteos Mateosian seated with his son’s family; Habousi village, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire. A larger original family photo was taken in 1912 and sent to Der Hayr’s son Krikor who was then working in the United States. This smaller composite photo was put together in Boston years later: other extended family members were cropped out, and Krikor was added in; photographers unknown.
In 1915 Turks tortured Der Matteos for a month and then killed him. His daughter-in-law Yeghsa Kanadanian Mateosian, standing behind him, faithfully brought him food every day during his month in prison. Only she and her older daughter Anna, at left, survived.
Courtesy of Anna Mateosian Miller, Der Matteos’ granddaughter, through the kindness of Charlotte Peters Nalbandian.

description:
Large group of children stretching or doing exercises. The photograph was most likely taken of a Young Pioneer Camp. Soviet Armenia, c. 1930-40s.
Handwritten on back on Armenian:
"Dear Tcho,
All the instructions you have given in your letter to Shishko will be done, if sons of a bitch don't get lazy. I'll be sending you soon one of my [illegible] I think you'll like it very much. Why don't you write to me? I am always preparing myself to write bar my laziness takes over. Regardless, I remember you with fondness. I am following the "Yerevan" newspaper. .
With salutations,
Melikset"

title: Gregorian church in Antioch after destruction
place:

Antakya
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
According to the description provided by the collector, this photo shows "The courtyard of the Gregorian [Armenian Apostolic] church, Antioch, strewn with the plunder returned to the Armenians by the [Ottoman] government. The "plunder" was not the real plunder taken in the massacre, but old rags and broken furniture. From the balcony in the background the priest was thrown headlong by the Moslems after he had been tortured to death. Of the congregation 112 men out of a total of 135 were killed, nearly all the women surviving!"
Photo probably by Rev. Stephen Trowbridge, American Missionary to Aintab, 1906 to 1911.

description:
Soviet Armenian kindergarten children do their daily routine of calisthenics designed to develop muscular tone and promote physical well being. The order and regimentation of the exercises was consistent with the Soviet Union’s Communist ideology. This press photo, without identification of the photographer, was most likely used to promote a public health message: fresh air, sun, and moving the body is the best medicine.

title: Christmas in Soviet Armenia
place:
Yerevan
Erivan
USSR
Republics of Armenia
Soviet Armenia/USSR/Republic of Armenia
description:
Children join Santa holding peace doves with Russian letters saying “Peace for the world.” Soviet Armenian school, probably Yerevan, c. 1950s. Names of subjects and photographer unknown.
Many Armenians around the world celebrate Gaghant (New Year’s Eve) with traditions familiar in the US as Christmas. On New Year’s eve, Gaghant Baba (New Years Eve Father)- comes down from Mt. Ararat dressed like a Santa Claus and brings gifts to put under the donatzar (holiday tree). Gifts are exchanged, and family and friends gather for the celebration of the New Year. Christmas observed on January 6 is similar to Easter, when after church services family and friends gather for a festive meal.
The children hold paper doves with letters attached which say “Peace to the World.” The overall message of the photograph depends on the beholder: it could be considered a coded Christian message, a sign that religion was practiced despite prohibitions, or merely a tool of Soviet propaganda in the midst of the Cold War.
Courtesy of Jack Torosian

title: Spot in Adana where missionary Henry Maurer was shot
place:
Adana
Ataniya
Adana Vilayet
Cilicia
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Handwritten on back of photo: The spot where Maurer was shot. The ruins of the fire in the background.  The incident Trowbridge is describing is as follows:
Ottoman authorities denied responsibility in the shooting deaths of two American missionaries in the city of Adana, indicating instead that "the Armenians" killed Protestant missionaries D.M. Rogers and Henry Maurer while they "were helping to put out a fire in the house of a Turkish widow." The Ottoman account of the killings was later contradicted by an eyewitness, American priest Stephen Trowbridge of Brooklyn. Trowbridge indicated that the men were killed by "Moslems" as they attempted to extinguish a fire threatening to subsume their mission. From the New York Times, May 2, 1909.
Photo probably by Rev. Stephen Trowbridge, American Missionary to Aintab, 1906 to 1911.
credit_line: Douglas Crary Collection of Stephen and Blanche Horton Trowbridge, American Missionaries in Aintab

description:

title: A skillful Arab weaver and his primitive loom weaving the finest of Syrian silk
description:
A skillful Arab weaver and his primitive loom weaving the finest of Syrian silk. Mt. Lebanon, Syria. c. 1900
This skilled weaver readies his shuttle to pass to the other side of the warp threads. He powers his loom with his feet on the pedals. At the right, notice the balls of warp weighted with stones to keep the fabric straight.

title: Winding the fine raw silk in a large American silk throwing plant
description:
Winding the fine raw silk in a large American silk throwing plant. Paterson, NJ. c. 1900
Skeins of raw silk, imported from China or Japan, are stretched over the hexagonal reels below and, under the watchful eyes of the "winders", the silk filaments (warp threads) are wound onto the small spools above. Many Armenians were employed in Paterson's silk industry.

title: Bahar Sahagian Malkasian with cousin and daughter
place:
Bazmashen
Pazmashen
Bizmishen
Kharpert
Harput
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Mezireh, Kharpert, Ottoman Empire, 1907. Photographer unknown.
Bahar Sahagian Malkasian with her cousin Hagop Sahagian, the son of her mother's brother, and her daughter Zarouhi Malkasian (later Mooradian.) Though the photo was taken in Mezireh, they apparently lived in Bashmashen.
This photograph identified as a "terk-i tabiiyet" photograph by Prof. Zeynep Gürsel.  Please credit appropriately when using this image.
For more information see Prof. Gürsel's lecture sponsored by Project SAVE:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uRmV6fj9KXA
Bahar's husband, Sarkis, was already in Whitinsville, Massachusetts, having immigrated in 1900, working in a wire mill. In 1907, Bahar and Zarouhi came to join Sarkis via Samsoun to Marseilles. Hagop eventually immigrated to Vienne, France.

title: Yeghia Mooradian and friend Garabed Bedrosian
description:
Cabinet photograph, taken in unknown location soon after the beginning of Armenian Genocide in 1915. Two friends, Yeghia "Eli" Mooradian, the donor's grandfather, and Garabed Bedrosian. Eli was an orphan of the Genocide.
The handwritten message on the back in Armenian reads as follows: "Where are you my dear friend, Yeghia Mooradian.[?] I'm waiting for your letter. Your friend, Garabed Bedrosian." The whereabouts of Garabed at this writing or subsequently are unknown. Both of them were from Bazmashen.

title: Warping view in a great silk weaving plant
description:
Warping view in a great silk weaving plant. Paterson, NJ. c. 1910
Here in the warping room, the small spools of warp threads are placed on the pegs of the vertical 'tables' at the right. These threads are wound around the huge creels at the left (8 yards in circumference), measured, and rewound on large warping spools behind the warping creel, ready for weaving.

title: Machines Weaving Dozens of taffeta silk ribbons
description:
Machines Weaving Dozens of taffeta silk ribbons. Paterson, NJ. c. 1910
Weaving ribbon is similar to weaving fabric- the main difference is that one loom can weave 20 to 30 ribbons at a time. Note: the vertical warp threads at the top of the loom; the separated warp threads at the back of the horizontal plain with the bobbin ready to pass through; and the spools of woven ribbon near the floor.

title: Finishing processes for silk cloth, breaking, folding and pressing in a Paterson silk mill
description:
Finishing processes for silk cloth, breaking, folding and pressing in a Paterson silk mill. Paterson, NJ. c. 1910
Silk fabric, as it comes from being woven, dyed, or printed, is often too stiff, thin, or full; or is rough, soiled, or lustreless. In this room are many kinds of machines which, with the nimble fingers of the workers, remedy such defects.

title: Festival of Nations Armenian Exhibit
description:
Members of the Nakashian/Keljik family at the Festival of Nations' Armenian exhibit.
Festival of Nations, established in 1932 by the  by the International Institute of Minnesota,  St. Paul, Minnesota, circa 1937. Photo by P.G. Robinson.
Festival of Nations, established in 1932 by the International Institute of Minnesota, is still held today bringing Americans of all backgrounds together.  It features artisans, food, music, and dance of over 40 ethnicities.  The 2009 festival included Armenians spinning silk and folk dancing.
Sarkis Arsene Nakashian, center, provided the rugs for this exhibit. His daughter, Happy Nakashian, left, is wrapped in a tablecloth as a costume. Seated to the right are Araxy Keljik and Arsine Nakashian, cousin and sister of Happy.
Featured in Project SAVE's 2010 Calendar "Armenians Roll Out the Carpet"

Sarkis Isbirian, born in Caesarea and founder of Isbirian Oriental Rugs, was the rug concession at Luckey, Platt & Co. beginning in 1927, and in other stores.  Later, he opened his own store front in Poughkeepsie, where today his son John continues the business.
Courtesy of Armine Arslanian Isbirian, wife of Sarkis
Featured in Project SAVE's 2010 clendar "Armenians roll out the carpet"

title: The Harootunian/Garabedian family
place:
Kharpert Vilayet
Harput Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Harootunian/Garabedian family, Mezireh, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, early 1890s; photographer unknown. The seated woman is the elder sister, by 20 years, of the girl at the right.
During the last 100 years of the Ottoman Empire, first the military, then civil servants, and then most upper class men and boys, both Muslim and non-Muslim, wore the brimless, maroon-colored fez. In 1925 at the beginning of the Turkish Republic, Mustafa Kemal Ataturk banned the fez in an attempt to end customs associated with the Ottoman Empire. However, in other countries formerly part of the Ottoman Empire, older men continued wearing the fez through the 1950s.
Courtesy of Jean and Andre Johnson (Andre's grandmother Higanoosh at right) and Zevart Hollisian (her father Garabed at left)
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Jean Johnson

Her husband Anoush Krikorian, born in Akhlat, Bitlis, snaps the picture. c. 1935, Van Courtland Park, the Bronx, New York.
This hilly section of the park was a popular Armenian picnic spot. Sosy, who always loved to dance, is a well-known dance leader and teacher who passed along Armenian dance traditions to the next generations of Armenians in America.

title: Building 41 Watertown Arsenal
description:
Dikran Karibian, center, father of photo donors, worked in the core-making area, Building 41, Watertown Arsenal, Watertown, Massachusetts, 1963; U.S. Army photograph.
The U.S. Army established and operated the Watertown Arsenal from 1816 until 1968, with research activities continuing until 1995.  It employed hundreds of Watertown residents.  In 1968 the eastern half of the Arsenal was transferred to the General Services Administration and sold to the Watertown Redevelopment Authority.  Building 41 was razed in the mid-1970s, and its site became a parking lot in the Arsenal Mall.
credit_line: The children of Dickran and Seranoush Karibian

title: Portrait of Dr. M. Simbad Gabriel (Sumpad Kaprileian)
description:
Portrait of Dr. M. Simbad (Sumpad) Gabriel, circa 1900. Portrait was taken at a studio in New York City. Photographer and studio is unknown.
Mgurdich Sumpad Kaprielian emigrated to the US to escape persecution for his political activities in Turkey.
As Dr. M. S. Gabriel, he was very active in speaking about the persecution of Armenians on Turkey,  often appearing in the NY Times.
By 1905, he was already starting to wither from hard work. This photograph is therefore likely taken around 1900.
Contextual information provided by a descendant of the Kaprielian family.

title: John C. Kebabian's room at Yale
description:
John C. Kebabian's room at Yale, New Haven, Connecticut, c.1885; photo by Leopold.
In 1878, at age sixteen, John emigrated from Rodosto (today Tekirdag, Turkey) on the European side of the Bosphorus, Ottoman Empire.  While a student at Yale, he began importing Oriental rugs and other textiles from Constantinople and selling them from his room. Today, the company he founded, Kebabian Oriental Rugs in New Haven, is the oldest rug business in the United States still operated by members of the same family.
Courtesy of Paul Kebabian, son of John.
Featured in Project SAVE's 2010 Calendar "Armenians roll out the carpet"

title: Virginia Meltickian in China
description:
Virginia Meltickian, Harbin, China where she grew up, 1932; photo by Avgoustov Studio.
Virginia was born in 1927 just over the Russian border in Inner Mongolia, China, the daughter of refugees from the Armenian Genocide-her father from Dikranagert, Western Armenia; her mother from Vanadzor, Eastern Armenia.
The teenage children of the Russian émigré photographer dressed up five-year-old Virginia like a doll in a costume of the Boyars, the highest social class during the Tsars' period.

title: Mesak Kermoyan at the Panama-Pacific International Exposition
description:
San Francisco World's Fair, 1915.
Mesak, born in Bitlis, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1885, took advantage of the Exposition photographers many costumes and props to pose as Armenian royalty, ready to defend the honor of his heritage. He owned Hollywood Florist in the heart of San Francisco. At home he loved to cook - his specialty was the Bitlis traditional tuhtoo (fermented cabbage soup).

description: The Tarzian Family: Mackrouhie and Hagop with daughter Valentine and son, Antranig; Dikranagert, Ottoman Empire, 1912; photo by Jacob Asfar, an Armenian/Assyrian married to Hagop’s first cousin. Note that Hagop, a butcher by trade, carries a knife in his godee (cloth wrap-around belt). After Hagop died in 1913 of natural causes, Makrouhi and Antranig journeyed to America to join members of her family and prepare for her mother and daughter Valentine to join them. But mother and daughter were killed in the Genocide before they could leave for New York. Courtesy of Antranig Tarzian, Union City, New Jersey

title: S. Telfeyan Company
description:
S. Telfeyan Company, Constantinople, Ottoman Empire, late 19th century,  baling Oriental rugs for shipment; photographer unknown.
The Telfeyan family was originally from Caesarea where in the 1850s they were in the silk business and dabbled in rugs on the side.  By the 1880s they had started a rug business in Constantinople with agents throughout Europe, Asia, the Middle East, and America.  They had an office in Manchester, England, and looms in Kashan, Persia-their trademark rug was the Manchester Wool Kashan.
In 1888 Sarkis established S. Telfeyan & Co. in New York City.  When he died in 1914, his brother John took charge of the business with younger brothers Herant and Albert, who later split the business between their sons.  Today Telfeyan Carpets, run by Sarkis' grand nephew Gary Telfeyan, is in Garden City, New York, doing broadloom sales and some Oriental rug repair and cleaning.
Courtesy of Garabed Telfeyan, son of John, father of Gary.

description:
Sign in Armenian held by men at left and right reads: "Sourp Purgich Vank (Holy Saviour Monastery) of Ourduz/in its dilapidated condition/1913." Ourduz is about ten miles from Malatia, Kharpert province, Ottoman Empire; photographer unknown.
Legend has it that here there was a special water that could cure many illnesses.
Assuming that this is an interior altar, an enormous amount of artificial light and a long exposure time was necessary to record this wide spread of faces and achieve as much detail as there is.

title: Brothers Albert and Carl Kapikian
description:
Brothers Albert and Carl Kapikian, left and center, visit their dear cousin Jack Zakian, right, at his family home where rugs are drying, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, c. 1930s; Jack's father Christopher Zakian was the likely photographer.
Hagop Zakian and his son Christopher began their door-to-door rug cleaning business in West Philadelphia in 1923, transporting the rugs to their home where they washed and dried them. In 1956 Zakian Brothers (Christopher, Aram, and Haig) moved to its current location-still in operation today-run by Haig's son Robert.  Cousin Jack helped run the business from 1960 to 1980.
Courtesy of Lucy Bazikian Zakian, wife of Christopher and mother of Jack.

title: Enkababian Family
place:
Sepastia
Sivas
Sepastia vilayet
Sivas vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Enkababian Family, Sivas, Turkey c. 1914.
Only three people in this photograph survived the Genocide, including Antranik Enkakbian, the photo donor's father.
People:
1st row
1. Mirijan's daughter (name unknown)
2. Antranik
3. Barkev
4. Haigaz
2nd row
5. Zarouhi
6. Mirijan
7. Mariam (Antranik's grandmother)
8. Araxi is the baby on Mariam's lap
9. Armenak
10. Mariam Gezurian Enkababian (Antranik's mother)
11. Karekin
12. Vartan
3rd row
13. Dirtad
14. Haig
15. Anna (last name unknown) Sultan's sister
16. Sultan
17. Zarouk Baliozian
18. Haiganoush
Only three members of the Enkababian family escaped the genocide. The family was deported from their home in Northern Turkey in June 1915. Antranik's father was killed during the journey and his mother Mariam decided to commit suicide by drowning herself and her children in the Euphrates River. Antranik (who was only six at the time), his four year old brother, and his mother survived. But, Antranik's two-year-old sister Araxy drowned.

title: The Ashjian Family
place:

Angora Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Three generations, Yosgat, Central Anantolia, c. 1900
Sahag Ashjian (center) with his son Artin (seated right) and Artin's wife (seated left) and the grandchildren, names not known.
Aram Ajemian, the photo donor, borin in Yosgat in 1913, was a survivor of the Genocide. He grew up and married in Beirut where he owned a shoe store and served as chairman of the Yosgat Hairenagtsagan Mioutiun (Compatriotic Association). He made it his life's work to collect and document the history and photographs of Yosgat, and this photo is from his collection. He and others from the Mioutiun published "The History of Yosgat". He retired to the United States.
Courtesy of Aram Ajemian, and Mike Youssefian who carefully saved his friends' photographs and book manuscript and donated them to Project SAVE Archives.

title: Melkonian brothers and Haroutyoun Tchaylakian
description:
Krikor and Garabed Melkonian, sitting is their nephew Haroutyoun Tchaylakian. Most likely Anatolia, c. 1870-80s.
In 1882 Harutyun Tchaylakian moved to Egypt with his maternal uncles Krikor and Garabed Melkonian from Kayseri, and by 1888 they established a cigarette factory in Cairo as well as tobacco shops in various parts of the country. The Melkonians were among one of the first Armenian families from Anatolia to start a tobacco businesses in Egypt in the 1880s, encouraged to do so by the country's more liberal political and economic environment, the availability of abundant and inexpensive labor force, as well as its suitable climate for the processing of a variety of cigarette types. After many years of work in the Melkonian factory, Harutyun Tchaylakian established his separate firm, which did not survive beyond one year owing to stiff competition, and the devastating consequences of the great economic depression of 1907. In the end, Tchaylakian had no other choice but to end his venture and to rejoin the Melkonian factory. Its owners Garabed and Krikor apparently appreciated his valuable expertise, and hence appointed their prodigal nephew as their factory's managing director.
Source: Torosian, Bedros Puzant. "In Search of Haven and Seeking Fortune: The Economic Role of Ottoman Armenian Migrants in British-Occupied Egypt (1882-1914)"  Dissertation. Beirut: American University of Beirut, 2019.
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/335639097_Bedros_Puzant_Torosian_Thesis#read

Noble
Mr. Kevork [Der Sarkissian?]
My close friend [literally, close-to heart]

seems to be your face. Although painfully hard, your life of an exiled has the [roots?] of
your future successful, confident, and comfortable future, if you know [how to fight?]...
Onward, don't be discouraged
[?]
Sarkis [?]

title: Armenian soldiers with clergy before battle, Echmiadzin, Armenia, c. late 1910s photographer unknown.
place:
Etchmiadzin
Ejmiatsin
Vagharshapat
Russian Empire
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description:
Armenian soldiers with clergy before battle, Echmiadzin, Armenia, c. late 1910s photographer unknown.
“Armenian volunteers of the Caucasus taking the oath of allegiance administered by unidentified Armenian clergy before leaving for battle in October 1914.” It has also been attributed to General Dro’s (Drastamad Kanayan) Second Armenian Volunteer Division of the Imperial Russian Army. However, research about the rifles with their long sword-like bayonets reveals them to be of Turkish issue. Armenians had access to this weapon only after winning major battles against the Turks in the Caucasus and Turkish Armenia in 1915 and 1916. Therefore it is difficult to know the exact date of this photograph.
Courtesy of Lillian Kassabian, additional research by Gilbert Nersesian

description: Armenian orphanage, Ordu, Turkey, 1918. The orphanage was run by Armenian teachers from Russia and Constantinople with financial aid from wealthy Russian and American Armenians. During the Genocide, these Armenian children had been separated from their families and forced to live in Turkish homes. After the Genocide, they were rescued and placed in the orphanage only to be caught in subsequent massacres and compelled to flee Ordu. Courtesy of Azniv Kouyoumjian Nazaretian, teacher, second row, second from left.

title: Rug merchant, Sultanabad
place:
Paghkn
Paghakn or Pahakn
Sultanabad
Shurabad
Berdashen
Shirak
Armenia
description:
Rug merchant Levon Davidian, center without hat, Sultanabad, Persia, December 16, 1925, with Persians—townsmen, soldiers, and guards, and Armenians—three in back (also not wearing hats) and two wearing brimmed hats; photographer unknown.
Levon rolled out the carpets to celebrate the previous day’s crowning of Reza Khan as the shahanshah (king of kings) of Persia.  Originally from Adana, Ottoman Empire, Levon traveled throughout Persia buying and shipping rugs to Michaelian & Kohlberg in New York City.  In 1937 he moved to New York and became an importer.  In 1960 his son John founded House of Davidian in Cleveland, Ohio, which he still operates today in Chagrin Falls, Ohio.
Courtesy of Armen Davidian, brother of Levon

title: Infantry Private John der Alexanian, bugler
description:
Infantry Private John der Alexanian, bugler, Camp Upton; WWI Induction Center, Long Island, New York, 1917; photo by unidentified Army photographer.
Pvt. Alexanian, 27, originally from Palu, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, volunteered for the US Army so he could be sent overseas to fight the Turks. However, because he was not yet a US citizen, the Army kept him in the States. At Camp Upton he blew reveille at 5 a.m.- to wake everyone- including Irving Berlin who wrote "God Bless America" and "Oh How I Hate to Get Up In the Morning" while being inducted and trained there.
One morning Pvt. Alexanian was awakened earlier than usual by the faint distant sound of a bugle playing a fire warning. He quickly roused his mates and received commendation for his action. He was assigned to a base in Georgia where he received his American citizenship and played clarinet in an Army band. He was honorably discharged at war's end in 1918.
Courtesy of his son, Edward Alexander

This information added by Zeynep Kezer, a researcher and scholar:
The Building on the right known as Bes Kardeslar(Five siblings) belonged to the Fabrikatorian family, owner of a local silk factory. The patriarch originated from Arabkir and his last name had once been Ipekjian. All of the family with the exception of two daughters-in-law died in the genocide.
During the early years of the republic the building was used as an orphanage. Official  [Turkish] histories portrayed it as a noble donation of local immigrants to the USA. 

title: The Bogigian Family
place:
Mezireh
Mezire
Mezraa
Harput
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Bogigian Family, 1898.
The family of Bedros and Miriam Bogigian (seated) with their four children, circa 1898, taken in Kharpert Province, Ottoman Turkey, most likely in Mezireh (El Azig). Standing on the right is the mother of the photo donor, Varter, about 14 years old. She married Mugrditch Nazarian a year later. They had six children. Mugrditch and all their children were killed in the Genocide, as well as the Bogigian parents. Varter and her brothers, pictured here, survived. Varter later came to the United States, remarried, and had another child before dying at age 44 in 1929.

title: Picture Bride
description:
Picture bride, Cairo, Egypt, c. 1922, photographer unknown.
Rose Hovsepian posed for this picture (the only one she ever had taken) especially to send to her future husband in Dover, New Hampshire. Rose emigrated without her family, had six children, and was widowed at a young age when her husband died of pneumonia.

title: Artin Efendi Vartarian with daughter and a business partner
description:
1. Artakes (Artin Efendi) Vartarian, from the village of Bellegesi, near Nearza
2. Daughter of Artakes, Nevart Hadji (Bedrosian Chalikian), the photo donor
3. Zakar Zakarian
The two men were business partners as plasterers in Constantinople. Once a year Zakar would return to home to Nearza.
Translation of Armenian writing using Turkish words on the back of the photograph:
"1914 May 10/23 Kenale Aida
My dear sister, accept our photo Zakar Zakarian, Artin Effendi Varterian, Nevart the daughter of our uncle,
to all of you sincere greetings.

title: The Kazarossian sisters
description:
The Kazarossian sisters, born in Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, reunited in Belmont, Massachusetts, 1949; photo by Yeprem Gechijian. After nearly fifty years of separation, Sister Marie Jenny D'Arc, nee Mariam (center), or Marseilles, France, reuinted with her sisters, Takoohi Avakian (left) of Providence, Rhode Island, and Varter "Rose" Bagdigian of Belmont, Massachusetts.
Courtesy of Natroohy Bagdigian Gechijian, Rose's daughter and Yeprem's wife.

title: Sarkis and Hadjekadan Vezirian and family
place:

Cesarea
Kayseri
Gessaria
Ankara Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Sarkis and Hadjekadan Vezirian, left and standing, with their two daughters Antrig and Mairenie, and Sarkis's mother Goulerank Tekian Vezirian.
Tomarza, near Talas, central Anatolia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1900. Photographer unknown.
It is not known if this family survived the Genocide.
Sarkis wears a fez indicating his merchant status; Hadjekadan (maiden name not known) wears her dowry necklace of gold coins and is perhaps pregnant- note the drape of her jacket; Gouleranl wears a traditional log fur-trimmed coat; and daughter Mairenie already has a start on her dowry necklace.

title: Sagharian and Vartanian Families
place:
Kharpert Vilayet
Harput Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Sagharian and Vartanian Families of Yegheke Village of Kharpert, circa 1913. Photo by Soursourian.
Everyone in this photo was massacred in the 1915 Genocide, with the exception of Nevart Sagharian, seated at right, front row, and her father Haroutune Sagharian, standing at far right. Nevart's pregnant mother, Esther Kaprielian Saharian (pictured next to Haroutune) along with Nevart's sister, brother, grandmother, aunt, cousins and extended family were all killed. Her mother's sister, Kermis Kaprielian Mouradian, cared for Nevart in Kharpert until she left for the United States in 1923.

place:
Aintab
Gaziantep
Antep
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Levonig Nazaretian Sulahian and her husband Hrand Sulahian and their children, left to right: Marie and Kevork.
Aintab, Ottoman Empire, c. 1902; photographer unknown.
In 1915, the family escaped to Damascus where Hrand was imprisoned. Fifteen-year-old Marie went to the prison and sang in Turkish, begging that her father not be hanged. The prison guard was moved, ad her father was spared. Later, Hrand was elected to the Syrian parliament.
Courtesy of Artemis Topjian Nazarian, Marie's daughter.

title: Sisters Louise and Lily Ann Zeytounian
description: Sisters Louise and Lily Ann Zeytounian, ages eight and twelve, pose on this empty street in Rosny-sous-Bois, a small town east of Paris near Ecissy-les-Moulinoux, one of the centers of the Paris-Armenian community. The handwritten message on the back of this black and white snapshot dates the event as Sep. 1944, just days after the liberation of Paris from German occupation during World War II. Everywhere streets were empty of privately-owned vehicles because the Germans had confiscated them. One can just imagine the colors of Victory the sisters are wearing. Their identical dresses and hand-knit argyle knee socks undoubtedly make a great patriotic statement. And their four-wheeler roller skates, called 'patins à roulettes' en français, strapped over their shoes quite possible provided a mode of transportation for them during the war. Now they look like they are ready to glide into a “Vict’ry Polka.” And no scrapes on their knees.
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Astrik Aznavorian

title: Hovaness and Hasmig Aslanian Garabedian and family Erzerum, Historic Armenia, c. 1907
place:
Erzeroum
Erzurum
Garin
Karin
Erzurum Vilayet
Russian Empire
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Hovaness and Hasmig Aslanian Garabedian with their children. Erzerum, Historic Armenia, c. 1907
Seated on the floor, daughters Nuvart (left) and Zuvart, display hats from their father who, as an international merchant, often returned home from his travels with such gifts. Some time after this photo was taken, their mother died, and later Hovaness remarried.
Hovaness knew of the evil brewing against Armenians in Erzerum but left secure in the relationships he had established with the Turks- the family shared their well water with Turkish neighbors and opened their summer home to the Turkish mayor. Yet in 1914 when an American-Armenian came Erzerum looking for a wife, and fell in love with 14 year old Nuvart, Hovaness allowed the marriage that took his daughter to a safer place.
Sometime after that he and his son Levon rode off on their horses to "a meeting with the Turks" and never returned- they were beheaded. Nuvart's sisters and stepmother eventually came to the United States.
Courtesy of Gladys Mosesian Ayvazian, daughter of Nuvart.

title: The Karagheussian Family
place:
Smyrna
Izmir
Smyrna Vilayet
Aydin Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Greece
Turkey
description:
The Karagheussian Family, Smyrna, c. 1905
Zartae Demirjian (seated left) and her husband Sarkis (center), a businessman, with their six children. Some time after this photograph was taken, the family moved to Kharpet where Sarkis was owner of an inn. Son Bedros (in front of Sarkis), died in Kharpet of pneumonia. During the Genocide, their eldest daughter Satenig's (standing right) husband, Hadju Sarkis Demoordjian (seated right), was killed. The family was torn apart. Six survivors were scattered between Cairo, Egypt, and Worcester, Mass. Satenig settled first in Providene, RI, where she remarried and had two children before moving to Worcester, MA.

title: The Charshavjian and Topalian Families. Dikranagert
place:
Dikranagerd
Diyarbakir
Amida
Diyarbakir Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Charshavjian and Topalian Families. Dikranagert, Historic Armenia, 1889
The photo donor, Vahan Topalian, is the youngster on his Uncle Mardiros Charshavjian's lap. Standing left to right are Vahan's mother Hiripsime Charshavjian Topalian, her mother Sousan (maiden name not known), and Sousan's brother's daughter, all wearing clothing typical of married women in Dikranagert. Vahan's father was alive at the time- it is not known why he is not in the photo.
Vahan and his brothers came to the United States before 1915. He worked his way through Mount Hermon School in Greenfield, Massachusetts. He pursued his passion for books throughout his life while working as a tailor and for 25 years as a valet at Boston's Copley Plaza Hotel. In 1945 he founded the Armenian Cultural Foundation to house his collection os book and objects d'art. He died in 1983.
Courtesy of the Armenian Cultural Foundation, Inc.
Featured in Project SAVE's 2005 Calendar "Armenian families in the Ottoman Empire before 1915"
This photograph is a contact print from a glass plate negative

Vartouhi (seated), maiden name not known, her husband Hadji Garabed Helvadjian, a wealthy businessman, and their daughter Guilane all died in the Genocide. title: Baptism
place:
Aleppo
Halab
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Syria
description:
Sirarpie, left, and her brother George Boshgezenian, hold candles after their baptism, Aleppo, Syria, July 5, 1926, photo by Derounian Frères.  Parents, Elias and Verkin Tachtajian Boshgezenian, originally from Aintab, penned this note on the back of the photo to their nephew Levon R. Zenian in Boston: “We send you this picture to remember our children’s baptism.” The children are older than usual for baptism in the Armenian Apostolic Church, and they hold their own candles.  Typically a child’s godparent holds both candles.
Courtesy of Rose Zenian Cravotta, daughter of Levon.

description: Diran Deirmendjian, Kartal, Gulf of Ismid, Ottoman Empire, 1917; photographer unknown.  This photo postcard includes message: To my medz mayrigis (grandmother) from Diran.  Diran’s immediate family was spared during the Genocide because his father, Navasart, was a physician in the Turkish army. But Navasart’s father, grandmother, and grandfather were all killed.  In 1921, the remaining family fled to Greece, and later, one by one, came to the United States.

title: Armenian school Christmas nativity scene, Addis Ababa
description:
Armenian school Christmas nativity scene, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, 1926; photographer unknown.
Photo donor, Vahram Goekjian, a Genocide Survivor, author, and community activist, first settled his young family in Ethiopia where a small yet vibrant Armenian community thrived, anchored by the Armenian church, school, and community organizations. The banner above the Nativity scene says Armenian School in three languages: Amharic (the official language of Ethiopia), Armenian, and French.
Courtesy of Vahram Goekjian, whose son Samuel portrays baby Jesus.

title: The Hazarian sisters
place:
Constantinople
Istanbul
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Constantinople/Istanbul/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
The Hazarian sisters: Rebecca, Araxi, and Yevnigé; Constantinople, Ottoman Empire, c. 1912; photographer unknown.
The sisters’ father Hagop, a businessman in Caesarea, central Anatolia, sent his wife Hranoush with their daughters to Constantinople for safekeeping during the Genocide. He joined them there after World War I. Hranoush wanted to go to the United States, but died before that was arranged. Her daughters eventually immigrated to Paris, France via Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, and never married. Their cousin, Arthur Mihran, living today in Paris, remembers “the sisters with very sad feelings, because they had a tragic destiny and really deserved to have a happier one. They never complained, but actually never integrated anywhere. They were victims of the tragic Armenian history, and they suffered their loneliness in silence.”
Courtesy of Haroutune P. Hazarian, the sisters’ uncle (father’s brother)

Hazarian, Araxi

place:

Harghan
Cilicia
Adana Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Graduating class with the sign “Ser yev Miutiun” (Love and Unity), Hadjin High School for Boys, Hadjin, Cilician Armenia, Ottoman Empire, 1913. Haroutune Izmirlian (first row left), the valedictorian, had to memorize his graduation speech in both Turkish and Armenian so that if he noticed a Turkish official entering the room, his next words would be in Turkish. Avak Mehagian (second row left), a faculty member and recent graduate, later became a successful businessman in Phoenix, Arizona. D.H. Rajebian (second row, center) the President of the high school, immigrated to California.
Courtesy of Leon Izmirlian, son of Haroutune

title: Demerjian and Tatoulian gathering, including survivors of the 1918 siege of Hadjin
description:
Demerjian and Tatoulian gathering, including survivors of the 1918 siege of Hadjin. c. 1960s
L-R: "Armen Demerjian, Never Demerjian Tatoulian, Mayda Tatoulian, Missek Tatoulian, Anoushian [names illegible in photo donor's notes]
Standing: Varoujan Tatoulian, sitting: Aguline Khandjian Tatoulian, Arpine Tatoulian Anoushian, Arshalouig Demerjian Fermanian Yergat" [names illegible]
One of the few survivors, Aguline Khandjian Tatoulian, third from right, was 18 at the time of the Hadjin siege and was a resistance fighter. She escaped by hiding in a cave overnight with other survivors. She was injured with a bullet in her hip that remained in her body the rest of her life.

title: Medical officer
description:
Levon Samuelian was born in Mezireh, Kharpert, circa 1890 and graduated from medical school in 1914-1915. He was drafted, commissioned and sent to the Front as a medical officer. Because of his service in the Ottoman Army, his family in Mezireh were not deported and remained there until 1922.
When Levon learned in 1915 that the Ottoman Army in which he was fighting against the British was comitting genocide against his own people, he sought an opportunity to surrender to the British, which he succeeded in doing. He was taken prisoner and sent to India until the war ended in 1918.
After his release, he settled in Egypt in the Cairo suburb of Heliopolis where he practiced medicine for the next 30 years. In 1922 he sent for his parents and sisters from Mezireh,Turkey.
The experience of Levon and his family are described in the book "Farewell, Kharpert" by Bogos Jafarian.

title: Kaspar and Nartuhe Arakelian Barsamian and family
description:
Kaspar and Nartuhe Arakelian Barsamian, natives of Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, with four of their five daughters, their son with arm resting on his father’s shoulder, and two grandsons (standing between their grandparents), who are daughter Victoria’s (back right) children, Fresno, California, c. 1912, shortly after moving there from Worcester, Massachusetts; photographer unknown.
Courtesy of Thomas A. Kooyumjian, son of the oldest daughter Yughaper, who at the time of this photo lived in Kharpert with her husband and six children. Of these, only she survived the Genocide. In 1920 her parents sponsored her to come to the United States where she remarried and gave birth to Tom. Thanks to Tom and his cousin Pamela Barsam Brown, daughter of the only Barsamian son, Harry, for assistance with documentation. Pamela suggested the year is more likely 1910, but without direct knowledge from those in the photo, an approximate date is the best we can do.
credit_line: Courtesy of Thomas Kooyumjian and Pamela Barsam Brown

title: Vartanants Memorial in Gyumri
place:
Gyumri
Leninakan
Alexandropol
Shirak Province
Russian Empire
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description:
Our volunteers Margaret and Jim Favre 2015 visiting the Vartanants Memorial in Gyumri, Armenia.
Led by General Vartan Mamigonian, the Armenian army of 66,000 faced a Persian army 4 times larger at the battle of Avarayr in 451 A.D. Despite massive losses, Armenians won the right to worship freely as Christians.
The Feast of St. Vartanants falls on the last Thursday before Lent, in honor of the man who represents courage, sacrifice, patriotism, and unfading faith.
The General is portrayed elevated on the horse surrounded by religious and political leaders who also led the resistance for Christianity. L-r: Prince Arshavir II of Kamsarakan, Sahakanoush, daughter of Saint Sahak the Great (General Vartan's mother), Fr. Ghevond Yerets, and Catholicos Hovsep I.

title: Kachadoorians in front of their family business
description:
Jack Kachadoorian, center, his uncle Jack, left, and father Tommy, in front of their family business, Tech Market, Russell Street, Worcester, Massachusetts, c.1973; photographer unknown.
The Kachadoorian brothers, Jack (left) and Tommy (right), and Tommy's son Jack (middle) pose in the early 1970s on Russell Street, Worcester, Massachusetts. The brothers' father Aharon started Tech Market in 1939 in the neighborhood of Worcester Polytechnic Institute. He was already known as the best butcher in town, and now he had his own store.
When Aharon retired in 1945, his sons bought the business. Though meat was still their specialty, they sold all types of groceries. Worcester residents today fondly remember Tech Market's cheerful service and home deliveries. The Kachadoorians were community minded, proud of their Armenian heritage, and active members of the Armenian Revolutionary Federation (ARF). WC 109

title: Dollar a Bushell Fundraising for Musa Dagh
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
Fundraising rally in NYC Audubon Hall, 1920, photographer unknown.
Fundraising in response to the siege of Mugsa Dagh, slogan "Dollar a Bushell" suggested by Capt Jim Choukalian the chairman of the ADL appealing to all Armenians to contribute $1.00 for a bushel of wheat (meaning bread) for the refugees of the heroic Musa Dagh.
At the Dollar a Bushell Mass meeting- addressing, Mrs. Onnik Sinanian. From left to right, Shahan Natali Fuad Mufarrij, Yervant Messian, Archbishop Hovsepian, Dr. Avedis Kaye, Mardig Baliozian and Vahan Kurkjian.
Also speaking at the rally was a Syrian representative to the UN who praised the Armenian people for their dedication for survival and their deep Christian faith.
Courtesy of Araksi Sinanian, photographed at the podium.

title: Evangelical Central Union Meeting
place:
Marsovan
Merzifon
Sivas vilayet
Sepastia vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Marsovan/Merzifon/Sivas Vilayet/Sepastia Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
1901 June. The missions of Central Turkey (Cesaria, Marsovan and Sivas) and the delegates from the missions of Bethany, Kharpert and Cilicia, as well as American Missionaries held a meeting in Marsovan. The most important issue was to restore the five seats reserved for the local representatives, an issue that was postponed because rumors were circulating about possible massacres. It was an important meeting. After 3 or 4 days the government ordered to suspend the meeting and the delegates returned home.
Capition from Adanalian Book, page 284
In 1901 the annual meeting of the Evangelical Central Union took place in Marsovan. For that meeting Prof. Hagopos Jejizian came to Marsovan. Since his fame had spread in the neighboring cities and villages, many came to Marsovan to see him and listen to his sermon. When he came up to the pulpit, both the church and the yard were so cortoded that the neighboring Apostolic priest led the people from the evangelical church to the larger Apostolic church. There, Prof. Jejizian delivered a wonderful sermon.
This happened when Sultan Hamid was in power. The governement ordered Jejizian to leave, and the meeting was closed early. This photo was taken at that time. Most of them are ministers, preachers, professors and missionaries. It is sad to remember that in 1915 most were martyred. (translated by Aram Sarkisian)
Courtesy of Marie Bashian Bedikian

title: Koutoujian, Marash wedding
place:
Marash
Kahraman-Marash
Kermanig
Cilicia
Halab Vilayet
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Wedding portrait: Abraham and Zarouhi Der Bedrosian Koutoujian, Marash, Cilician Armenia, May 15, 1909; photo by Alexianos Uvezian; Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives, courtesy of Peter Koutoujian, their son, Waltham, Massachusetts.  Abraham and Zarouhi pose in typical upper-class fashion of a hars yev pesa (bride and groom) in Marash during the first decade of the 20th century—he, clutching his worry beads, is very ethnic in his wool shalvar (abundant, low-crotched pants) full of pleats at the waist, and lace-embellished bolero jacket over a silk shirt with lace collar and cuffs, seated as if a king in charge of his destiny; and she, handkerchief in hand, stands tall and stately like a queen in her crushed silk-velvet, European-styled gown of an undetermined rich color trimmed in lace, with her dowry of gold Ottoman coins around her neck and a crown of fresh daisies on her Victorian hairdo.  Little did they know that during Genocide their two children would die, and that they themselves would be separated for four years. In 1923 the International Red Cross miraculously reunited them in the United States. Their second set of children, three sons and a daughter, are well-known in the Boston area for their public and community services. Their six grandchildren continue the tradition of service to their communities, as will, no doubt, their eleven great grandchildren.
Courtesy of Peter Koutoujian, their American born son.

title: Albert's cleaners
description: Businessman Armenak “Albert” Mardirossian, center, with his staff in 1946, was both Baron Armenak and Mr. Albert to a wide range of customers at the cleaners and tailoring shop he started in 1929 in Passaic, New Jersey.  Born in Bulgaria, he grew up in Constantinople, and as a young man witnessed the Genocide. He immigrated to the United States in 1921 and became a citizen in 1927.  With the success of Albert’s Cleaners, Baron Armenak ventured into real estate development. He became a well-respected Armenian community leader and philanthropist, remembered especially for his 35 years of service as president of the Emerson, New Jersey Home for the Armenian Aged.  Courtesy of Albert Mardirossian

title: Grigor Ookhtens and his brother Hagop
description:
Grigor Ookhtens and his brother Hagop just after arriving at the Kategyugh orphanage, Istanbul. 1919. Grigor is the photo donor's father.
Grigor is the second one from the left. Hagop is the tall one standing in the back.
After the orphage was closed in the early 20s, they left for Greece. There, they lived and worked for a few years before returning to Armenia. Due to the poor conditions caused by the Society system, they escaped to Iran by swimming across the Araks river at night. Grigor and his family lived in Iran until 1974 when they finally immigrated to the U.S. They lived in Los Angeles until Grigor passed away in 1993. After the 1920s, Hagop remained in Greece and eventually left for France. He resided in Marseilles until he passed in 1974. Hagop passed away weeks after Grigor had one final chance to visit him as he immigrated to the U.S.
