title: Pampish Mariam (Mary) Koundakjian working on lac
description:
Pampish Mariam (Mary) Koundakjian working on "hand work" lace, date, location unknown.
She was from Hassanbeyli, a mountainous region in Cilicia. A part of a letter she wrote in 1909 was discovered by her grandson Harry. She wrote the letter on May 7, 1909 to her three daughters after the massacre of her husband, Rev. Hagop Koundakjian of Hassanbeyli of the Armenian Evangelical Church. Hagop "was killed on the way to Adana, burned alive inside his church with his 28-person congregation, and did not see the sudden destruction and premeditated attaches our city." "On April 11th 1909 we had our Communion. I was rather heartfelt ceremony. Nobody knew that this would be his last sermon. On the next day, Father journeyed for the annual Conference of the Armenian Evangelical Churches. As you might already have heard, all in the group were burned alive in Osmanya and your father was killed with the 28 delegates of the congregation from our church. We, the womenfolk, were driven, exiled towards Bakche, where we are until today." Letter printed in in the Armenian Weekly, 90th Anniversary Commemorative Issue, April 24, 2005 pg. 33.
Courtesy of Harry Koundakjian

title: Opening of Star Market
description:
Opening of Star Market, Morton St, Mattapan section of Boston, Massachusetts, 1974, photo by Mike Horwitz/Atlantic Photo, Boston.
Left to right: Rev. Harold G. Ross, Jr., John Mugar, and Dr. Kenneth Guscott.
Rev. Harold G. Ross, Jr., Baptist minister, worked with the Association for Better Housing to help Black families obtain home mortgages in the neighborhood of the new Star Market where there had been no grocery store; John Mugar, President of Star Market Company, connected with community leaders to bring jobs and a market offering fresh food and fair prices to benefit their neighborhood; Kenneth Guscott, nuclear engineer, businessman, community activist, and President of the Boston branch of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), was a personal friend of John's.
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of John Mugar

Sarkis Tchooldjian (later changed to Kazanjian), seated left, owner of copper factory in Dikranagerd (now Diyarbekir), Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1900; photographer unknown. title: Tsitsernakaberd, Genocide Memorial Complex
place:
Yerevan
Erivan
Russian Empire
USSR
Republics of Armenia
description:
Tsitsernakaberd, Genocide Memorial Complex, Yerevan, Soviet Armenia, April 24, c. early 1970s; photographer unknown.
Crowds gather to pay tribute to victims of the 1915-1923 Genocide. Twelve cantilevered beams representing the twelve lost Armenian provinces in the Ottoman Empire (present-day Turkey) form a circle around an eternal flame. The 144.4 foot spire represents the rebuilt nation.
Construction of the complex was completed in 1968, inspired by demonstrations during the 1965 fiftieth Anniversary commemoration of the Genocide in Yerevan-quite an accomplishment during the Soviet era.
credit_line: courtesy of Oscan Ketchian and the Pan Sebastia Rehabilitation Union

title: Berj Zamkochian, organist
description:
Berj Zamkochian, organist, c. 1970; photographer unknown
Based in Boston, Berj participated with Rouben Gregorian in the first Armenian Night at the Pops in 1951, and regularly toured Europe and Asia. title: Holy Etchmiadzin Cathedral
place:
Etchmiadzin
Ejmiatsin
Vagharshapat
Russian Empire
USSR
Republic of Armenia
Etchmiadzin/Ejmiatsin/Vagharshapat/Russian Empire/USSR/Republic of Armenia
description:
Holy Etchmiadzin Cathedral. Etchmiadzin, Historic Armenia, Russian Empire, c. 1860.
The seat of the Armenian Apostolic Church and home of the Catholicos of All Armenians.
Salt paper print by photographer Gabriel Khatissian, of Tiflis, Georgia.
Parts of this pristine view do not exist today, namely the man-made Lake Nercessian (in the foreground), named after its creator, Catholicos Nerses of Ashtarak (1843-1857), and the undulating wall that surrounded and protected the monastery complex.
"Salt prints, the earliest positive prints, were made by contact printing from paper negatives or from collodion (wet plate) glass negatives. The print was made by sensitizing a sheet of paper in a solution of salt (sodium chloride) and coating the paper on one side with silver nitrate thus forming light sensitive silver chloride. When dry, the paper was placed beneath the negative under a sheet of glass in printing frame and exposed outdoors in sunlight with exposure determined by visual inspection. The exposed paper print was then fixed with "hypo" and washed and dried."
Excerpt from: Thomasian, Ruth. "Etchmiadzin Salt Print", The Journal, New England Journal of Photographic History, No. 146/147, 1995.
Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives, courtesy of a gift from the Photographic Historical Society of New England.

Nakhichevan 1918 Soldiers in General Antranig's army as Armenian independence is achieved; photo by Rouben Der Stepanian, MD, physician to General Antranig and his troops. Amateur photographer Dr. Der Stepanian, born in Kharpert, Historic Armenia, graduated from Tufts University Medical School, Medford, Massachusetts  in 1910.  He was among the first volunteers sailing from Boston in 1914 to join legendary General Antranig's Armenian Volunteer Corps of the Russian Army to defend Armenian people and their land in the Caucusus mountain area against the Turks.

title: Freedom Fighters
place:
Armenia
1st Republic of Armenia
USSR
2nd Republic of Armenia
Hayastan
description:
Freedom fighters, Zangezour, Armenian Republic, c.1920; photo by Rugen Der Stepanian, MD. Veterans of Antranig's Army and Tashnag political party members: Katchal Ghazar (Bald Lazarus); right, unidentified; middle: Sparabed (General) Karekin Njdeh, charismatic millitary hero and prominent intellectual. General Njdeh stressed the importance of a national historical memory, without which he said you cannot plan your path. He fled to Persia when the first republic collapsed, and from 1933 to 1934 he lived in the Boston area where he founded the Armenian Youth Federation. By the late 1930s he was in eastern Europe intervening to save thousands of Armenian civilians and soldiers from Nazi persecution. In 1945 Soviet troops arrested him in Bulgaria for his anti-Soviet activities and alleged collaboration with Nazi Germany. He was exiled to Siberia where he died in cpativity in 1955.
Courtesy of Rima Der Stepanian Aghayan, daughter of the photographer; documentation by Vahram Der Parseghian.

description: Sepastia, the ancient Armenian name; Sivas, the official name; Ottoman Empire, c. 1912.  Photograph by Encababian Brothers. “View of the clock and the environs of the ancient citadel, the site of the ancient town photographed from high in the minaret of the grande mosque.” (Translated from the French caption).  Sivas was an important trading center on the Baghdad Samsoun highway. The minority Armenian population had almost complete control of the import/export business, which was the basis for the economy of the area.

title: Choir of St. James Armenian Apostolic Church
description:
Choir of St. James Armenian Apostolic Church, Watertown, Massachusetts; choirmaster Deran S. Dinjian, seated right; two clergy pose with the choir: center front Bishop Mampre Kalfayan; center back Archbishop Karekin Hovsepian, 1937 on the occasion of the church consecration; photographer unknown.
In 1937, photo donor Elizabeth Boyajian was the youthful organist of St. James Church and was considered part of the choir, which at that time essentially served as the youth organization.  She said choir activities went beyond the call of Sunday worship to social events like picnics where members wore Armenian costumes and danced to Armenian music-she was the dance instructor!  When she petitioned the church to upgrade its pump organ to an electric Hammond Organ, the choir sponsored a dance at the elegant Longwood Towers in Brookline to raise the money.  The choir also sponsored recitals and performed a concert at the Boston Museum of Fine Arts.  Choir members met and married, and Elizabeth played at their weddings!
Courtesy of Elizabeth Boyajian Arakelian, third row, second from right

title: 50th year jubilee of Anatolia Girls' School; Marsovan, Turkey. 1914
place:

Sivas vilayet

description:
50th year jubilee of Anatolia Girls' School; Marsovan, Turkey. 1914
"Girls School Commencement, 1914. Jubilee celebration on the 50th anniversary of the opening of the Merzifon American School for Girls in 1864. Prof. V. H. Hagopian (near piano) was murdered on August 12, 1915, a day after he was deported from the campus. Miss P. Gureghian (seated five to the left of Hagopian), a long-time teacher at the Girls School would perish in the death marches along with many of the women, girls and audience members captured in this photo."
Background information about this photograph provided by Armen Tsolag Marsoobian, in "Dildilian Brothers' Memories of a Lost Armenian Home, Photography and the Story of an Armenian Family in Anatolia, 1888-1923"

title: Kesag's Church
place:
Constantinople
Istanbul
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Aleppo/Halab/Aleppo Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/French Mandate/Syria
description:
Kesag's Church, Gedik Pasha Church, A.A. Bedikian's father's church, Constantinople c. 1950s
The building was constructed over a number of years and was used as an orphanage, although the children were called students not orphans. Hrant Guzelian undertook the project with the help of A.A. Bedikian and donations from the Armenian Missionary Association. The whole community came together to contribute to the project. H.G. was involved with transporting the children from interior parts of Turkey to the Church and raising money for them. He and his wife could not have children of their own. His wife later divorced him, saying that he could "marry himself to his work."
Still called "Kesagi egeretzi" even though Kesag died after only a few years as minister

title: Saboundjian family, Aintab
place:

Gaziantep
Antep
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Saboundjian family, Aintab, c. 1910. Photographer unknown.
Three generations. Seated: Digeen (Mrs.) Hripsime Yacoubian Saboundjian and Baron (Mr.) Yeprem Saboundjian, their daughter Loucine, and Yeprem's mother Selver; standing, left to right: sons Roupen, Melkon, Krikor, and Hagop.
This Saboundjian family was spared the death march, detained by the Turks in order to make use of Yeprem's skills as a mechanic who maintained Aintab's water wheels that powered flour mills. Yeprem's three brohters' families, totaling 76, were sent to Der Zor in the Syrian desert. Only two survived. Yeprem and his family were able to escape to Aleppo in 1921.

title: Maestro Andranik Aroustamian, kemancheh player, composer and arranger
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
Maestro Andranik Aroustamian, kemancheh player, composer and arranger, 1982; photo by Studio Mario, Astoria, New York.
In 1918, Aroustamian was born in Russia into a musical family originally from Iran, where they returned in 1937 when the Soviet Union deported those without citizenship. Andranik continued his classical music training in Iran, and concertized. In 1946 he and other family members accepted Soviet Armenia's invitation to return to the homeland. As he tells it, it was 25 years of torturous living hounded by the KGB. In 1971 he took advantage of a new arrangement orchestrated by the United Nations that allowed Soviet citizens with immediate family into the west to emigrate. Andranik joined his brother in the United States and is credited with establishing the kemacheh as a classical instrument for the concert stage.

title: Hripsimé Nalbandian Toumas with her children
description: Hripsimé (maiden name unknown) Nalbandian Toumas, a Genocide survivor from Ismit, stands with her 3-year old son Norig. She holds her baby son Varoujan; Arab Punar, (modern-day Kobani), Syria, 1930; street photographer unknown. In 1919 Hripsimé had been sold by Kurds as a bride to Garabed Nalbandian’s family in Arab Punar after the Nalbandians had escaped from Ourfa to Syria. When Hripsimé’s husband died in 1929, she already had five children. She was immediately remarried to Garabed’s sister’s son, to ‘keep the family together.’ Norig is Varoujan’s half brother and first cousin once removed. Norig excelled in his studies and became a nuclear physicist in the United States.  Courtesy of Norig Asbed.

title: Unidentified Armenian family of Chemeshgezek
place:
Kharpert Vilayet
Harput Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Unidentified Armenian family; Chemeshgezek (Çemisgezek), Kharpert Province, Ottoman Empire, c. 1905; photo by Krikor Takvorian.
This three-generation family represents both traditional and worldly styles. The photographer positioned the traditional elders with their granddaughters in the middle, and framed them on the left with their young grandson, ready to lead in his three-piece western-styled business suit, and on the right with the fashionably European-attired children's mother and wife of the elders' absent son. The family's whereabouts today is unknown. Photographer Takvorian escaped Chemeshgezek during the Genocide in mid life, and lived and worked the rest of his life as a woolen-mill dyer in Lawrence, Massachusetts. He had brought this photo with him and later gave it to our photo donor's father with whom he worked in the mill.
Courtesy of Nubar Manoog Ashjian, Chemeshgezek Genocide Survivor
Written on back of photograph:
“Kirkor came to Lawrence, Ma maybe before WW1, did not take up photography. Worked in wood mill as dyer alongside Nubar’s father Manoog”

title: Akabi and Ohannes Altounian
description:
Akabi Kalenderian Altounian and her husband Ohannes, Amasia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1912; photographer unknown. Akabi was from Marsovan. Ohannes, a native of Amasia, was one of ten children. He and five of his brothers were killed as the Genocide began. The seventh brother Garabed, away at school, returned home to rescue his three sisters, sisters-in-law, and their children—but only he and two nieces survived. Akabi and her infant son Vahram died on the death march leaving her two-year old daughter Marie an orphan. No one knows how this photograph survived.
Courtesy of Maral Poladian Ayanian, Akabi and Ohannes' great granddaughter.

title: Orphans, Kokkinia, Greece, 1923.
description:
Sarkis Davidian, center, born in Adana, Cilician Armenia, 1916, poses with his two older buddies, at an orphanage in Kokkinia, Athens, Greece, 1923; photographer unknown.
The boys, ready to depart for a new life in Georgetown, Ontario, were among the first 40 boys, referred to as ‘The Georgetown Boys,” to be welcomed by Canada through the support of the Armenian Relief Association of Canada. The orphans were cared for until they were 15 years old, during which time they worked on local farms growing their own food. Later Sarkis became an US citizen and worked in the oriental rug business.
Courtesy of Margaret Davidian Bazarian, sister of Sarkis.

title: Armenian Night at the Pops, Symphony Hall, Boston, June 24, 1955
description:
Armenian Night at the Pops, Symphony Hall, Boston, June 24, 1955, Rouben Gregorian conducting, Agnes Dadekian, soprano soloist; photo by Drtad Boyajian.
Armenian Night at the Pops, an event with the Boston Pops Orchestra (BPO), was first started by Robert Hagopian in 1951, and since then has been sponsored by the Friends of the Armenian Cultural Society (FACS).  Over the past 55 years, the BPO, a subsection of the Boston Symphony Orchestra, has presented prominent artists—and promising new artists—of Armenian descent to an American and very appreciative Armenian audience. Courtesy of Barbara Boyajian Bousian, the photographer’s niece, who attended this Armenian Night at the Pops with her family, Hayward, California; and Zareh Gregorian, the conductor’s brother who provided information, Belmont, Massachusetts

title: Women of the Armenian Red Cross welcome General Antranig
description:
Women of the Armenian Red Cross (later known as the Armenian Relief Society) in official dress welcome General Antranig and his aides accompanied by WWI veterans, Baikar Building, Boston, 1919.
Antranig Ozanian, born in Shabin Karahisar, Anatolia, Ottoman Empire, had little formal education, but was a military genius and far-sighted leader and politician. He was a member of the Tashnag Armenian political party, but often disagreed with its Central Committee directives. In 1919  he left the new First Republic of Armenia as well as the Tashngas and came to the United States. He settled in Fresno, CA where he died in 1927.
Boghos Der Hovanessian and his brother Hovaness is peeking around the corner in the bottom left.

title: The Abrahamian and Boulboutian Families
place:
Ismid
Izmit
Nikomedia
Istanbul Vilayet
Ottoman Empire

description:
The Abrahamian and Boulboutian Families Ismid Railroad Station (Asian side of the Bosphorus) 1908
Yevkine Boulboutian Abrahamian (front, second from left) and her husband Roupen, behind her to the right) with their four daughters- three standing at left in light colored dresses and the fourth in the train's doorway- are traveling with other family members to their eldest daughter Mariam's wedding in Adapazar, 50 miles east, where the groom's family lived. Yevkine and Roupen's only son Garo, the photo donor, four years old at the time, was left at home. Roupen who owned a leather shoe making business, suffered a stroke in 1913 when the Turks seized his 17 tanneries and workshops, and died the following year. Forty member of the family were exiled in 1915; 12 were rescued, 11 settled in Marseille, France. The photo donor immigrated to the United States in 1923. Courtesy of Garo Willard Ray, Ne Abrahamian.

title: Chooljian family
place:
Husenig
Hussenig
Husenik
Ulukent
in Kharpert
description:
Chooljian family, Kharpet, Ottoman Empire, 1897,
Photograph by Soursourian
Top row, L-R:
1. Mrs. Sarah Nahigian Chooljian, maternal grandmother of the photo donor. Wife of #5
2. Anna Chooljian Mugar, daughter of #1 and #5. Mother of the photo donor.
3. Sarkis ("Jack") Nahigian
4. Esther Nahigian Gorgodian, daughter of #1 and #5
Middle row, L-R:
5. Hovhannes Chooljian, husband of #1. Maternal grandfather of the photo donor. He was a weaver of felt carpets, left for Watertown, MA before her family joined him 8 years later. His youbgest daughter, Victoria (Bessie) Nahigian Margosian, not pictured, was born in the US.
6. Nikolas Nahigian, father of #1. Administered orphanages in Kharpet for American Missionaries after 1897 massacre. He was one of the first Armenians to come to America. He lived and worked in Worcester, MA then returned to Hussenig.
7. Mrs Serpouhi Rustigian Nahigian. Wife of #7. She was raised by her mother in law, and married Nikolas at age 15.
Bottom row, L-R:
8. Mihran Chooljian (Marvin Julian), son of #1 and #5, settled in Boston and became an artist
9. Bedros Chooljian, son of #1 and #5, settled in New York City
10. Khourken Gorgodian, son of #4. Settled in Fresno, CA.
Esther's husband (#4) was in America and this photograph was made for him. She is wearing her traditional wedding dress. The men are wearing fezes, which were mandatory headwear. The photographer placed books in the hands of the children. The little boys wear dresses.
When Sara (#1) came to America, she accompanied her daughter Anna (#2), who was betrothed to Mardiros Der Megerditchian (Martin Mugar) who arrived several years earlier, and with his brother, Charles, had opened Mugar's Cafe on the corber of Mass Ave and Washington St., a combination restaurant, lunchroom and lodging house, a well known hostelry.
Nikolas (#6) and Serpouhi (#7) remained in Hussenig and were lost in the desert during the Genocidde in 1915.

title: Turabian Arabian Family, Alexandria Egypt
description:
Turabian Arabian Family Alexandria Egypt c 1911
In 1873 Sultan Abdul Aziz granted self government to Egypt. By 1882 the British had taken control and remianed for 72 years- till 1954.
Four generations reunite for a rare visit. Adrien Turabian Arabian, who was born in Alexandria, and her sons Aram (left) and Mitchell, all dressed in light colors. They are visiting from Providence, Rhode Island, where Adrine was married- her family arranged a marriage for her there- and the boys were born. Her grandmother (seated, name not known) and mother Turabian, both widows and wearing dark dresses, had lived in Alexandria since the days when Egypt was under the Sultan's control.
Courtesy of Adirenne Arabian Baksa Simidian, daughter of Aram.

description:
The Chalian Family, Adana, Cilician Armenia, c. 1905;
Photo by A. Kapamadjian .
Garabed Chalian (seated right), a well-known lawyer in Adana was influential with the French military, during their mandate, 1918-1919. He organized and led a group of Armenian freedom fighters to free the people of Marash from a siege by the Turks. He and his men never returned.
His wife Zarouhi and their children became refugees in Greece. She and her son (at left) settled in Soviet Armenia and her only daughter married and raised a family in Buenos Aires, Argentina.

title: The Omartian Family
place:
Chomakhlou
Comaxlu
Ankara Vilyaet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Omartian Family Four generation. Chomaklu, Caesarea, c. 1912; photo by S. Minasian of Everek, Caesarea.
The family were farmers and owned a grist mill. However, the photo donors' father Dertad (standing back left) was a teacher at the Chomaklu school. In 1915, he escaped just before the Turks entered the village and rounded up all the men. His father, Koutlushah (seated center) was one of them; his mother Areknazan Shelian Omartian (standing left holding her nephew), comfronted the Turks, insisting that they taker her also, saying "my place is beside my husband." They were both imprisoned and brutally tortured. Areknazan died in prison; Koutlushah was released a broken man and died soon after. Koutlushah's mother, widow Sara Menedian Omartian (seated right of center), holds another son's son, Vartan.
Dertad joined tow of his brothers in Springfield, MA- they were always homesick for Chomaklu, but ever grateful for their home in the United States. Their sister Araxi (standing back left) who was on the death march with other family members, survivied, married and lived in Jordan.
Courtesy of Dertad's daughters, Areknazan and Sara Omartian, named respectively after their grandmother and great-grandmother.

title: The Ashekian/Kazanjian Families
place:
Aleppo
Halab
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Syria
description:
The Ashekian/Kazanjian Families, Aleppo Syria, c.1915, shortly after they were deported from Mersin and Adana.
The patriarch Dikran Ashekian (seated front center) was an educated man and had been employed by the Ottoman government. The Ashekians traced their roots to Persia where one of their forebears who operated camel caravans between Persia and Turkey, eventually settled and married in Caesarea. By the 1880s two Ashekian brothers had gone south to Adana and established a flour mill, and before the turn of the century, they had convinced their younger brother Dikran to join them.
By the time of the Genocide, Dikran was head of the family, his brothers having died earlier of natural causes. He escaped to Aleppo with his wife, Mare Bakalian Ashekian (seated to his left), their daughters (back 3rd, 6th, and 7th from left), son (back 5th from left), their Kazanjian son-in-law (back left), and three grandchildren. Following the Genocide, some family members remianed in Aleppo, others resettled in Beirut, Baghdad, Mareseille, France, Scotland and Toronto.
Courtesy of Pakrad Bakalian, nephew of Marie
In 2010 due to the war in Syria, many Armenian Syrians moved to Armenia. The knitting/crotchet work of women grew into a vibrant industry - for ornaments, finger puppets sold around the world, and in the US at Crate & Barrel. Organizations like Sharan and Homeland Development Iniative Fund (HDIF) employ women, and often Syrian women to make the high quality wimsical pieces. https://www.forbes.com/sites/jackieabramian/2021/02/18/hdif-secures-sustainable-jobs-for-women-in-armenias-rural-communities/?sh=4fe7086624d7&fbclid=IwAR1HLgPSXwZIi9oOO0bgQGy_oGgFk2azwkI4-xtTAhvPtEvtSgrCNiCdecE

title: Grikor Suni Chorus, Boston
description:
Grikor Suni Chorus, Boston, 1924; photographer unknown.
Chorus members' names have not been identified except for the photo donor, Vahram Der Parseghian, 4th row 3rd from left.  The chorus master, Grikor Suni, recently arrived from Istanbul, sits second row center.  The chorus was probably out on a picnic 'social.'
Grikor Mirzaian Suni (1876-1939), composer, conductor, musicologist, and teacher, was a colleague of Komitas Vartabed (scholar priest), and a student of all the master teachers in Russian Armenia including those at Etchmiadzin Cathedral's Kevorkian Jemaran (Academy/Seminary).  In St. Petersburg, he studied with Rimsky-Korsakov.  Suni traveled widely in the Russian and Ottoman empires, collecting and arranging Armenian folk songs, and creating and conducting choruses.  In 1923 the Armenian Church of America brought him to the United States for the purpose of developing church choirs.  Soon he resumed organizing secular folk choruses for which he is well remembered in Boston, Chicago, Detroit, New York, and Philadelphia, where he resided.

description:
Three generations, Alexandretta (also known as Iskenderun), Cilician Armenia, c. 1908
Before 1915, the family had sent two sons and a daughter to Egypt, a third son to France, and the youngest son to New York. The photo donor's father Avedis (standing center), one of the sons sent to Egypt, married there and after WWII repatriated with his family to Soviet Armenia. His daughter, the photo donor and her family were able to leave Soviet Armenia in 1976 and resettled in the United States, thanks to her New York uncle who sponsored them.

title: Garabedian, Dingilian and Haigazian Families
description:
Three generations of the Garabedian, Dingilian and Haigazian Families, all with educational, religious, and business connections in Europe and America, Constantinople, 1910.
The Garabedians and Dingilans from Brusa (aka Bursa) and the Haigazians from Konia gathered in Constantinople to celebrate the wedding anniversary of the family matriarch and patriarch Anna Kaprielian Garabedian (seated left of center and Rev. Lazarus Garabedian, a stern Protestant minister, who had left Brusa after 27 years a minister of the Protestant church to preach at the American Bible House in Constantinople.
Honoring them are their five adult daughters, two sons in law, three sons- youngest wearing sailor suit (at left), and seven grandchildren. Daughter Matilda's (seated left) husband, Armenag Haigazian (standing second from left) earned his Bachelor of Divinity at Hartford Theological Seminary in 1896 and in 1898, his PhD in Philosphy at Yale University. From 1907 to 1921 he was president of Jenanyan Insitutue in Konia. In 1921, after helping his students, faculty, and family reach safety, he was arrested, put on the death march and died of typhoid fever alnong the way. In 1954 Matilda and Armenag's eldest daughter Mary (seated left on floor) and her husband Steve Mehagian joined Stephen Philibosian in founding Haigazian College (now univeristy) in Beirut in honor and in memory of Armenag.
Daughter Beatrice's (seated right with daughter Armine on lap) husband, Stepan Dingilian (standing right), a businessman was tortured and killed in 1914. Hovhannes Garabedian (left of Stepan) a graduate of Robert College was one of the youngest men rounded up on April 24, 1915. On the train taking the Armenians to certain death, Hovhannes was able to escape through the kindness of a Turkish trainman who told him when to jump off the train. He and other family members, including Anna and Lazarus, escaped to the United States.
Courtesy of Juliet Aroian Carpenter and her mother Armine Dingilian Aroian.

title: The Kurkjian family
place:
Kharpert Vilayet
Harput Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The Kurkjians, three generations: Kevork, center, his mother Hripsime Berberian Kurkjian, his wife Takouhie Chevorian Kurkjian and their seven children; Kharpert, Historic Armenia, c. 1912. Photographer: Soursourian Freres.
Kevork was a wedd-established silk businessman and furrier- note the silk fabrics worn by everyone and his mother's fur-trimmed coat. Kevork sent this photo to his brother Mesak Furrier (Anglicization of "Kurkjian") in Lynn, Massachusetts. Kevork and Takouhie's daughter Elmas, standing left, graduated as a teacher from Euphrates College, a five-minute walk from their upper Kharpert home. Kevork was killed in the Genocide. Elmas escaped with her 95-year-old grandmother Hripsime to the United States, and Takouhie, with a daughter and two sons, relocated to Sao Paulo, Brazil.
Courtesy of Colonel George Deranian, Elmas's son

title: Varter Bogigian Nazarian (Deranian)
place:
Aleppo
Halab
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
French Mandate
Syria
description:
Varter Bogigian Nazarian (Deranian)
(Deported's Varter)
Aleppo, French Mandate of Syria, 1919
Varter (Rose) was born in Hussenig, Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire in 1885. She lost her merchant husband Mugurditch Nazarian and their six children in the horrors of the Genocide. Only through her strong belief in Jesus did she survive the excruciating trauma of death and deportation. On the march through Ourfa, she met Rev. Jakob "Papa" Kunzler, who through his German connections, provided a way for her to escape to Aleppo, where this photograph was taken.  In 1920 she was able to go to America.
In 1925, now remarried and living in Worcester, MA, Varter wrote several messages in Armenian about this photo to her three-year old son, Hagop Martin Deranian:
This photograph is a reminder of my desparate, hopeless, and sad days. Let it be a living memorial of my days of death. But my life was saved through Christ. Preserve it [this photograph] my son, and always be guided by my hopes for you that you lead a God-Fearing and good life.
Varter Bogigian Nazarian Deranian died unexpectedly on May 4, 1929. She was buried on Mother's Day in Hope Cemetery, Worcester, MA. Her life is an example of the miracles of faith.
Courtesy of M. Martin Deranian, Varter's loving son.

Courtesy of Arthur Panosian, Victoria’s son.

title: United States Citizenship Naturalization Class
description:
United States Citizenship Naturalization Class, Lansing, Michigan, 1939; photographer unknown.
Lifelong learners Haroutune and Makrouhee Sahreyan Kassabian (at left facing teacher) emigrated from Gallipoli, Turkey, in 1931. When they became U.S. citizens, Haroutune was 85 and Makrouhee 73—the oldest new citizens at that time. Haroutune died the next year, and Makrouhee 9 years later, at peace with their new Armenian-American identity.
Courtesy of Dorothy Isgouhe Tarpinian, their granddaughter.

Dikranagerd/Diyarbakir/Amida/Diyarbakir Vilayet/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Dikranagert, Historic Armenia in the Ottoman Empire, 1911
The Malkasian Family of Dikranagert
Lacking information about how these family members are related, we can only guess that the patriarch is training the boy to fill his shoes--quite literally.  Most likely, the youngster's oversized shoes, gold timepiece, and center-front position were all bestowed upon him by the patriarch. With the absence of second-generation men, the patriarch placed all of his hope in his grandson. Symbolically he traded seating positions with him and gave him his shoes to wear.
Only three of those pictured here survived the Genocide: the young boy, Harutiun, the small girl on the right, Toontouch "Florence" Malkasian, and the young woman Arek, standing on the left. The rest all died at the hands of the Turks.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Antranig Tarzian

title: Rug merchants, Hamadan, Persia
description:
Kaloust Tekeyan, buyer of rugs, front, third from left, poses with local rug merchants, Hamadan, Persia, early 1920s; photographer unknown.  From Hamadan, an important rug weaving center for centuries, Kaloust served as the general manager of Iranian overseas operations for the A & M Karagheusian Rug Company of New York City. Other Armenians in the photo include Mardiros Simidian, front, 5th from left, and Kaloust’s father-in-law, Haroutune Buchakjian, front, 6th from left.  Kaloust and his wife came to the United States in 1963 to retire.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives
Courtesy of Haig Tekeyan, son of Kaloust

title: The Bogos Bogosian family composite
place:
Palou
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Bogos Bogosian Family,  family
Village of Nipshee, Palou, near Kharpert / Haleb, Syria / Providence, RI
Circa 1910, 1924, and unknown
Composite family photograph. Older photograph (circa 1910) taken in Village of Nipshee, Palou, near Kharpert. Two men in suits on left and potted plant were added later, covering other non-family members that were in the original photo. The potted plant was included to cover the hand of one of the people who were covered up. The man on the far left (1924, Syria) is the photo donor, as is the child on the far right. The man in the suit on the right (the photo donor's father, Sahag Bogosian) was photographed in Providence, RI.
At the time of the original photo, Sahag Bogosian was in the U.S. working in the Department of Sanitation in Rhode Island. The photo was sent to him as a remembrance of his family. Afterward, everyone in the original photo except for Serop and Sahag Bogosian was killed in the genocide. When Serop and Sahag were reunited years later in the U.S. through the International Red Cross, Serop had a photographer "drop in" a picture of his father as well as one of himself to reunite the family in the photograph.
Left to right
Front row (seated)
1. Bogos BOGOSIAN, ff (75 years old)
Middle row (children)
2. Lucy BOGOSIAN, s (6 years old)
3. Serop BOGOSIAN, self (5 years old)
Back row (adults and baby)
4. Serop BOGOSIAN, self (about 18 years old)
5. Sahag BOGOSIAN, f
6. Mariam BABOYAN BOGOSIAN, m
7. Anousheg BOGOSIAN, s (2 years old)
8. Katchkatoo DERDERIAN BOGOSIAN, fm (70 years old)
This photograph's story is discussed by our founder, Ruth Thomasian in this video, starting at time marker 2:02:
Courtesy of Serop Bogosian, the little boy/the man, Astoria, New York

title: Mirhan Guveyan sitting at his Singer sewing machine
description:
Mirhan Guveyan, the photo donor's grandather at his sewing machine. Mirhan's stepmother, Shankar Guveyan, sits on a divan knitting. Jamaica Plain, Boston MA, 1943
Per the photo donor:
"This photograph was taken on the 2nd floor, at 898 Parker Street, Jamaica Plain, in the little cubby hole that served as my grandfather's work area.  898 was the tenement house where my grandparents had settled when they moved to the Boston area from NJ after arriving through Ellis Island.  The man in the photo is my grandfather, Mirhan Guveyan, sitting at his treadle-operated Singer sewing machine.
Mirhan had lost his left arm in a train accident in the '30s and had soon after manufactured his own prosthesis to enable himself to hold and position his craft work at the machine. He was a master craftsman. He made the most beautifully ornate backgammon boards, leather wallets, purses, and slippers. He made all of his own tools: melted-down (then) lead toothpaste tubes into various size juice cans to hold and position his work on the sewing machine, sharpened used hacksaw blades into almost surgically shaped precision cutting knives to make his delicate celluloid stencils, made his backgammon boards out of discarded furniture pieces, etc. He was originally a bootmaker with his brother in Turkey/Armenia before immigrating to America, so he already possessed many of the skills needed for his new world. (He and his brother only survived the Turkish onslaught because they were excellent bootmakers for the Turkish officers.)"
The family is uncertain as to how he got the name Guveyan. There is some evidence that the original name was Kyureghian in Boston.

title: The framing of Saint Sarkis Armenian Apostolic Church
description:
The "framing" of Saint Sarkis Armenian Apostolic Church, Douglaston, New York, August 1989; photo by Bedros Zerdelian.
Saint Sarkis' traditional Armenian architecture with its three classic pyramidal domes was designed by architect Hovnan Giveleghian. The first service was held November 18, 1990—the acoustics are so good that microphones are unnecessary. The congregation celebrated the mortgage burning in March 2009.

title: Kharpert, 1915 - Armenians marched out of town
place:
Kharpert
Harput
Elazig
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The following information was provided by the photo donor and is in relation to the photo donor.
Caption from Spirit of Survival Exhibit reads:
GENOCIDE BEGINS
Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, 1915
Photographer unknown
Armenians are marched out of town under the guard of armed Turkish soldiers to prison in nearby mezireh. Knowing the Turks intended to kill them, the Armenians bribed the Kurdish prison guards to bring in kerosene and set fire to the prison, killing themselves, rather than die at the hands of the Turks.
Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives, courtesy of an anonymous donor
A longer version of the caption in the donor file indicates that the prisoners were being marched to prison in nearby Mezireh and includes the following quotation from the U.S. Consul to Kharpert (1914-1917), Leslie A. Davis, in his diplomatic report (published by Aristide D. Caratzas, New York, 1989 in The Slaughterhouse Province: An American Diplimat's Report on the Armenian Genocide, 1915-1917):
"There were a few isolated cases of resistance on the part of the Armenians....The most important instance of all was the burning of the prison, which occurred on the morning of august 5th [1915]. The Armenians who were confined there, knowing that they were almost certain to be killed anyway, set fire to it [the prison] in an attempt to escape. Some of them in the prison, and all the rest were shot in the courtyard by the guards. Not one succeeded in escaping."
Featured in Project SAVE's 1987 calendar "Celebrating People and Places"
credit_line: Courtesy of an Anonymous Photo Donor

title: Hampar, British intelligence officer in uniform
place:
Afion Karahisar
Afyon
Hudavendigar Vilayet
Bursa Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Ardashes Hamp, aka Hampartzoumian and Hampar, British Army intelligence officer serving in Scottish Seaforth Highlanders Regiment in Afion-Karahissar, Ottoman Empire, 1919.
When Ardashess Hampar went behind the Turkish lines to gather information for the British, he also scouted for Armenian waifs who roamed the streets like scavenging dogs. He took them out of Turkish territories and placed them with Armenians. He shortened his name to "Hamp" to avoid being recognized by the Turks as an Armenian.
Information provided by Jim Le Blanc of Gardner, MA (Scottish bagpiper):
Uniform of Seaforth Highlander regiment, headquartered in Inverness, disbanded after WWII, c.1960
Balmoral bonnet features emblem of elk's head with antlers, McKenzie tartan

title: Kevorkian Jemaran
place:
Etchmiadzin
Ejmiatsin
Vagharshapat
Russian Empire
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description: Kevorkian Jemaran (Accademy/Seminary) for boys; Sourp (Holy) Etchmiadzin, the See of the Armenian Apostolic Church to the right; Etchmiadzin, Historic Armenia, c. 1900. Kevorkian Jemaran first opened in 1874 to train teachers as well as religious leaders, and by the turn of the century, had become well known for its preparation of intellectual leaders. It was closed in 1917, and reopened in 1945, subject to Soviet regulations. In the present post-Soviet period, the Jemaran has been renovated and is being developed as a seminary.

title: The clinic in Nor Arapgir - K1999.001.755
Group of Tashnag men in Worcester
description:
Group of Tashnag men in Worcester, c. 1915.
Information provided by photo donor:
Bottom row, 3rd from left, is Mihrtad (Harry) Yagoobian, born in Mezre 1905- d. 1970, who immigrated to the US with his family in 1908.
Bottom row, 4th from left is Mihrtad's brother, Mihran Yagoobian (photo donor's grandfather) who was born in Mezre around 1980, d. 1946, Newton, MA. According to the photo donor: "The Yagoobians in Worcester were known to be Tashnags, although to what extent I don't know. Mihran's wife, conveyed to me, that Mihran was a devoted Tashnag because they helped him escape from Ottoman Turkey in 1906. He traveled by night, with the help of Tashnags and stayed in safe houses by day."

title: Kouyoumjian family
description:
Kouyoumjian family, c. 1905, Melrose, MA.
Information provided by photo donor:

title: Khatch-Khatoon Akmakjian Yagoobian, Kharpert
place:
Kharpert Vilayet
Harput Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Khatch-Khatoon Akmakjian Yagoobian, Kharpert, c. 1900, photo donor's great grandmother.
Information provided by photo donor:
She was born around 1860 and lived in Mezre, her husband was Hampartzoom Yagoobian and they had at least seven children. Her children started to emigrate from Mezre to Worcester, MA in the late 1880s. She died on board a ship somewhere from Ottoman Turkey to France in late 1907 or early 1908. Her husband and five of their children were also on the journey with her. They arrived in New York on Feb. 17, 1908 and settled in Worcester, MA. Mihran, one of her children, was the photo donor's grandfather.
This portrait was extracted and enlarged from a family photo. There were more than a few of these photos for family members made after she died.

title: Zouloumian Wedding
place:
Kharpert
Harput
Elazig
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Zouloumian wedding. Photograph taken in front of the groom's home, the Zouloumian family's home. St Stepanos neighborhood of Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, 1892
The bride, from cosmopolitan Constantinople, wears a European styled gown and veil. The groom, to the left of the bride, wears the "king" decoration on his fez (brimless hat). The bride wears the "queen" decoration at her hairline under the veil. The gunkahayr (godfather) stands to the right of the bride. As was the custom, the community came to greet the bride and groom. The photo donor is the two-year-old boy peeking through the slats of the balcony rail.
Subjects (The information below was provided by the photo donor and is in relation to the photo donor):
(left-most woman dressed all in white) Hripsime KARAJIAN HAMPARTZOUMIAN, mother
(left of bride, the groom) _____ ZOULOUMIAN
(the bride, in forground, in white) _____ ____ ZOULOUMIAN
(face just to the right and above the bride's) ____ ____ (priest who performed the wedding)
(man wearing a fez to the left of the priest) Mgrditch KARADJIAN
(two people to the right of the bride) Bagdasar HAMPARTZOUMIAN, father
(woman's head, at lower right) Anna _____
(child peeking through balcony slats) Ardashess HAMPAR, self
(two women in white on the balcony) Kohar and Hripsime KARADJIAN
(pictured, but location not indicated) Hagop FERMANIAN (best man)
(head pictured just between bride and groom's) ____ FERMANIAN (father of Hagop)
The bride was from Constantinople. Many of the people in the foreground resided in Boston at the time that the photo donor was interviewed (1979). The wedding ceremony was in the church, and the reception was in the home pictured with the balcony.

title: Kouyoumjian family
description:
Kouyoumjian family, c. 1905, Malden, MA.
Information provided by photo donor:
Left to right:
- Heghime Kouyoumjian, b. 1872 Kesirig- d. May 17, 1905 Fresno, CA. She emigrated with her oldest son, Krikor, standing, in 1897 and settled in Malden, MA. She contracted Tuberculosis (TB), and died in Fresno, CA in 1905. The family went there in hopes of prolonging her life.
- Standing: Krikor Kouyoumjian, b. May 1887 Kesirig- d. April 1906, Boston, MA, from TB. He was a very talented painter and had received a scholarship to study in France.
- Mary Kouyoumjian Yacobian, photo donor's grandmother, b. 1898 Malden- d. 1987 Weston, MA.
- Little boy: Ardashes Kouyoumjian (Comjean), b. 1901 Malden, MA- d. 1965, Newton, MA.
- Manoog Kouyoumjian, b. Kesirig 1865- d. 1918 Peabody, MA. He emigrated to Malden around 1890.

Family and friends celebrating the marriage of Dikran Samourkashian and Vartanoush Shapendarian, Amasia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1910, photographer unknown.
Photographer unknown, notes indicate may have been Dildikian. view of the Yeshil Irmak (Green River) valley in the background.
Attired in a white wedding outfit, the bride is concealed behind her veil although the wedding ceremony has already taken place. The groom, like most of the men in the photograph, wears European-style tailored clothing along with the Ottoman fez (brimless hat).

place:

Ottoman Empire
Turkey
Constantinople/Istanbul/Ottoman Empire/Turkey
description:
Gedik Pasha Armenian Congregational Church Constantinople, Ottoman Empire, Turkey. 1891
The congregation gathers in front of the camera to document the results of their all-night effort to frame their church building. Denied a building permit, church members constructed their humble church overnight, thus complying with a law, still on the books in Turkey, that says a structure built in one night does not require a permit. Years later this building was replaced by a stone edifice, still in use today.

Interview notes on file copy indicate that Setrak Timourian, Garabed Telfeyan, and Mgrditch Bashian owned a business together in Constantinople exporting Persian rugs to the U.S. The sons of Garabed and Nouritza Telfeyan were "members" of G.Telfeyen & Sons, Importers of Persian Rugs in N.Y.C. One grandson still [at time of accession] carries on the business in Manhattan. Notes also indicate the company came to Union City in 1878 and New York City in 1888. The following contact info is noted:
Telfeyan Carpets
Gary Telfeyan
953 Franklin Ave
Garden City, NY 11530
516-747-1505
305-776-0146
The second and third generations of this family immigrated to the United States, where they continued their oriental rug import business.
Courtesy of Marie Bashian Bedikian, Leonia, New Jersey"
The Telfeyans were in the oriental rug export business between Constantinople and New York City. Eventually, all of the second and third generation Telfeyans immigrated to the United States, where they continued in the rug business.

title: Cilician landscape
description:
Cilician landscape, Ottoman Empire, 1912
"The legendary hill community of Zeitun, Armenia, in a photograph taken in 1912. It's inhabitants were known for their fierce resistance to Turkish oppression and atrocity and their frequent acts of defiance became honored even in far off France and Russia. On hearing the 'Zeitoun March' as sung by Armenian warriors in the Caucausus, the Russian composer Ippolitov-Ivanov adapted it to symphonic genera but, unhappily, titled it 'March of the Sardar.'"

title: Returning home --Armenian deportees return to Marash
place:
Marash
Kahraman-Marash
Kermanig
Cilicia
Halab Vilayet
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Under the protection of the British mandate, Armenian deportees return to Marash from exile in the Syrian desert. 1919
Under the protection of the British and later the French, Armenian deportees return to Marash from Turkish-imposed exile in the Syrian desert expecting to rebuild their lives in peace and freedom -- a hope short-lived. In September 1919 the British deserted Marash, leaving their mandate to the French Army, which, in turn hastily withdrew protection in February 1920, forcing Armenians to either remain undefended against belligerent Turks or walk defenseless behind the retreating French in a blinding snow storm. Either way, few survived.
Photograph by Stanley E. Kerr, medical missionary

Abraham and Shushan Hartunian and thier family, Genocide survivors from Marash, Cilician Armenia, Ottoman Empire, pose in front of the camera on board the King Alexander, a Greek ship out of Athens before stepping into a new life.
The ship docked at a pier near Ellis Island on October 31, 1922.
Published in the Boston Globe on Wednesday, September 12, 1990, in a profile of Rev. Vartan Hartunian on the occasion of his return to Ellis Island.

title: Badarak (mass) offered in one of the refugee stations at Port Said
place:
Port Said
Ottoman Empire
British Protectorate
Egypt
description:
With the opening of the massacres in Armenia proper, the Armenians of the community of Musa Dagh then in the northeastern Syrian province of the Ottoman Empire, chose to resist rather than allow themselves to be decimated by the Turks. After approximately six weeks of heroic self-defense, the embattled mountaineers boarded on French and British vessels operating in the Mediterranean and the appox. 5,000 Armenian emigre contingent was taken to a refugee camp at Port Said, Egypt where subsequently some returned with disastrous results to their homes after the war, others settled in various Middle Eastern countries, France and the United States. A priest of the Armenian Apostolic church offers Badarak (Mass) at the tent city at Port Said.
Courtesy of the Armenian Revolutionary Federation Archives

title: Abrahamian Family: Two Generations
place:
New Julfa
Nor Jughay
Jolfa
Isfahan
Esfahan
Persia
Iran
description:
Abrahamian Family, New Julfa, Persia, 1894
Two generations, household servants and memorable photographs.
New Julfa is a community of Ispahan established for Armenian artisans by Shah Abbas I in the early 17th century. The Abrahamians were textile merchants who did busienss in England and India.
The two Abrahamian brothers are seated in the middle row with their unmarried sister between them and their wives on either side. The children are all cousins. Aram Abrahamian sitting on floor, second from left, the photo donor's father.
The entire group pictured lived in the same house built around a courtyard, with private quarters for each family and a shared kitchen and sitting room. In the summer they lived in the shady side of the house, and in the winter they lived on the sunny side. The photographs in the front, framed between teo vases of flowers, are probably deceased relatives.
Several things distinguish these Armenian Iranians from their contemporaries living in the Ottoman Empire. In Persia, Armenian men did not wear hats, while in the Ottoman Empire Armneian men, like all men, were required to wear the fez. Armenian Persian women tended to wear dresses lighter in color then Armenian women to the north and west. Their head pieces were different also. Married women wore a white lace-trimmed veil over a flat base; in some places in the Ottoman Empire Armenian married women wore the sharvash, a large dark shawl for the head; in other localities they wore the Turkish yasma, a yard square cotton cloth scarf wrapped around the head and often up over the chin and mouth.
Courtesy of Meda Abrahamian Sirakian and Peggy Hovanessian

title: Preparing mahs
description:
Making mahs for St. James Armenian Apostolic church in Watertown, MA c 1960s photo by Nigoghos Knaian.
The Armenian word “mahs” or “pajhin’ means a portion or a share of the Badarak.  Mahs is a thin flat bread, blessed by the Priest, then distributed by members of the parish council at the end of Badarak as the congregation is leaving.
The holy “mahs” is usually taken home, given to someone who could not attend the Badarak, someone who may be sick or elderly.
Courtesy of Armenouhi Ouzounian Knaian

title: Near East Relief Orphanage
description:
Near East Relief Orphanage
Corinth Greece 1923
Photo by NER Photographer
Armenian children, most under the age of 12, in formation of a telling New Testament Bible verse: We do not want you to be uninformed, brothers and sisters, about the troubles we experienced in the province of Asia. We were under great pressure, far beyond our ability to endure, so that we despaired of life itself.  Indeed, we felt we had received the sentence of death. But this happened that we might not rely on ourselves but on God, who raises the dead.  He has delivered us from such a deadly peril, and he will deliver us again. On him we have set our hope that he will continue to deliver us,  as you help us by your prayers. Then many will give thanks on our behalf for the gracious favor granted us in answer to the prayers of many. Second Corinthians 1: 8-11
Courtesy of Araxie Bodourian Zenian
Featured in Project SAVE's 1989 calendar "Armenians: A Look Back"
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Araxie Bodourian Zenian

title: Genocide Survivors and Victims
place:
Ourfa
Urfa
Yetesia
Sanliurfa
Edessa
Aleppo Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Survivors of the Genocide hold a burial service for the Ourfa Armenian victims whose bones, found strewn around the monastery yard, are a testimony to their cruel fate. At the top, the words of Armenian poet Krikor Zohrab in translation say: "We are gone now, but the nation has 400,000 orphans. Save the."
Photograph by Fr. Rafael, Catholic Priest
Sign says "THE BASES OF CIVILIZATION IN THE NEAR EAST ARMENIAN CRANES[?] OURFA 1914-1919"
Standing third from the left is Rev. Ephraim K. Jernazian, who in his book Judgment Unto Truth, describes this event, which he helped organize. He also relates his childhood experience in the massacre of 1895. His words describing "the Turks shooting at the beast on the moon" where the inspiration for Richard Kalinoski's play title originates.
Courtesy of Garbis Kazanjian and Alice Jernazian Haig,  daughter of Rev. Ephraim K. Jernazian.

title: Anait Madoyan and Nicoghos Davidian
place:
Soviet Armenia
USSR
Republic of Armenia
description:
Anait Madoyan of France, front, and Nicoghos Davidian of Belgium lug buckets of cement in their volunteer rebuilding efforts, following the December 1988 earthquake. Spitak, Soviet Armenia, c. 1989; photo by R. Atayan, Armenpress.
The entire city of Spitak was destroyed and rebuilt at a location next to the original town with much assistance from foreign volunteers-one of the first places where the Soviet Union allowed workers from other countries to take part in rescue and relief efforts. Many of the dwellings in the new Spitak mirror the architecture of the countries that donated or built the homes.
credit_line: Project SAVE Photograph Archive, Courtesy of Aram Ohanian

ARMENIAN AMERICAN SPONSORED REFUGEE CAMP
Aleppo, Syria, 1920s
Photograph by Vartan Derounian
The original image was a postcard used to raise funds for the camp. To support themselves, these refugees, mostly women and children, from the vicinity of Kharpert, made needlework to sell.
Courtesy of Margaret Kedonian Melickian.

description:
Mushger, Village of Arapgir, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire
Armenian Apostolic Church School, 1915.
Armenian Apostolic Church School students (most of the children in the village) and their teacher. Only four of these students, including the photo donor, survived the Genocide; the teacher was taken away by Turkish soldiers and never seen again.
Identified people:
John (Zaven) MIRAKIAN, donor [back row, seventh in from left, holding bible]  (genocide survivor)
Parentsem AVEDIAN (CHILINGERIAN) [fourth row, first person, marked with an x]  (genocide survivor)
Hovsep MIRAKIAN, f bro son [back row, fifth from left]  (genocide survival unknown)
Mary MONSOURIAN (genocide survivor)
Avedis MIRAKIAN, f bro son [front row, fourth from right]  (genocide survival unknown)
Photo donor stated only four named students in the picture survived the genocide. The original photograph, of which this is a copy, was given to him by an aunt, who rescued him in the genocide.

title: On the Steps of the United States Capitol
description:
On the steps of the United States Capitol Building
Washington, D.C., April 14, 1920
Photograph by S. Arakelyan
Armenian-American veterans posed on the southwest side of the Capitol Building. They were financed by the women of the Armenian Red Cross, now the Armenian Relief Society, to petition Washington to diplomatically recognize the Republic of Armenia. Moses Nahigian was Commander of the Boston Veterans. Mr. Nahigian is pictured. The veterans gathered at South Station in Boston, MA, and took a train to Washington, going directly to the steps of the Capitol. The then senator of Massachusetts, Henry Cabot Lodge, Sr., spoke to the assembled veterans, saying "Our battleships cannot reach Mt. Ararat."
Armenian American World War I veterans lobbied for United States diplomatic recognition of the Republic of Armenia. On April 23, 1920, the United States extended de facto recognition to the Republic of Armenia on April 23, 1920 and granted the new nation $6,000,000 in aid.
Seal in English at bottom right reads: "Boston Branch, A.A.W.W. Veterans" (also in Armenian)
Inscription at bottom reads: "Armenian-American veterans at Washington, April 14, 1920" (also in Armenian)
Photo signed by photographer "[copyright symbol] 1920, S. Arakelyan, Cambridge, Mass."

description:
Sourp Stephanos (St. Stephan) Armenian Apostolic Church
Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, before 1915
Armenian life in Kharpert centered around church activities and school classes from morning through evening every day of the week. Christian Armenians (note their church, center front with priests on the adjacent roof) and Moslem Turks (note the dome of their mosque, upper left) lived here as neighbors as they did in many parts of the Ottoman Empire.

Rally in support of the Artsakh movement, Lenin Square, Yerevan, Armenia (the Soviet Union had fallen, but Armenia was not yet the Republic of Armenia), July 1, 1990; photo by Hovannes Armenakian.  Outside the Council of Ministers building, Azat Haik (Free Armenia) organization lobbies for Artsakh independence.  Banner at lower left translates, "Open all roads leading to Karabagh." title: Open-air fruit stand
description:
This information was provided by the photo donor and is in relation to him/herself:
Open-air Fruit Stand
776 Main Street, Worcester, MA
1926
Photographer: K. S. Melikian
Fruit stand owned by Farsakian brothers. The house, formerly the Tomajian house, had a night watchman, and the fruit was stored on the porch. The brothers owned another fruit stand on Main Street, further downtown. Garabed died young, and Takvor moved to Detroit and set up another fruit stand. The man on the left, Sarkis Adjeleian, was a worker from Everek and brother-in-law of Macrouhi Adjeleian.
L-R
1. Sarkis ADJELIAN, worker
2. Irene BILZERIAN (BABIGIAN), worker
3.
4.
5. Garabed FARSAKIAN, m bro, proprietor
6. Seth BILZERIAN, worker/distant cousin of #2
7. Takver FARSAKIAN, m bro, proprietor
Caption from Spirit of Survival Exhibit:
OPEN-AIR FRUIT STAND
776 Main Street, near Piedmont, Worcester, Massachusetts, 1926
Photograph by K. S. Melikian from a glass-plate negative
Brothers Grarbed (seated right of center) and Takvor (standing right) Farsakian later opened a fruit store on Main Street. Takvor continued in the fruit business after moving to Detroit.
Courtesy of Alice Gulanian Papazian, Worcester, Massachusetts

title: Armenian American Wedding
description:
This information was provided by the photo donor and is in relation to him/herself:
Armenian American Wedding
June 10, 1951
Church of Our Saviour, Worcester, MA
Photographer: George Cocaine
Wedding of Harry and Aliz Demirjian Hoogasian. One of the last weddings to take place at this church. Photograph featured in Spirit of Survival Exhibit and A Look at Ourselves (1984). No record of dimensions in paper file. Subjects of photograph identified by Alice Demirjian Hoogasian during interview in October, 1980.
People (L-R)
Front row (standing):
1. Richard CASPARIAN, cousin of groom (5)
2. Charles HOOGASIAN, bro of groom (5)
3. Edward HOOGASIAN, bro of groom (5), husband of 9
4. Hagop Martin DERANIAN, friend of groom (5) & best man
5. Harry HOOGASIAN, groom
6. Alice DEMIRJIAN HOOGASIAN, bride
7. Ann DEMIRJIAN, sis of bride (6)
8. Alice JEANKERR (REESE), flower girl
9. Sylvia BADRIGIAN HOGASIAN, wife of 3
10. Christine PARSEKIAN (SARAYDARIAN)
11. Arpine VARJABEDIAN (GUVEYAN)
Back row (standing)
12. Reverend Agop MEKJIAN
ARMENIAN AMERICAN WEDDING
Church of Our Saviour, Worcester, Massachusetts, 1951
Photograph by George Cocaine
Harry and Aliz Demirjian Hoogasian were married in the Church of Our Savior. They pose with their large, typically American wedding party after the priest performed the ceremony in Armenian. The bema, a platform in front of the raised altar, is characteristic of Armenian Apostolic Church design.
Courtesy of Marian Parsekian, and Aliz Demirjian Hoogasian

title: Unknown Armenian officer, possibly a doctor, serving in the Turkish Army
place:

Garin
Karin
Erzurum Vilayet
Russian Empire
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Unknown Armenian officer, possibly a doctor, serving in the Turkish Army, Erzerum, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1910;p photo by V.H. Voskeritchian Freres.
From 1909, when their military service was finally permitted, Armenian citizens of the Ottoman Empire served with honor and apprehension--proud to serve yet fearful of repeated ethnic violence directed at them. Serving in Erzerum near the Russian border would have been a dangerous assignment for an Armenian, as the Ottoman government considered them to be allied with the enemy, Russia.

title: Celebration outside St. Vartans Cathedral - K2010.010.049
The Vosbikian Continental Band of Philadelphia
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
The Vosbikian Continental Band of Philadelphia playing in New York City or somewhere in New England, late 1950s; photographer unknown.
Front l-r: Steve Ajdaharian, piano; Albert Santerian and Joe Vosbikian, dumbeg; Sam Vosbikian, oud. Back l-r: Mike Vosbikian, saxophone; Jirair Hovnanian, vocal; and Jim Vosbikian, clarinet and vocal.
Philadelphia’s Vosbikian Band was very popular throughout the Armenian communities of the United States, especially on the East Coast, starting in the ‘Big Band’ era of the 1940s.  They developed an American-Armenian style by combining traditional Armenian folk/urban sounds and rhythms with those of American jazz and popular music—music to dance to. They played mostly western Armenian music, but also some Arabic, Jewish, and Turkish, thus the ‘Continental’ in their name.  They played for other ethnic communities—lots of Assyrian weddings—and also for VIPs.  When the US State Department was honoring the Shah of Iran, there was no Persian band available, so the Vosbikians were invited to entertain the Shah and his entourage at New York’s Waldorf Astoria Hotel.
credit_line: Courtesy of Eleanor Der Parseghian Caroglanian

"Stakhanovian" refers to Stakhanovite movement – a campaign intended to increase worker productivity and to demonstrate the superiority of the socialist economic system. Named after Alexey Grigoryevich Stakhanov a Soviet and Russian miner, who set a productivity record in 1935 during his mining shift. Stakhanov's records set an example throughout the country and gave birth to the Stakhanovite movement, where workers who exceeded production targets could become "Stakhanovites".

title: Jerry Murad's Harmonicats
description:
Jerry Murad's Harmonicats, a mouth-organ trio; left to right: Al Fiore, Jerry Murad, and Don Les, Chicago, IL, c. 1946; photographer unknown.
Jerry Mouradian, who was born in Istanbul, organized the group in 1944 when he was a Marine stationed in New York City during WWII. Al was his high schol buddy from Chicago who pleayed a 30-inch harmonica when Jerry met him in New York. Their big hit, "Peg O' My Heart," put them on the charts in the late 40s.

title: The Surabian sister playing the Deagen marimba
description:
The Surabian sisters left to right, Julia, Lillian, and Anna playing the Deagen marimba, Hartford, CT, c. 1936 during high school years; photographer unknown.
The sisters had a unique style of all three playing at the same time on the same instrument, with two and sometimes three mallets in each hand. Anna, the eldest, who arranged the music, credits their father Nishan with the musical genes of the family. He played a four-holes Armenian flute.
The sisters played at local Armenian events and during WWII did a gig along with Arlene Frances and others at a War Bond Rally fundraiser in New York City. After two of the sisters married and started families, they didn't have the time to perform together.
credit_line: Courtesy of George and Martha Boyajian Yaghjian, and Anna Surabian Malootian

title: The Ajemian sisters
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
The Ajemian sisters, mid 1940s; photographer unknown.
Based in New York City, both sisters graduated from the Juilliard School of Music- Maro was still a student in 1942 when she introduced the now-famous Khatchaturian Piano Concerto to Americans. Anahid was a 1946 winner of the prestigious Naumburh Foundation Competition Award. By 1948 the sisters were already renowned for their masterful interpretations of the classics as well as the music of new American composers. At the same time, they promoted Armenian music and musicians through their Friends of Armenian Music organization and are credited with helping to launch the career of composer Alan Hovhaness. Both Anahid and Maro recorded for RCA Victor.

description:
Euphrates College Orchestra (Yeprad College Nvakakhoomp), Kharpert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, 1908; photographer unknown
Although setting numbers on the face of the original photograph is not acceptable practice today, numbering has made it possible to connect names with faces- all but five (6, 9, 17, 19 and 20) are matched with names on the reverse side of the original photograph.
The conductor, #14 at right, is Edward F. Carey, a preacher with the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions, and a teacher of violin at Yepard College. The photo donor's father, Bedros Terzian, holds his violin in the second row, 3rd from left.

description: Jerusalem, main entrance to the Cathedral of St. James, 1934. Pilgrims, clergy, and seminarians participate in the Cutting of the Hair Ceremony, the culmination of the promise childless couples had made, that, if God blessed them with a baby, they would let the child's hair grow for seven years. Then, upon fulfillment of their vow, they made a pilgrimage to a shrine or a monastery for the ceremonial hair cutting.

title: Akabe Kehyaian Saryian, c. 1920s
place:
New York City
New York
USA
description:
Akabe Kehyaian Saryian, c. 1930s. New York City, NY
Akabe Kehyaian Saryian was born 1910 in Kayseri, Turkey.
Photo donor's research on his grandmother states:
"The story begins in 1913 when my grandmother was 3 years old and my great-grandfather, Boghos "Paul" Kehyaian, who was 31 at the time, travelled to the U.S. from Turkey [where he had a brother who sent for him]. By the time my grandmother was 6 or 7, her mother [Rose] had died [in 1916] and she became homeless - forced to live on the streets of her hometown in Kayseri, Turkey. She survived on scraps of discarded food and the carcasses of dead animals. She also recalls singing and dancing on the streets for coins people with give her. She was eventually found and placed in an orphanage when she was about 6-7 years old."
From the orphanage, Bogazliyan Orphanage, she traveled to the United States in 1923 with the help of her father in the US, on the ship "Madonna", where she reunited with her father at Ellis Island.
Full interview here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=seToZA0qgF4

title: Madame Rose Zulalian
description:
Madame Rose Zulalian, dramatic contralto soloist at a Hampartzoum Berberian concert, Boston, probably at John Hancock Hall, c. early 1950s; photo by Marty Hartutunian.
The photo donor dondly recalls singing in "Mr. Berberian's concerts...we practiced at St. James [Armenian Apostolic Church] Hall in Watertown," and she remembers Rose Zulalian as a "Very warm people-person who mixed with the youth and always encouraged us. She even wanted all the lights on when she sang so that she could 'see her people.'" But Rose as also popular in the American community, especially for her full-throated rendition of the "Star Spangled Banner." Adrina continued, "By the way we heard that Madame's dress, all gold threads, weighed 50 pounds!"

title: The Kef Jees at St. Vasilios Greek Orthodox Church
description:
The Kef Jees at St. Vasilios Greek Orthodox Church, Peabody, Massachusetts, February 3, 1967; photo by Heather Sterni.
High School senior Al Shavarsh Bardezbanian, oud player seated left, started the band with his high-school buddies in 1964 only two months after he took up the oud.  Seated at right is Skip Sampsonas, accordion; standing are Richard Aram Yagjian on the dumbeg and Nick Mandagouras, clarinet.The Kef Jees played at many venues, both Armenian and Greek.  Their very first gig was at the AYF (Armenian Youth Federation) Olympics in Lynn, Massachusetts. The next year they played at the AYF Olympics in Hartford, Connecticut.  The Kef Jees came to an end when Al went off to college.  Al later became an oud virtuoso and played with his Al Shavarsh Bardezbanian Ensemble until his untimely death in 2006.

title: Family of Garabed and Hripsime Deriklian, Dikranagert
place:

Diyarbakir
Amida
Diyarbakir Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
The family of Garabed and Hripsime Deriklian, Dikranagert, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, circa 1912.
This information was provided by the donor:
Garabed Deriklian was a cabinet maker. Donor never saw this family after 1915, and assumed they had all perished in the Genocide.
However, when this photo was exhibited in Paris, a woman named Haygouhi Deriklian Tfenkidjian saw it and recognized the youngest child as her own father (she had the same photograph), and said that her father, his mother, and her father's sisters survived. She was not familiar with Tarzian, the donor, and wondered if they were related, but he unfortunately was deceased by then, and his daughter didn't know anything about their connection to the Deriklian family.
Haighouhi was able to provide the following information:
Left to right:
1.
2. Garabed Deriklian, cabinet maker
3. Lousine Deriklian
4. Manoushag Deriklian
5. Hripsime ________ Deriklian, wife of #2
6. Yervant Deriklian

description:
Statue of Liberty souvenir postcard photo of Makrouhi and Mardiros Taraian, c. 1928; photo by Pete Peterson, who had a contract with the U.S. Army to operate the photo concession at Liberty Island in New York harbor.
Peterson made composite tourist photos using two negatives--one a reusable background image of the statue which was laid over the other of the tourists standing on the pier with their backs to the Brooklyn Bridge. He instantly developed and printed with the box of his postcard camera.
Makrouhi was born in Smyrna (present day Izmir), Mardiros in Palou, Ottoman Empire.
Credit Line: Project SAVE Armenian Photograph Archives

title: Singer Sewing Machine salesman Stepan Dingilian
place:
Ankara
Angora
Ankara Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description:
Singer Sewing Machine salesman Stepan Dingilian commissioned this photo of himself and his client in 1899 in the town of Sivri-Hissar near Angora (Ankora), Ottoman Empire.  He appears to be demonstrating the quality of the stitching. Note the crank wheel and below it, on the ground at the left, the carrying-case cover, clearly marked with the Singer logo.  Our salesman wrote a greeting on the back of the photo to “my loyal friend, Dikran Effendi Tobatian, wishing him “a Happy New Year on the threshold of the 20th century.” Even then Singer sewing machines were referred to as domestic, and not industrial technology, and were marketed to women in North America and Europe for making their family’s clothing and also earning money doing piece work. Interestingly, this photo in the Ottoman Empire, a relatively new market for Singer, does not include women.
Couresy of Armine Dingilian Aroian

title: Artist Koren Der Harootian
description: As a young man, Koren Der Harootian (1907-1992), a natural-born artist, was encouraged and supported by his mother and sisters. They had all fled Kharpert, Historic Armenia, during the Genocide after their husband/father was tortured and murdered. In 1921 they settled in Worcester, Massachusetts, and Koren attended Worcester Art Museum School, where after a short time they told him he was such a natural they could teach him no more. During summers he went to live among artists in Provincetown on Cape Cod. Later in life, Koren was well known internationally as a sculptor. Here we see Koren in 1929.  Photographer K. S. Melikian, with his artist’s eye, seated him in a stylish chair ready to put finishing flourishes on his watercolors—sailboats, sand dune, and Cape Cod houses. His sister Marion, who opened Marion’s Beauty Salon the following year, was his hair stylist and costumer.

title: Wedding of Rosa Messomian and Kegham Kaboolian.
place:
Agn
Egin
Kemaliye
Kharpert Vilayet
Mamuretulaziz Vilayet
Ottoman Empire
Turkey
description: Hars (bride) Rosa Messomian Kaboolian and pessa (groom) Kegham ‘John ‘ Kaboolian; Agn, Ottoman Empire, 1910; photographer unknown. Rosa, from Arabkir, and Kegham, from Agn, barely knew each other when they married. In 1912, after their daughter Arpen was born, Kegham left to find work in the United States. Rosa and Arpen lived with Kegham’s widowed mother until she died in 1915. Then a Turkish family hid Rosa and Arpen during the Genocide, after which mother and daughter journeyed six years through many countries before finally reaching Kegham in the United States. Courtesy of Arpen Kaboolian Abrahamian, daughter and Genocide Survivor

title: Gevolanian family in Siberia
description:
Siberia, Soviet Union, April 1955; photo by kind Russian neighbor; Gevolanian family in front of their one-room, thatched-roof home.
Soon after arriving in Soviet Armenia in 1949, the Gevolanians were exiled to Siberia due to Bedros' Tashnag membership in Aleppo. Medz Mayrig Zabel is seated in front next to son Bedros. Her granddaughter, Zabel (our photo donor), stands in back at very left. After Stalin's death in 1955, the family was allowed back to Armenia and this photograph was taken before they returned, but they had lost everything.

title: The Balancing Act at Marmnamarzagan Agoump
description: September 1913, Alexandria, Egypt, athletes of the Marmnamarzagan Agoomp (transliteration of Armenian words on back fence meaning Gymnastic Club). Posed for the picture with suited gentlemen-judges or teachers looking on, are uniformed gymnasts. Four young men with strong backs, shoulders, and arms balance on the parallel bars: at the ends of the bar, two do handstands while one in the middle does a backbend with another standing on his arched abdomen waving flags to create a human tower. Two athletes stand on the ground with team mates balanced on their shoulders. Four others look on, perhaps waiting for their turn to participate.

title: Dr. Hagop "Jacob" Hekimian
description:
Dr. Hagop "Jacob" Hekimian, a resident in general surgery, posed in the operating room at Memorial Hospital, Pawtucket, Rhode Island, August 26, 1917. Dr. Hekimian's name indicates his profession. In Turkish, hekim means “doctor.” Back in Aintab, his father was the beloved Hekim Hagop jan, the honorable doctor who ministered to all in the community regardless of ethnicity or ability to pay. His life-saving medical services were so valued that Turkish officials protected him and his family during the Genocide. His son Hagop, Jr., after graduating from Aintab College, decided to follow his father’s profession elsewhere. In 1910 he arrived in the United States and began his medical education in Boston. After his residency in Rhode Island, he practiced medicine in New Jersey.
The unidentified registered nurse anesthetist at his side is a graduate of Memorial Hospital's School of Nurse Anesthesia, as indicated by the style of her cap.

Professional photographs of Soviet Armenia in the 1970s. Many are propaganda photographs. Locations include: Yerevan, Vasburagan Observatory, Lake Sevan, and Matenadaran (book repository) in Yerevan. The photos include portraits of famous people and documentation of cultural events, science, and industry. These professional photographs were taken as part of a Soviet propaganda campaign to portray a hard working, harmonious, and technologically advanced society. The photographers are not identified. Many of the photos also appear in K1996.001.
These photos were gifted to the photo donor Avak Sarafian's grandparents during a visit to Soviet Armenia during the 1980s.

title: Fresno, California, Armenian Red Cross float
description: The women of the Fresno, California, Armenian Red Cross chapter sit proudly with their daughters and a sewing machine on their float c. 1916-1918. The crepe paper Liberty Bell, streamers, and the starry striped bunting on the truck’s hood attest to their patriotism. The truck, of local manufacture, was probably used for farm work or to haul logs when not showing off in parades. The Armenian Red Cross, founded in 1910, was a women’s philanthropic organization whose first mission was to aid Armenian Genocide survivors. Today it is known as the Armenian Relief Society (ARS). Some members were trained as nurses and went to Armenia during WWI as field-hospital workers. Others were auxiliary members with no medical training. On this special occasion, they wear the standard white Red Cross uniform. Courtesy of Suren Saroyan, and his agent Walter Karabian.

title: The Babaian Family
description:
The Babaian Family, originally from Bitlis, Historic Armenia, Ottoman Empire, immigrated to the United States, c. 1908. Here they pose in Varna, Bulgaria, seaport and resort on the Black Sea, 1913; photographer unknown.
Vaharshag Babaian, standing, whose maternal grandfather was killed in the 1895 massacres, was employed as a buyer for the Greek-owned Kazan Rug Corp. in New York City, where he lived with his growing family. His work took him to Bulgaria to oversee the corporation’s manufacture of rugs and carpets. Accompanying him were his widowed mother Shagro, seated right, his wife Satenig Krikorian, and their son John, born in New York City, 1910.
In 1914, Shagro returned to New York to live with another son, while Vaharshag and Satenig traveled back to Bitlis, their hometown, with John and his baby sister (absent from photo because she was crying). Too soon the Genocide began. Vaharshag was killed and Satenig taken away. Satenig’s parents, then in the United States, later found the children in an Aleppo orphanage and brought them back to the United States.
Courtesy of John Suren Babaian (child in photo)

description:
Maranjian cousins Nerses and Arakel, standing, possibly with other relatives, town of Ulash, Sepastia, Ottoman Empire, c. 1913; photographer unknown.
Their attire is a mix of Ottoman and European styles: the fez (red brimless flat-topped cone hat worn by men in the Ottoman Empire between 1826-1925) wound with a turban for a more village look; generous mustaches and expressive hands—one holding a cigarette; and tailored vests and lapelled jackets with watch fob chains giving a touch of sophistication.
Courtesy of Berjouhie Betty Maranjian Nersessian, Nerses’ daughter

title: Vahan Totovents in army uniform
description:
Writer Vahan Totovents as a soldier in September 1915. On the back of this postcard he has written in Armenian: " George--I'll be leaving for the frontline in two days. The country is in a devastating state. [illegible….]the mother and the others[illegible….]if we find them alive[illegible….]in the photo [illegible….] as a memory. "
Totovents served on the Caucasian front during World War I and served as bodyguard, translator and secretary to General Andranik Ozanian. After the war he lived in Yerevan and was widely acclaimed as a writer, equally facile in prose, poetry, translations, drama, and criticism. In 1937 he fell prey to Stalinism and was exiled to Siberia.

title: Ohanes Topalian in United States Army
description:
United States Army, probably Portland, Maine, circa 1898. Ohanes Topalian, far right, seated, had recently arrived from Gurun, Historic Armenia, volunteered for U.S. military service, and was immediately granted U.S. Citizenship. Private Topalian served during the Spanish-American War in Portland, Maine and in Cuba from January to May 1899. After his military service, he was employed as a machinist at the Boston Navy Yard and later owned a shoe repair shop.
Courtesy of Ethel Topalian, daughter of Ohanes

title: Dr. Garabed Vartanian
description: Dr. Garabed Vartanian of Constantinople graduated from Bellevue Hospital Medical School (now New York University College of Medicine) in 1863, and was soon an officer in the 18th Regular Infantry of the Union Army of the Tennessee under Brigadier General William Rosecrans. At the Battle of Chickamauga Creek (August 15-September 12, 1863) Garabed was taken prisoner. He survived, returned to Constantinople where he practiced medicine, and died in 1905.

title: Captain Ruth Santoyan, U.S. Women's Army Corp
description:
Ruth Santoyan of Worcester, Massachusetts, enlisted in the Women's Army Corp (WAC) in 1943. She attended Officers Candidate School and college, completed two tours of duty in Germany, and rose to the rank of Captain during her 20 years of service.
Courtesy of Barbara Santoian Tomasian, Ruth's niece
credit_line: Courtesy o f Barbara Santoian Tomasian

title: Harpoot Assyrian United Asso. of America outing honoring Lady Surma the Bait, Marshemoon
description:
Harpoot Assyrian United Associtation of America outing honoring Lady Surma the Bait, Marshemoon; Surma d'Mar Shimun is the main figure in the photo.
Lady Surma d'Mar Shimun, born in Qudshanis, Turkey was forty-three during her visit to the United States in 1926. She was the aunt of the young Assyrian Patriarch of the Church East, Mar Eshai Shimun XXIII, and sister to the previous two patriarchs. Her seven-month speaking tour across the U.S. to raise funds and awareness for the Assyrian people's quest for a homeland in the Middle-East was supported by friends of Assyrians in England and the U.S. She visited about fifty cities in the U.S. and Canada over seven months before returning to Iraq to continue her work on behalf of the Assyrian people.
Her diary entry for the day of the photo:  Jul 4: [Surma d'Mar Shimun] "Today we arrived at Worcester [MA] where I was taken to a park. I was greeted with a procession and poems were recited. I spoke to them in English." [SurmaDiary].
July 4, 1926; Photograph by K. S. Melikian

2. Tommy Donabedian, son of 1.

4. Rose Parechanian Surenian, sister

5. Elizabeth "Betty" Parechanian Donabedian
6. James Parechanian, brother (just his head is visible)
7. (baby) June Donabedian
8. Daniel Donabedian
9. (baby) Richard Boyajian, son
10. Haig Boyajian
11. Mary Parechian Boyajian, wife of 10.
12. Sarkis Parechanian, father
